title: Noelia Castillo: Psychiatric Suffering, Decisional Capacity, and the Limits of Euthanasia Law
text:
Who: Noelia Castillo Ramos (2000–2026), a young woman from Barcelona, Catalonia, Spain.
What: After a 2022 suicide attempt left her paraplegic and in constant pain, she asked to die under Spain’s euthanasia law. Her request was approved, then tied up for 601 days by her father’s legal appeals before she died at age 25.
Where / When: Catalonia, Spain, 2024–2026, under the 2021 Organic Law Regulating Euthanasia.
Why it matters: Castillo’s case forces the hardest question in end-of-life ethics: can a person whose suffering is bound up with mental illness make a genuinely autonomous, irreversible choice to die — and who gets to decide whether she can? It also reverses the usual family fight: here a competent patient fought for death while her family fought to keep her alive.
Pair with: Euthanasia, Assisted Death, and Their Critics

title: Noelia Castillo: Psychiatric Suffering, Decisional Capacity, and the Limits of Euthanasia Law
text:
The Case
Noelia Castillo was born in Barcelona in November 2000. Her childhood was unstable. When she was about thirteen, her parents lost their home and separated, and her mental health began to suffer. She later said she sometimes had to follow her father to bars and wait there until the early hours of the morning. She was diagnosed with two conditions that would shadow the rest of her story: obsessive–compulsive disorder and borderline personality disorder.

Two diagnoses, in plain terms.

Obsessive–compulsive disorder (OCD) involves intrusive, unwanted thoughts and repeated behaviors a person feels driven to perform to ease the anxiety those thoughts cause.
Borderline personality disorder (BPD) brings intense, unstable emotions, a shaky sense of self, and stormy relationships. It also raises the risk of self-harm and suicide, which matters here.

Neither condition, by itself, removes a person’s ability to make decisions. But BPD can drive suicidal feelings. So it sits at the center of the dispute that follows: when such a person asks to die, is that a free choice or a symptom of the illness?

From 2015 to 2019, Castillo lived in social care. She left at eighteen, as a legal adult. In interviews she said she had survived several sexual assaults that she never reported, and so they were never investigated or confirmed in court. In October 2022, days after one of these assaults, she attempted suicide by jumping from a building. She was twenty-one.
She survived the fall, but it changed everything. The injuries left her with irreversible paraplegia — permanent paralysis of the lower body — along with chronic physical pain, neurological damage, and deep psychological suffering. She now used a wheelchair and lived, in her own words, in constant pain.
Eighteen months later, in April 2024, Castillo formally asked to die under Spain’s euthanasia law.

Spain’s euthanasia law. The 2021 Organic Law Regulating Euthanasia lets a capable, conscious adult request an assisted death. They must have a “serious and incurable illness” or a “serious, chronic, and disabling condition.” It must cause “constant and unbearable physical or mental suffering” with no relief the person finds tolerable (Cortes Generales (Spain) 2021). Spain does not require that the patient be dying. A request must be made twice in writing, reviewed by independent doctors, and signed off by a regional committee. A doctor may then either give the lethal medication directly (euthanasia) or supply it for the patient to take (assisted death).

In July 2024, Catalonia’s Guarantee and Evaluation Commission approved her request unanimously. The procedure was set for the following month. It did not happen for another 601 days.
The obstacle was her father. With the support of the Christian Lawyers Association, an ultra-conservative advocacy group, he launched a series of appeals to stop the procedure. His central argument was about her mind. He claimed that her personality disorder meant she could not make a sound judgment about ending her life. The state, he said, had a duty to protect her from a decision driven by illness.
The courts disagreed, again and again. In March 2025, a Barcelona court rejected the appeal and found Castillo capable of deciding. The High Court of Justice of Catalonia upheld her right in September 2025. Spain’s Supreme Court rejected the father’s appeal in January 2026, and the Constitutional Court dismissed his claim. On March 10, 2026, the European Court of Human Rights rejected his final appeal. At every level, the courts found that Castillo had the capacity to choose.
In a television interview recorded the day before she died, Castillo defended her decision and made a point of not recommending it to anyone else. “I just want to go peacefully now and to stop suffering,” she said. “I don’t want to be an example for anyone — it’s simply my life.” On March 26, 2026, at age twenty-five, she received euthanasia at a healthcare center in Sant Pere de Ribes. Her mother asked to be in the room. Castillo chose to die alone.
Her case became a national argument before and after her death. It was debated in Spain’s Congress. Several parties spoke in support. The People’s Party and Vox criticized the outcome. Catholic bishops condemned it, with one calling it “another step toward a culture of death.” A great deal of false information also spread. Political figures claimed she had been attacked by migrant children, and many reports said she had been granted euthanasia “for depression.” Fact-checkers found both untrue (Jones 2026). The official basis for her euthanasia was the chronic, irreversible physical pain from her injuries.
That last fact is the knot at the center of the case. On paper, her euthanasia was approved for a physical, disabling condition — suffering the law plainly covers. But the reason it was fought all the way to Strasbourg was psychiatric. The claim was that a mind shaped by trauma and personality disorder cannot truly consent to its own death. The law treated her as a disability-and-pain case. Her father treated her as a psychiatric one. Both were describing the same woman.

title: Noelia Castillo: Psychiatric Suffering, Decisional Capacity, and the Limits of Euthanasia Law
text:
The Argument Castillo Started
Castillo’s case is not hard because the facts are unclear. It is hard because two genuine values point in opposite directions. One is respect for what a suffering adult decides about her own life. The other is protection of people whose suffering may be distorting that very decision. The exchange below traces how that conflict unfolds, each move answering the one before.

The argument from autonomy
The starting point is the principle behind Spain’s law and most assisted-death laws: a competent adult may decide that her suffering is no longer bearable, and that judgment is hers to make.

The Autonomy Argument

A competent adult has the right to make decisions about her own body and life, including how much suffering she will endure.
Noelia Castillo was a competent adult enduring constant, irreversible suffering with no relief she found tolerable.
Therefore, the decision to end that suffering through euthanasia was hers to make, and the law was right to honor it.


Premise 1 is widely accepted in the abstract. So, as in many euthanasia cases, the whole weight of the argument falls on premise 2 — the claim that Castillo was genuinely competent. That is exactly where her father attacked.


The capacity challenge: symptom or choice?
Her father did not argue that suffering people should be forced to live. He argued that Castillo’s particular suffering came wrapped in a mental illness that made her wish to die unreliable. On this view, a borderline personality disorder marked by suicidal urges does not just accompany the request to die — it may produce it. The wish is a symptom, not a settled preference.

The Capacity Challenge

A choice counts as autonomous only if it reflects the person’s stable values rather than a symptom of illness.
Castillo’s wish to die may be a symptom of her psychiatric disorders, which involve suicidality.
The state has a duty to protect vulnerable people from acting on such symptoms.
Therefore, her request should not have been honored without resolving whether it was a stable choice or a symptom.


This challenge raises a problem psychiatrists have not solved. With a terminal cancer, doctors can say with confidence that the illness is irremediable — beyond cure. With a psychiatric condition, predicting that suffering will never lift is far harder. The line between preventing suicide and assisting death can blur (Grassi et al. 2022). The same health system offers suicide prevention to one patient and euthanasia to another. What decides which door a person walks through?

Capacity and irremediability. Decisional capacity is the ability to understand relevant information, appreciate how it applies to oneself, reason about options, and communicate a choice. It is decision-specific, not global: a person can have a diagnosis and still have capacity for a given decision. Irremediable suffering is suffering that cannot be relieved by any means the patient finds acceptable. Both concepts are easier to apply to a failing body than to a suffering mind — which is why psychiatric cases sit at the frontier of these laws.



The reply: a double standard
Castillo’s defenders — including a group of philosophers writing in The Lancet Psychiatry after her death — argue that the capacity challenge hides a double standard. They identify three moves against psychiatric euthanasia that they regard as fallacies (Rueda et al. 2026).

Branding the wish as a symptom. Treating any death wish rooted in mental illness as automatically incompetent applies a test we never apply to physical illness. When a cancer patient in agony asks to die, we take it seriously; we do not dismiss it as the disease talking. Capacity must be judged case by case.
The “better support” objection. Saying that stronger family or social support might have changed her mind does not show that her actual decision was invalid. Almost any choice could be waved away by imagining kinder circumstances that never existed.
The “time heals” objection. Saying trauma might ease with more years and more treatment imposes an open-ended waiting period on the patient — a standard of proof that no other irremediable condition has to meet.


The Anti–Double-Standard Reply

We accept a suffering patient’s request to die when their condition is physical and judged irremediable.
Refusing the same request when the suffering is psychiatric, solely because it is psychiatric, treats like cases differently without justification.
Castillo was found to have capacity by every court and committee that examined her.
Therefore, refusing her request would have been unjust discrimination against the mentally ill, not protection of them.


The reply is powerful precisely because it turns the protective instinct around: shielding the mentally ill from their own choices can itself be a way of not taking them seriously as persons.


The vulnerability counter: a free choice, or a failed society?
But the strongest objection to Castillo’s euthanasia does not deny her capacity at all. It accepts that she chose freely — and asks what she was choosing between.
The philosopher Loughran Butcher argues that defenders of psychiatric euthanasia answer the wrong worry. They point to safeguards: independent reviews, capacity assessments, committee approval. But safeguards only check whether a person qualifies. They do nothing about why a person reaches that point (Butcher 2025). Castillo’s life held a lost home, years in institutional care, untreated trauma, poverty, and assaults that were never investigated. Each of those is a social failure, not a medical fact. A safeguard confirms she is eligible. It does not ask whether better support would have left her wanting to live.
This is also where the disability-rights critique enters. Castillo was not dying. She was paralyzed and in pain. Many disabled people live with such conditions for decades and do not regard them as a reason to die. Critics worry that approving her death sends a message: that a paraplegic life is a life not worth living. The British pianist James Rhodes, himself an abuse survivor, offered to pay for her physical and mental healthcare. His gesture dramatized the objection. Was death offered to her more readily than a real chance at a livable life?

The Vulnerability / Disability Argument

A choice is only as free as the alternatives a person actually has.
Castillo’s alternatives were narrowed by social failures — poverty, lost housing, institutional care, untreated trauma, and inadequate support.
When death becomes more available than the support that would make life bearable, “choosing” death is not a clean exercise of autonomy.
Therefore, honoring her request may have ratified a social failure rather than respected an autonomous person.


Notice that this argument does not call Castillo incompetent. It grants everything the double-standard reply demands — and still finds something wrong. The Spanish bishops, in their own register, made a parallel point: the case showed “an accumulation of personal suffering and institutional shortcomings that calls the whole of society into question.”


The unresolved frontier
So the case ends where the law itself is unsettled. Only a handful of countries — the Netherlands, Belgium, Spain — permit assisted death for suffering that is not terminal and is partly or wholly psychiatric. Canada twice voted to extend its system to people whose sole condition is a mental illness. Twice it postponed the change, most recently to 2027. Clinicians could not agree on how to tell irremediable mental illness from the treatable kind (Grassi et al. 2022). The very question Castillo’s father raised is one that a whole national health system has so far refused to answer.
Her case also inverts the older end-of-life disputes. In the landmark American cases, families fought to stop treatment while the state insisted on preserving life. Here a competent woman fought for death while her father fought to keep her alive. The courts sided with the woman against the family. That reversal is not a footnote. It shows how far the center of gravity has shifted toward individual autonomy. It also shows why the people most worried are not only religious conservatives, but disability advocates and psychiatrists.
What remains unresolved is whether Castillo’s death was autonomy honored or vulnerability abandoned. Both descriptions fit the same facts. The courts answered the narrow legal question — did she have capacity? They answered yes. They did not, and perhaps could not, answer the larger one. Would a just society have given her something to live for before it gave her a way to die?

title: Noelia Castillo: Psychiatric Suffering, Decisional Capacity, and the Limits of Euthanasia Law
text:
Discussion Questions

Every court that examined Castillo found she had the capacity to choose. Is a finding of capacity enough to settle whether honoring her request was ethical, or can a fully capable choice still be the wrong one to grant? Explain the gap, if there is one.
The Lancet Psychiatry authors argue that treating a psychiatric death-wish as automatically incompetent applies a double standard we never apply to physical illness. Is the difference between physical and psychiatric suffering a real moral difference, or just a prejudice? Defend your answer.
Butcher argues that safeguards check whether someone qualifies for euthanasia but ignore why they reached that point. If a person’s wish to die grows out of poverty and lack of support, is their choice still autonomous? What would have to be true for it to count as free?
Castillo’s father argued the state should have protected her from her own decision; the courts ruled that protecting her meant respecting it. When a competent adult and her family disagree this completely, whose judgment should the law privilege, and why?
Canada has twice postponed allowing euthanasia for mental illness alone because doctors cannot reliably judge when such suffering is irremediable. Given that uncertainty, should a country permit psychiatric euthanasia now, wait until the judgment is more reliable, or refuse it permanently? What does your answer cost?

title: Noelia Castillo: Psychiatric Suffering, Decisional Capacity, and the Limits of Euthanasia Law
text:
References


Butcher, Loughran. 2025. “MAiD, Mental Disorders, and Vulnerability: How Common Responses to Vulnerability Concerns Are Inadequate.” Canadian Journal of Bioethics / Revue Canadienne de Bioéthique 8 (4): 56–63. https://doi.org/10.7202/1121335ar.


Cortes Generales (Spain). 2021. Ley Orgánica 3/2021, de 24 de Marzo, de Regulación de La Eutanasia. Boletín Oficial del Estado, no. 72. https://www.boe.es/eli/es/lo/2021/03/24/3.


Grassi, Luigi, Federica Folesani, Marco Marella, et al. 2022. “Debating Euthanasia and Physician-Assisted Death in People with Psychiatric Disorders.” Current Psychiatry Reports 24 (6): 325–35. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11920-022-01339-y.


Jones, Sam. 2026. Spanish Woman Who Won Legal Battle for Right to Euthanasia Has Assisted Death. The Guardian. https://www.theguardian.com/society/2026/mar/26/spanish-woman-noelia-castillo-euthanasia-assisted-death.


Rueda, Jon, Rosana Triviño-Caballero, Iris Parra Jounou, Stuart Youngner, and David Rodríguez-Arias. 2026. “Three Fallacies in Arguments Made Against Euthanasia for People with Psychiatric Disorders.” The Lancet Psychiatry, ahead of print. https://doi.org/10.1016/S2215-0366(26)00154-9.

title: Adriana Smith: Brain Death, Pregnancy, and Bodies the Law Has No Box For
text:
Who: Adriana Smith (1994–2025), a thirty-year-old registered nurse in Atlanta. Her mother, April Newkirk, became her decision-maker. Her partner, Adrian Harden, was the baby’s father.
What: In February 2025, Smith went to a hospital with severe headaches and was sent home. The next morning she was found gasping for air. Blood clots had flooded her brain. She was declared brain-dead — legally dead — at nine weeks pregnant. The hospital kept her body on machines for about four months, over the family’s objection, citing Georgia’s abortion law. Her son Chance was delivered prematurely in June. Smith’s body was removed from support four days later.
Where / When: Emory University Hospital Midtown, Atlanta, Georgia, February–June 2025. A custody fight over Chance ran into December 2025.
Why it matters: This looks like an abortion case but is not one. It forces three questions a clinician can meet on any shift:

When a patient is already dead, who decides what happens to her body — and to the pregnancy inside it?
Why does this situation fit none of medicine’s familiar categories — organ donation, withdrawal of care, or research?
When the law is unclear, whose body ends up absorbing the doubt?

Pair with: Reproductive Ethics (Pregnancy, Autonomy, and Maternal–Fetal Conflict)

title: Adriana Smith: Brain Death, Pregnancy, and Bodies the Law Has No Box For
text:
The Case
Adriana Smith was thirty, a nurse, and nine weeks pregnant. In early February 2025 she went to Northside Hospital in Atlanta with a severe headache. She was given medicine and sent home. No scan of her brain was done. The next morning her partner, Adrian Harden, woke to the sound of her gasping for air and called 911. At Emory, a scan showed several blood clots in her brain. Her mother gave permission for a procedure to relieve the pressure, but doctors said it could not help. The injury was too far gone. Within a short time, Smith met the criteria for brain death.

What brain death is. A person is brain-dead when the entire brain, including the brainstem, has permanently and completely stopped working. Under the Uniform Determination of Death Act — the model law in Georgia and almost every other state — brain death is legal death. It is the same, in law and in medicine, as the heart stopping for good. A brain-dead patient is not in a coma, not unconscious, and not “on life support” in the usual sense. The patient has died. A ventilator and drugs can keep blood moving through the organs of a body whose owner is gone.

So the hospital was not keeping Adriana Smith alive. She was dead. The machines kept her organs working for the sake of the nine-week fetus she carried. Her mother, April Newkirk, later told reporters what the staff had said: Georgia law required them to continue support because the fetus had a detectable heartbeat. The family was given no choice. The plan was to maintain Smith’s body until roughly thirty-two weeks, when the baby would have a fair chance outside the womb. “We didn’t have a choice or a say about it,” Newkirk said. “We want the baby. That’s a part of my daughter. But the decision should have been left to us — not the state” (Lewis et al. 2025).
The law the hospital pointed to was the LIFE Act.

The LIFE Act (O.C.G.A. § 16-12-141). Georgia’s 2019 statute bans most abortions once a “human heartbeat” can be detected — about six weeks. It defines an “unborn child” as a member of the human species carried in the womb, and it treats that child as a legal person for more than abortion. Under Georgia law, a fetus with a detectable heartbeat can be claimed as a tax dependent and can be the subject of a wrongful-death lawsuit. Legal scholars describe the LIFE Act as, at its core, a fetal-personhood law, with the abortion ban as one branch of it (State of Georgia 2019).

For months the machines ran while the fetus grew. Reports later said scans had found possible problems with the fetus. The family, by their account, was never offered a way to stop. In mid-May, with the pregnancy at about five months, Newkirk went to the local press. The story was national within two days.
It drew a coalition you would expect — abortion-rights groups, reproductive-justice organizers, civil-liberties lawyers. It also drew one you might not. Several abortion opponents said the law did not require what Emory was doing. The state’s Republican Attorney General, Chris Carr, put it in writing.

Carr’s statement, May 2025. “There is nothing in the LIFE Act that requires medical professionals to keep a woman on life support after brain death. Removing life support is not an action ‘with the purpose to terminate a pregnancy.’” Georgia House Republican leaders agreed, calling the law “completely irrelevant” to Smith’s case (Office of the Attorney General of Georgia 2025).

Emory did not change course. The hospital said only that it used clinical experts, the medical literature, and legal guidance to make decisions “in compliance with Georgia’s abortion laws and all other applicable laws” (Lewis et al. 2025). In other words: the Attorney General’s opinion was not the hospital lawyers’ opinion, and the lawyers were the ones in charge.
On June 13, 2025, surgeons delivered Chance by emergency cesarean. He was born at about six months of pregnancy — extremely premature by World Health Organization terms — weighing one pound, thirteen ounces. (Accounts of his exact gestational age vary.) Four days later, Emory removed Adriana Smith’s body from support. A funeral followed that month.
The question her case raises is not whether anyone broke the law. The Attorney General said the hospital was not forced to do this. The question is harder: what kind of medical act was Emory performing for four months, and what rules should govern it?

title: Adriana Smith: Brain Death, Pregnancy, and Bodies the Law Has No Box For
text:
Eight People in One Room
Before taking sides, it helps to slow down and ask a plain question that nurses and ethics committees ask all the time: who has a stake here, and what does each one want? This is called a stakeholder analysis. You list everyone the decision touches, name their interest, and note where those interests collide. It does not tell you the answer. It keeps you from missing someone — which is exactly what the family says happened here.
The stakeholders in Adriana Smith’s case:

Adriana Smith herself. She was dead, but the dead still have interests we honor — in dignity, and in not having their bodies used in ways they would have refused. She left no advance directive, so no one knew her wishes.
The fetus, later the baby Chance. An interest in being born and in a decent life. But scans suggested possible harm, and his odds were uncertain.
April Newkirk, the mother. Adriana’s legal decision-maker. She wanted the baby — but wanted the choice to be the family’s. She called the months of waiting “torture.”
Adrian Harden, the father. Deeply invested, present from the first 911 call. He had almost no legal standing the whole time.
The bedside nurses and doctors. Many were Smith’s own former colleagues. They gave months of intensive care to a body that could not get better, with no clear goal and real moral distress.
The hospital as an institution. Worried about prison time for its doctors and uncapped lawsuits under a personhood statute.
The state of Georgia. A stated interest in protecting fetal life — voiced loudly by some officials and waved off by others.
The public. Watching a case that would shape how the next family is treated.

The list points to a recurring tension in the case. The person with the strongest legal voice — the hospital’s lawyer — had the least personal stake. The people with the deepest personal stake — the mother and father — had little or no legal voice.

title: Adriana Smith: Brain Death, Pregnancy, and Bodies the Law Has No Box For
section: Why This Is Not an Abortion
text:
Why This Is Not an Abortion
The first thing to get straight is the one most headlines got wrong. This was not an abortion case, and that is not a technicality. It turns on a distinction that runs through all of ethics: the difference between what you intend and what you merely foresee.
The LIFE Act defines abortion as an act done “with the purpose to terminate a pregnancy.” That word, purpose, is the hinge. When doctors stop machines on a brain-dead body, what is their purpose? Standard medical practice says: to stop futile treatment on a patient who has died. The death of the fetus is a foreseen side effect of that — sad, but not the aim. On this reading, the act is simply not an abortion, because the purpose is different (Watson 2025).
This is a real-world use of a principle called the Doctrine of Double Effect (DDE).

The Doctrine of Double Effect. An old idea, often traced to Thomas Aquinas, that an act with two effects — one good, one bad — can be permissible even if the bad effect is certain, provided you aim at the good one and merely tolerate the bad one. A doctor may give a dying patient enough morphine to ease pain even if it shortens life, because the aim is comfort, not death. Same outcome, different intention, different moral (and legal) verdict.

DDE explains the strange coalition. It let abortion opponents say the ban did not apply without giving up the belief that the fetus is a person. You can hold that the fetus is a full person and still say: stopping a dead woman’s machines is not an act aimed at ending the pregnancy. That is why even some pro-life voices agreed disconnection was permissible.

The “Not an Abortion” Argument (the Attorney General’s position)

The LIFE Act bans only acts done with the purpose to terminate a pregnancy.
Stopping support for a brain-dead patient aims at ending futile treatment on a dead body; the fetus’s death is foreseen, not intended.
Therefore the LIFE Act does not forbid — and does not even speak to — stopping support, and the family’s wishes should govern.


Here is the catch, and it is the heart of the hospital’s fear. DDE is controversial, and “intent” is slippery. Critics of the doctrine have long argued that the line between intending a harm and foreseeing it is easy to blur and easy to game. From the outside, the two acts look identical: in both, machines come off and the fetus dies. The only difference lives inside someone’s head — and a courtroom is exactly the place where someone else gets to second-guess what was really in your head.
That is precisely what Emory’s lawyers seem to have worried about. It is one thing for the Attorney General to announce that the purpose is innocent. It is another to bet a doctor’s freedom on a future prosecutor — or a wrongful-death jury — agreeing about what that doctor “really meant.” Under the LIFE Act, a physician can face up to ten years in prison, and the institution faces lawsuits with no dollar cap. So the soft spot in DDE — that intent is hard to prove — was not a seminar puzzle for Emory. It was the legal risk. The hospital read every ambiguity in the most defensive direction, because the doctrine that should have protected it was one a hostile reader could attack.
The contrast with Kate Cox’s case in Texas is instructive. There, a single physician, Damla Karsan, put her own name on a lawsuit and accepted the personal legal risk. Emory, by contrast, was an institution weighing liability rather than an individual acting on conscience. A similar kind of statute produced opposite choices.

title: Adriana Smith: Brain Death, Pregnancy, and Bodies the Law Has No Box For
text:
Almost, But Not Quite
If this is not an abortion, what is it? The honest answer is that it does not fit any box medicine already has. It looks like several familiar things and turns out to be none of them. That is what makes it genuinely hard.
It is almost like organ donation — but the analogy breaks at both ends. We routinely use a dead person’s body to help others. The reason that works, ethically and legally, is that organs are treated almost like property: things that can be given, with consent, under gift law. We have a whole system of donor cards, registries, and family authorization. But a fetus is not property. Under the LIFE Act it is a person, with standing to sue and to be claimed on taxes. You cannot “donate” it, and you cannot discard it, the way you would a kidney. So the consent-and-property machinery that settles organ cases has nothing to grab onto. And it breaks from the other side too: organ donation needs the dead person’s prior yes. Adriana Smith never gave one. No one used her like a registered donor; they used her without any donation at all.
It is almost like withdrawing care from a dying patient — but she was not dying. She was dead. Normally a family can refuse life-sustaining treatment, and the law backs them. Georgia even has an Advance Directive Act for exactly this. But that law only applies to a patient with “a terminal condition or state of permanent unconsciousness.” Brain death is neither. It is death. A dead person cannot have treatment “withdrawn,” and an advance directive does not apply to her, because directives are instructions from the living (Watson 2025). The familiar end-of-life framework simply switches off.
It is almost like research — and here the experts openly disagree. The bioethicist Arthur Caplan argued that keeping a brain-dead body pregnant for months has almost no evidence base, no settled protocol, and no proven track record. A medical act like that, he said, is not treatment at all — it is closer to an experiment. And experiments on human subjects require informed consent, which no one here could give and no one sought.

The Experimentation Argument (Caplan)

A medical act with essentially no evidence base, no established protocol, and no prior outcomes is research, not ordinary care.
Research on a human subject requires informed consent — from the subject or a legal surrogate.
Long-term support of a brain-dead pregnant body meets the test for research, and no such consent was sought for Smith.
Therefore Emory performed unconsented human experimentation, which research ethics forbids no matter what an abortion law says (Caplan 2025).


But this claim is disputed. A 2025 review in The American Journal of Bioethics by Ariane Lewis and colleagues pushes back hard. They count at least forty-seven reported cases of brain death in pregnancy since 1982, and they point to detailed international guidance — the World Brain Death Project even publishes a management checklist for it. On their account, this is “not novel or experimental.” It is rare and difficult, but known (Lewis et al. 2025).
So who is right? The two sides may not fully contradict each other. Caplan could mean that the four-month length, and the post-Dobbs legal pressure, were unprecedented even if brain death in pregnancy is not. Lewis could be right that the basic practice has decades of cases behind it. Underneath the disagreement sits a sharper question: what exactly makes a medical act count as “experimental”? It could be any of three things:

That it has never been done before.
That it has no settled protocol.
That there is no good evidence it works.

These come apart. Many accepted treatments are short on evidence, yet no one calls them experiments. Where the line falls decides whether Caplan’s powerful argument lands.
Even declaring brain death is done differently in pregnancy. One more sign this case sits outside the normal categories: the usual brain-death exam includes an “apnea test,” which briefly stops the ventilator to see if the patient breathes. Recent work suggests this test may be risky for a fetus, so some experts say it should be skipped in pregnancy in favor of blood-flow scans. Some hospital policies have even barred declaring brain death in a pregnant patient at all until the fetus is delivered (Lewis et al. 2025). Here, the pregnancy affects how death itself is determined.

title: Adriana Smith: Brain Death, Pregnancy, and Bodies the Law Has No Box For
section: When the Baby Was Born, the Problem Moved
text:
When the Baby Was Born, the Problem Moved
The trouble did not end when Chance was born. It just changed shape — and exposed a second gap in the same law.
The LIFE Act had treated the fetus as a person with powerful standing: enough to keep a dead woman on machines for months, enough for tax and wrongful-death purposes. But when Chance was actually born, his biological father, Adrian Harden, discovered he had no automatic legal rights to his own son at all. Because Harden and Smith had never married, Georgia law gave an unwed father no parental standing by default — even with a positive DNA test, and even though the baby’s mother was dead. His attorneys warned that Chance could be placed in foster care without a court order (Dukes 2025).
So Harden had to sue for custody of his own child. The fight stretched across most of the year:

August 2025 — he filed an emergency petition for legitimation and custody.
September 2025 — a court granted temporary custody, letting Chance go home with him.
December 2, 2025 — a judge issued the final order granting sole custody.

That last date was nearly six months after his son was born, and ten months after Adriana collapsed.
These two outcomes come from different bodies of law. The LIFE Act is fetal-protection law; an unwed father’s rights are governed by Georgia’s family and legitimation statutes, which long predate it. There is no logical contradiction between them. But placed next to each other in a single family’s story, they show how unevenly the law assigns standing. The fetus had standing the moment a heartbeat was detected. The biological father, with a positive DNA test and the mother deceased, had none until a court granted it months later.

title: Adriana Smith: Brain Death, Pregnancy, and Bodies the Law Has No Box For
text:
Whose Body Absorbs the Doubt
A final factor is harder to measure but well documented. Georgia’s LIFE Act is, on its face, race-neutral; it says nothing about race. Yet the way ambiguous cases actually get resolved often tracks who the patient is — something that, in principle, should make no difference to the outcome.
The pattern is documented, not merely suspected. Black women in the United States are three to four times more likely to die from pregnancy-related causes than white women, and studies find racial gaps in how quickly pregnant patients are even seen in emergency rooms (Lewis et al. 2025). Adriana Smith was sent home with a headache and no brain scan; whether bias played a role in that first visit is something her own mother raised.
The deeper worry is structural. When a law is ambiguous and the penalties are severe, someone has to absorb the doubt — and it is rarely distributed evenly. Hospitals with thin budgets and many low-income patients have the least room to risk a lawsuit, so their lawyers read every gray area in the most defensive way. Wealthier hospitals have more freedom to read the same gray area in the patient’s favor. The same statute can land very differently depending on whose body it lands on. The reproductive-justice group SisterSong, a longtime opponent of the LIFE Act, made Smith’s case a rallying point for exactly this reason (SisterSong Women of Color Reproductive Justice Collective 2025; Roberts 1997).
None of this decides whether Emory was right. It adds a further consideration: when the rules are unclear, the cost of that uncertainty is not shared equally. It tends to fall hardest on the hospitals — and the patients — with the least room to absorb legal risk.

title: Adriana Smith: Brain Death, Pregnancy, and Bodies the Law Has No Box For
text:
Discussion Questions

Write out The “Not an Abortion” Argument in your own words, premises first. Which single premise was Emory’s legal team most afraid to rely on, and why?
The case turns on the gap between intending a death and foreseeing it. Describe one other medical situation where that gap matters. Is the distinction real, or just a way to feel better about the same act?
Caplan calls the four months “experimentation”; Lewis calls it rare-but-known care. Pick the one feature you think best decides whether a medical act is “experimental,” and use it to judge Smith’s case.
You are the night-shift charge nurse in a Georgia ICU. A patient is declared brain-dead at eleven weeks pregnant. Her sister, the legal decision-maker, asks that support be stopped; the hospital lawyer has not called back. What do you say in the meeting, and which idea from this case is doing the work?
The law gave a nine-week fetus more standing than it gave the baby’s living father. Propose one change to fix that mismatch — then name one thing your change would not fix.

title: Adriana Smith: Brain Death, Pregnancy, and Bodies the Law Has No Box For
text:
Further Reading

Ariane Lewis, Gwendolyn Quinn, and Kimberly Mutcherson, “Ethical Controversies in the Adriana Smith Case in Georgia,” The American Journal of Bioethics (2025). The clearest medical-ethical overview; the source for the claim that brain death in pregnancy is rare but not new.
Arthur Caplan, “The Adriana Smith Case Unfolding in Atlanta Raises Many Questions,” Bioethics Today (May 2025). Short, sharp, and the origin of the “experimentation” framing.
Katie Watson, “Brain Death in Pregnancy — Abortion, Advance-Directive, or End-of-Life Law?” New England Journal of Medicine 393:4 (2025). A three-page tour of why the case fits no legal box.
Hilde Lindemann Nelson, “The Architect and the Bee: Some Reflections on Postmortem Pregnancy,” Bioethics 8:3 (1994). The philosophical roots of the worry about using the dead.
The Wikipedia article “Adriana Smith pregnancy case” — unusually well-sourced for a factual timeline.

Dukes, Deidra. 2025. Court Grants Sole Custody to Father After Mother Declared Brain-Dead. FOX 5 Atlanta. https://www.fox5atlanta.com/news/court-grants-sole-custody-father-after-mother-declared-brain-dead.

title: Sylvain Lesné and the Aβ*56 Affair: Research Misconduct, Replication, and the Amyloid Hypothesis
text:
Who: Sylvain Lesné (b. 1974), neuroscientist at the University of Minnesota; Matthew Schrag (b. ~1984), Vanderbilt neurologist and image sleuth; Karen Ashe, Lesné’s senior collaborator and a founding figure in mouse models of Alzheimer’s disease.
What: A 2006 paper in Nature claimed to have isolated a form of amyloid-beta — “Aβ56” — that caused memory loss in rats. The paper anchored the leading theory of Alzheimer’s for sixteen years. In 2022, a whistleblower published evidence that dozens of Western blot images in Lesné’s papers had been digitally faked. Nature* retracted the paper in 2024.
Where / When: University of Minnesota; Vanderbilt University; published in Science July 2022; retraction June 2024; Lesné resigned February 2025.
Why it matters: The case sits at the crossroads of research misconduct, the replication crisis, and subject protection. If a basic preclinical result is faked, every drug trial built on it enrolls human volunteers under false clinical equipoise — and the field’s billions in public funding rest on a mirage.
Pair with: Research Ethics (Protecting Research Subjects)

title: Sylvain Lesné and the Aβ*56 Affair: Research Misconduct, Replication, and the Amyloid Hypothesis
text:
The Case
In August 2021, Matthew Schrag got a phone call. He was a junior neurologist at Vanderbilt, thirty-seven years old, soft-spoken, with a small sign on his desk reading “Everything is figureoutable.” A colleague wanted to connect him with an attorney. The attorney represented two short-sellers — investors betting that a small biotech, Cassava Sciences, would lose value. Cassava was developing an Alzheimer’s drug called Simufilam. The short-sellers thought the underlying research was fake. Would Schrag, who had already criticized the FDA’s approval of another anti-amyloid drug, agree to examine the published images? He said yes. Over the next months, the attorney paid him about $18,000 (Piller 2022).
What began as a paid side job became something larger. Schrag’s method was unglamorous. He downloaded published figures, zoomed in, and looked for telltale signs that an image had been built in Photoshop rather than captured from a real experiment:

Patches of pixels repeated in places they should not be.
Bands that looked identical when they should be unique.
Edges too clean to come from a working gel.

His targets were Western blots. A Western blot uses electricity to drag protein samples through a gel, separating them by size into stacked bands that are stained and photographed. Two blots from the same true experiment can never look pixel-identical. If they do, someone copied and pasted.

Western blot. A Western blot detects a specific protein in a tissue or fluid sample. The sample runs through a gel that separates proteins by size. The proteins are then moved to a membrane and tagged with antibodies that bind only to the target protein. The result is a photograph of dark bands at certain positions — a visual fingerprint. The exact pattern of background noise and band shape is random, so two real blots should never match.

While studying Cassava, Schrag noticed something far bigger. The same patterns of image trouble appeared in an older and much more famous paper: a 2006 article in Nature, first-authored by Sylvain Lesné. Lesné was then a postdoc at the University of Minnesota, working with the well-known Alzheimer’s scientist Karen Ashe (Lesné et al. 2006). The paper reported the isolation of a specific form of amyloid-beta. The authors called it Aβ56 (pronounced “A-beta star fifty-six”). They claimed that injecting purified Aβ56 into young rats made them fail memory tests they had passed before. In the language of biomedical research, this was a smoking gun. It was a direct claim that a particular amyloid species causes dementia, not merely tracks it.

The amyloid hypothesis. Since the early 1990s, the leading theory of Alzheimer’s has held that sticky protein deposits called amyloid-beta plaques in the brain are a main cause of memory loss. The hypothesis has shaped drug development for thirty years. Hundreds of clinical trials have tested antibodies and small molecules meant to prevent, dissolve, or clear amyloid. Most have failed. The few approved drugs — aducanumab (Aduhelm), lecanemab, donanemab — produce small, disputed benefits and can cause brain swelling or bleeding.

The 2006 paper was cited more than 2,300 times. It launched a generation of follow-up studies. It helped Lesné win his own NIH-funded lab at Minnesota in 2009. And, according to Schrag, dozens of the Western blots in it and in Lesné’s later papers showed signs of digital editing: bands repeated where they should be unique, backgrounds spliced together, edges that looked drawn rather than photographed (Piller 2022).
Schrag was careful. He refused to use the word “fraud” in his public critiques. He could not prove intent. He could not see the original, unedited image files. “I focus on what we can see in the published images, and describe them as red flags, not final conclusions,” he told the journalist Charles Piller. “The data should speak for itself.” But when Science sent his findings to top independent image analysts and senior Alzheimer’s researchers, they agreed with him. The reviewers included:

Elisabeth Bik, a microbiologist and the world’s best-known forensic image consultant.
Donna Wilcock, an Alzheimer’s researcher at the University of Kentucky, who called some of the images “shockingly blatant.”
Thomas Südhof, a Nobel laureate at Stanford.

Bik concluded that the authors “appeared to have composed figures by piecing together parts of photos from different experiments… The obtained experimental results might not have been the desired results, and that data might have been changed to better fit a hypothesis” (Piller 2022).
Piller’s investigation appeared in Science in July 2022 under the title “Blots on a Field?”. It named Schrag publicly for the first time. The fallout came in stages:

Nature issued an expression of concern within days of the article.
In June 2024, the editors and most of the original authors agreed to retract the 2006 paper.
In February 2025, the University of Minnesota concluded its inquiry. It found that Lesné had committed research misconduct, and he resigned his faculty post.

The deeper damage is harder to count. The U.S. National Institutes of Health spent roughly $1.6 billion on amyloid-related Alzheimer’s research in fiscal year 2022 alone — about half the federal Alzheimer’s budget. Südhof’s verdict was blunt: “The immediate, obvious damage is wasted NIH funding and wasted thinking in the field because people are using these results as a starting point for their own experiments” (Piller 2022).
The Aβ*56 affair turned out not to be alone. Over the next three years, Piller and his collaborators documented apparent image manipulation in the work of several other senior figures:

Eliezer Masliah, hired in 2016 to lead the National Institute on Aging’s expanded Alzheimer’s program.
Berislav Zlokovic, a stroke researcher at USC whose work supported a federally funded trial that was later suspended.
Marc Tessier-Lavigne, the president of Stanford University, who resigned in 2023 after a student journalist found altered images in papers from his lab.

Across forty-six leading Alzheimer’s researchers, imaging experts flagged nearly six hundred suspect papers. Those papers had collectively been cited some 80,000 times (Piller 2025).
The question the Aβ*56 affair raises is not only whether Sylvain Lesné faked data. That question is now largely settled. The harder question is what it means for a field, and for the human volunteers downstream of it, when a basic result turns out to have been an “elaborate mirage.”

title: Sylvain Lesné and the Aβ*56 Affair: Research Misconduct, Replication, and the Amyloid Hypothesis
text:
The Argument Aβ*56 Started
The Lesné affair is interesting less for the misconduct itself than for the quality of the disagreement it produced. The disagreement is about what counts as harm, what counts as evidence, and what a medical research enterprise owes the people who enroll in its trials. What follows is a connected exchange. Each move answers the last.

Schrag’s argument: fraud in preclinical research is a research-ethics violation
Schrag’s public framing is careful, but it contains a sharper ethical claim than his prose makes obvious. He worried from the start that volunteers in Cassava’s Simufilam trials “faced risks of side effects with no chance of benefit.” That worry generalizes. If the preclinical foundation of a drug-development program is faked, the trials built on it cannot meet clinical equipoise. Equipoise is the rule that a community of skilled clinicians must be in honest, good-faith doubt about whether the experimental treatment beats the alternative. Equipoise is what makes it acceptable to expose trial subjects to risk. Without it, the trial is not research. It is human experimentation in the older, condemned sense.

Schrag’s Argument

A clinical trial is ethically permissible only if there is genuine community equipoise about whether the experimental intervention will benefit participants.
Equipoise requires that the preclinical evidence supporting the intervention be honestly produced.
Several anti-amyloid drug programs rely, directly or by citation chain, on preclinical results that appear to have been faked.
Therefore, those trials enroll participants under a false equipoise and violate the conditions for ethical research.


The disputed premise is the second one. Does equipoise depend on the honesty of the preclinical evidence in particular, or on the honesty of the broader scientific community at any given moment? If the field still has other good reasons to believe amyloid matters, perhaps a single faked study does not poison the well. That is where the main reply starts.


The principal reply: science is robust to individual fraud
A defender of the amyloid hypothesis can grant most of Schrag’s facts and still resist his conclusion. The hypothesis does not stand or fall with Aβ*56. The link between amyloid and Alzheimer’s was established long before 2006. Three independent lines of evidence pointed at it:

Pathology. Amyloid plaques were identified in Alois Alzheimer’s original 1906 case.
Genetics. The amyloid-precursor-protein gene was tied to inherited Alzheimer’s in 1991.
Down syndrome. People with an extra copy of that gene develop early Alzheimer’s pathology with striking reliability.

Aβ*56 was one proposed mechanism among many. Losing it does not collapse the hypothesis.

The “Robustness” Reply

The ethics of a clinical trial depends on the overall state of evidence supporting the intervention, not the integrity of any single paper.
The amyloid hypothesis is supported by multiple independent lines of evidence (genetics, pathology, imaging) that do not depend on Lesné’s work.
Therefore, even if Lesné’s specific findings are faked, current anti-amyloid trials need not violate equipoise.


The reply’s soft spot is empirical. How much of the field’s confidence in amyloid actually traveled — by citation and by influence on grant decisions — through the Aβ*56 result and the broader network of suspect papers? If the answer is “most of it,” the reply is weak. If “very little,” it is strong. Piller’s team estimates that roughly six hundred questionable Alzheimer’s papers have been cited around 80,000 times (Piller 2025). That is not noise. It also does not, on its own, prove that the field has been bent.


The counter-reply: structural harm and the therapeutic misconception
The counter-reply does not argue that the field has been bent. It argues that the participants have been wronged regardless. Trial volunteers — especially in early-stage Alzheimer’s, where cognitive decline is the condition being treated — are vulnerable subjects. They suffer from what the bioethicist Paul Appelbaum named the therapeutic misconception. This is the tendency of seriously ill research participants to believe that what is being done to them is being chosen for their personal benefit, the way a doctor’s prescription would be.

Therapeutic misconception. The therapeutic misconception is the false but natural belief that a research protocol is a form of personalized care. Patients in trials commonly do three things at once:

They underestimate the chance of being randomized to placebo.
They overestimate the chance of personal benefit.
They fail to register that the trial’s main purpose is general knowledge, not their own treatment.

The misconception is strongest in conditions like Alzheimer’s, where standard care offers little hope.

The structural harm, in this telling, is not that the trials produced bad medical outcomes. Many anti-amyloid trials are honestly run by investigators who had no idea anything was wrong with the preclinical literature. The harm is that the consent the participants gave was undermined upstream. They were told, in effect, that skilled independent researchers had reason to believe the drug might help. That claim turned out, in part, to be false. The wrong is a wrong of disclosure and of respect for persons. It is not erased by the fact that the trialists were themselves deceived. Here is where Kant’s idea of treating persons as ends, never merely as means, earns its keep. A research subject used to test a drug whose preclinical basis was faked has been used.


A reframing move: Stegenga and medical nihilism
A different kind of critic accepts most of the exchange and then shifts the frame. The philosopher of medicine Jacob Stegenga argues, in his book Medical Nihilism, that we should “have low confidence in the effectiveness of medical interventions” in general (Stegenga 2018). The reason is not that researchers cheat. It is that even honest clinical evidence is shaped by structural problems:

Small effect sizes.
Selective publication of positive results.
p-hacking — running analyses until something looks significant.
Surrogate endpoints that may not track real benefit.
Conflicts of interest from industry funding.
Career pressure to find positive results.

On Stegenga’s view, the Lesné case is not an outlier in an otherwise healthy science. It is a dramatic version of the field’s normal pathology.

The Medical Nihilism Argument

The honest evidence base for most medical interventions is distorted by small effect sizes, surrogate endpoints, selective publication, industry funding, and pressure to find positive results.
Once those distortions are properly discounted, the residual evidence shows that most medical interventions deliver small benefits relative to their harms and costs.
Therefore, we should have low confidence in the effectiveness of most medical interventions.


The disputed premise is the second. Skeptics of Stegenga grant that the distortions are real but deny that discounting for them leaves most interventions looking marginal.
The two anti-amyloid drugs to reach the U.S. market are a test case. The first was Biogen’s aducanumab, sold as Aduhelm. The FDA approved it in June 2021 over the near-unanimous objection of its own advisory committee. Three committee members resigned in protest. Key facts:

The approval used the accelerated approval pathway. This pathway lets a drug reach patients based on a surrogate endpoint — here, the clearance of amyloid plaques from PET scans — rather than direct evidence that patients live longer or think more clearly.
The two pivotal trials had been stopped early for futility.
A post hoc re-analysis of a subset of patients given the highest dose produced a slim positive signal. The FDA accepted it.
Biogen priced the drug at $56,000 per patient per year. The Medicare program raised Part B premiums for every beneficiary in the country to cover the projected cost.
By early 2024, Biogen had withdrawn Aduhelm from the market for commercial failure.


Accelerated approval and surrogate endpoints. Accelerated approval is an FDA pathway that lets a drug for a serious illness reach patients based on a surrogate endpoint — a measurable proxy (a blood test, a scan finding, a biomarker) “reasonably likely” to predict real clinical benefit. The maker must complete confirmatory trials after approval. The pathway speeds access in conditions like cancer and HIV. Its risk is that the surrogate sometimes fails to track what patients actually care about: surviving longer, thinking more clearly, walking without help.

Lecanemab (sold as Leqembi) and donanemab (Kisunla) are the successors. The FDA approved lecanemab in January 2023 and donanemab in July 2024. Their trials were not stopped early. Their data are real. The headline numbers:

They slow cognitive decline by about half a point on the eighteen-point Clinical Dementia Rating Sum of Boxes scale over eighteen months. Many neurologists openly doubt whether that difference matters in daily life.
They cause amyloid-related imaging abnormalities (ARIA) — brain swelling or small bleeds visible on MRI — in roughly one in five treated patients, with rare but documented deaths.
Annual cost runs to about $26,000 per patient. That figure does not include infusion, monitoring MRI, or clinic fees, all of which Medicare now covers.

If Stegenga is right, the bioethical question is not only “how do we catch the next Lesné?” It is “what should we do when even our non-fraudulent medicine routinely overpromises?” The amyloid program looks, on this view, like a field that absorbed faked data so smoothly because the rest of its evidence base was, even at its best, weaker than its advocates claimed. Aduhelm did not require fraud. It required an honest field willing to count amyloid clearance as good enough.
Stegenga’s prescription is what he calls gentle medicine. The default should lean toward less intervention. Aggressive treatment should be reserved for cases where the evidence base clears a high bar:

The Gentle Medicine Argument

When confidence in an intervention’s benefit is low and its harms are non-trivial, prescribing it does more to violate non-maleficence than withholding it does to violate beneficence.
For most medical interventions in their current evidence base, confidence in benefit is low and harms are non-trivial.
Therefore, medicine should default to less intervention, reserving aggressive treatment for cases where the evidence is unusually strong.


A defender of standard practice will press on the second premise. “Most” is doing a great deal of work. And the alternative to prescribing a marginal drug is sometimes prescribing nothing — itself a choice with costs. Some patients would have benefited. Family caregivers carry a heavy burden the drug was meant, however slightly, to ease. The argument does not, on its own, tell a memory-clinic nurse what to say to a frightened sixty-eight-year-old. It does suggest the conversation should start somewhere other than the brochure.
This is not a comforting frame. It implies that better misconduct policing — sharper image-forensics software, mandatory raw-data deposits, stronger journal retraction rules — addresses a real problem but not the deepest one. The deepest problem is the gap between what biomedical research can reliably deliver and what patients, regulators, and journalists are induced to expect from it.


The move that hasn’t ended
The institutional fallout has been steady:

June 2024. The original authors of the Aβ*56 paper agreed to retract it.
September 2024. The NIH found that Eliezer Masliah — until that month the leader of the agency’s Alzheimer’s program — had committed research misconduct. He was removed from his post (Piller 2025).
February 2025. The University of Minnesota concluded its inquiry. Lesné resigned.

None of these moves answers the live question.
The live question is what biomedical research should do now that the tools of image faking and the tools of image forensics are both becoming much more powerful. Generative AI can produce Western blots that look real to the eye. Forensic AI can scan an entire journal’s back catalog for duplications in minutes — Elisabeth Bik did her early work by hand. The arms race is real. It is being run on top of an incentive structure — publish-or-perish, novelty-or-irrelevance, single-laboratory secrecy — that the field has not seriously revised since the 1970s.
The parallel to Tuskegee is instructive but limited. Tuskegee produced direct, identifiable victims and a clear set of failures: no consent, no treatment, no public oversight. The Aβ*56 affair produces diffuse, statistical victims:

Alzheimer’s patients who enrolled in trials that should have been redesigned.
Families who paid out of pocket for drugs that should have been studied differently.
A generation of postdocs whose careers were built on a hypothesis that was overinvested in.

There is no single wronged person to apologize to. There is, instead, a field-level question about what the public is owed when it funds science. Not perfect results, but a system honest enough that its mistakes are recoverable. The question is whether we now have one.

title: Sylvain Lesné and the Aβ*56 Affair: Research Misconduct, Replication, and the Amyloid Hypothesis
text:
Discussion Questions

Write out Schrag’s Argument and the Robustness Reply in your own words — premises first, then the conclusion. The two arguments share one premise about what makes a clinical trial ethically OK to run. Identify it. Then say which side has the better read on what that premise requires.
The counter-reply says that patients in anti-amyloid trials were wronged even when their own trials were honestly run — because the science behind those trials had been corrupted upstream by Lesné’s fraud. Is this idea of “being wronged” too broad? If you accept it, where does it stop? If you reject it, why?
Stegenga’s medical nihilism says the Lesné case is just an extreme version of how biomedical research normally fails. Push back on this claim. Name one part of biomedical research that you think does work well, and explain why it survives Stegenga’s critique.
You are a nurse in a memory clinic. A patient with mild cognitive impairment asks you whether she should enroll in a new Alzheimer’s drug trial. She mentions news coverage of the “new Alzheimer’s drugs.” She has not read the Science article on the fraud. What do you say to her? What do you not say?
The 2006 Nature paper was retracted in 2024 — eighteen years after it was published, and only after a whistleblower (paid in part by stock-market short-sellers) brought it to public attention. Design one specific reform — at the journal, the university, the NIH, or the FDA — that would have caught the problem earlier. What trade-off would your reform force on the system?

title: Sylvain Lesné and the Aβ*56 Affair: Research Misconduct, Replication, and the Amyloid Hypothesis
text:
Further Reading

Charles Piller, Doctored: Fraud, Arrogance, and Tragedy in the Quest to Cure Alzheimer’s (Atria, 2025) — book-length investigation.
Charles Piller, “Blots on a Field?”, Science, 21 July 2022 — the original exposé.
Jacob Stegenga, Medical Nihilism (Oxford, 2018), chapters 1–3 — the philosophical reframing.
The PubPeer entries on Lesné’s papers (pubpeer.com) — the actual flagged images, with public commentary.
John Ioannidis, “Why Most Published Research Findings Are False”, PLoS Medicine (2005) — the foundational replication-crisis paper.

text:
References


Lesné, Sylvain, Ming Teng Koh, Linda Kotilinek, et al. 2006. “A Specific Amyloid-Beta Protein Assembly in the Brain Impairs Memory.” Nature 440 (7082): 352–57. https://doi.org/10.1038/nature04533.


Piller, Charles. 2022. “Blots on a Field?” Science 377 (6604): 358–63. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.add9993.
