title: Dax Cowart and the Right to Refuse Life-Saving Treatment: Autonomy, Beneficence, and Paternalism
text:
Who: Donald Herbert “Dax” Cowart (1947–2019), a 25-year-old former Air Force pilot from Henderson, Texas.
What: Burned over roughly two-thirds of his body in a 1973 propane explosion. He repeatedly, and competently, demanded that treatment stop, and was overruled for more than a year.
Where / When: Parkland Memorial Hospital (Dallas), a rehabilitation hospital in Houston, and John Sealy Hospital at the University of Texas Medical Branch (Galveston), 1973–1974.
Why it matters: Cowart’s case became the most-discussed American example of a clash between patient autonomy (a competent person’s right to refuse treatment) and clinical beneficence (the duty to act for the patient’s good). Even after a full recovery and a successful career, he insisted his refusal should have been honored. The argument he started has run for fifty years and is not over.
Pair with: History & the Clinical Relationship

title: Dax Cowart and the Right to Refuse Life-Saving Treatment: Autonomy, Beneficence, and Paternalism
text:
The Case
On the afternoon of July 25, 1973, Dax Cowart and his father, Ray, drove out to look at a piece of rural land in east Texas. Dax — then named Donald, a name he would later change — was twenty-five. He was a former high-school football captain, a rodeo rider, and an Air Force pilot recently back from service. By every account, including his own, he was a young man who took his strong, fast, capable body for granted.
They did not know that an underground propane pipeline near where they parked had been leaking. The gas had pooled, invisible and odorless, in the low ground around the car. When Ray turned the ignition to leave, a spark from the engine ignited it. The explosion threw Dax clear of a wall of fire. He ran through pine woods until he could not run anymore, beating at flames with hands that were already destroyed.
A farmer reached him first. Cowart’s first recorded words, to that stranger, were a request for a weapon: “Get me a gun. Can’t you see I’m a dead man. I’m going to die anyway.” Both men were taken to a small hospital in Kilgore, then transferred together — 140 miles, in the same ambulance — to the burn unit at Parkland Memorial Hospital in Dallas. Ray Cowart died on the way. Dax survived. The damage was severe:

Burns covered about 65% of his body.
The deepest, third-degree burns destroyed the skin of his face, ears, and hands.
He was blinded in both eyes.
Most of his fingers were later amputated.
He was conscious, and he stayed conscious.

What followed was fourteen months of treatment that Cowart would later describe, without exaggeration, as “pure hell.” The medical necessity was real.

Why the treatment was unavoidable. Burn depth is graded by layer:

First-degree burns affect only the outer skin (an ordinary sunburn).
Second-degree burns blister and are intensely painful.
Third-degree (full-thickness) burns destroy all skin layers.

The amount burned is reported as percent of total body surface area (TBSA). At ~65% TBSA, much of it third-degree, Cowart was at the edge of what was survivable in 1973. Survival required debridement — the physical removal of dead tissue, because new skin cannot grow over it and dead tissue breeds lethal infection. This was often done in a Hubbard tank, a steel pool of warm antiseptic water that softened the dead skin so it could be scrubbed and cut away. The procedure works. Before modern pain control, it was also one of the most agonizing routine treatments in medicine.

From the start — at Parkland, and at every stage afterward — Cowart asked the people treating him to stop. Not in passing, and not only when the pain was worst. He asked his doctors, his nurses, and his mother, again and again, to end treatment and let him die. He understood what he was asking for. He understood that without the tankings and skin grafts he would die of infection within weeks. He wanted that outcome over the one being forced on him. He felt, he said, that he was “being skinned alive.”
A psychiatrist, Dr. Robert B. White, was brought in to evaluate him at Galveston. The implied hope was that White would find Cowart too depressed or too overwhelmed to be making a real decision. That would have authorized treating him against his will with a clear conscience. White concluded the opposite. Cowart, he found, was “not in the least incompetent or mentally ill.” It was during this period that White recorded the interview that became the 1974 film Please Let Me Die — a competent man, on camera, lucidly asking to be allowed to die, and being treated anyway (White 1974).
The most revealing turn in the story is one the standard retellings often skip. After about eight months at Parkland, Cowart was moved to a rehabilitation hospital in Houston. There he again refused further treatment — and the rehabilitation physician accepted the refusal. For a moment, the system did what Cowart asked. Then his mother, Ada, his legal next of kin, and his attorney intervened. They had him transferred to the burn unit at John Sealy Hospital in Galveston, where treatment — and the tankings — resumed under the unit’s director, Dr. Duane Larson. The decisive fact of the case is not that no one would listen. Someone did. He was moved away from the person who listened.
He survived, was discharged blind and severely disabled, and was, by his own description, deeply depressed for years. He attempted suicide more than once. Then his life changed. He earned a law degree from Texas Tech University in 1986, married, practiced law, and became one of the most articulate patient-rights advocates in the country. He died on April 28, 2019, at seventy-one, of complications from leukemia and liver cancer.
For the rest of that life he said two things that many listeners could not hold together. He was glad to be alive. And his physicians had been wrong to keep him alive against his will. He did not think these statements contradicted each other. The good outcome, he insisted, was never the question. The question was whose decision it had been — and that question became one of the longest-running arguments in modern bioethics.

title: Dax Cowart and the Right to Refuse Life-Saving Treatment: Autonomy, Beneficence, and Paternalism
text:
The Argument Dax Started
What makes Cowart’s case central to bioethics is not the horror of the burns. It is the quality of the disagreement it produced. Over fifty years, Cowart, his physicians, and a series of philosophers and lawyers built an argument, attacked it, repaired it, and attacked the repair. The pieces below trace that exchange as a connected dialogue. Each move answers the one before.

The argument from autonomy
Cowart’s own position is the cleanest. He insisted the issue was control over his body, not the value of the result: “the right to control your own body is a right you’re born with, not something that you have to ask anyone else for, not the government, not your treating physician, not your next-of-kin” (Cowart and Burt 1998).

The Autonomy Argument

A competent adult has a moral right to refuse any medical treatment.
Dax Cowart was a competent adult who refused treatment.
Therefore, treating Cowart over his objection violated his moral rights.


Premise 1 is now close to settled doctrine. Courts and ethicists broadly accept that bodily self-determination includes the right to decline care. So the argument’s whole weight falls on premise 2 — and that is exactly where his physicians attacked it.

Capacity, competence, and the surrogate. Decision-making capacity is a clinical judgment that a patient can do four things:

Understand the relevant information.
Appreciate how it applies to them.
Reason about the options.
Communicate a choice.

Capacity is decision-specific and time-specific. “Competence” is the parallel legal term. When a patient lacks capacity, a surrogate (often next of kin) decides for them — but a surrogate has no authority to override a patient who still has capacity. Two psychiatrists found Cowart had capacity. His mother’s authorization was nonetheless treated as controlling. That is the legal pressure point premise 2 exposes.



The beneficence reply: pain, shock, and “they almost always change their minds”
The physicians did not deny premise 1. They denied premise 2. Their claim was not that Cowart could not talk coherently. It was that a person in catastrophic pain, in shock, facing a future he could not yet imagine, is not making a settled, autonomous choice. He is crying out from an unbearable present. Dr. Larson, on the film Dax’s Case, put both the conviction and its limit in one breath: “No other burn patient has made it so clear … that they really do desire to die. … This is an ongoing battle that we have to individualize.”

The Beneficence / Temporary-Incapacity Reply

If a patient is in extreme pain and shock, their capacity to refuse treatment is undermined.
Dax Cowart was in extreme pain and shock.
Therefore, Dax Cowart lacked the capacity to refuse treatment.


This is essentially the position the philosopher H. Tristram Engelhardt later defended in the Dax’s Case essays. He called it a genuine conflict “at the roots of health care” between freedom and best interest (Kliever 1989). The reply also has empirical backing that did not exist when commentators first took Cowart’s side:

A 1978 NIH consensus statement held that shock makes early decision-making nearly impossible for burn victims.
In a later study, burn survivors interviewed years afterward said almost uniformly that informed consent was unrealistic at the moment of injury.
They said the capacity to consent developed over time.
None thought withdrawing support would have been right for them (Brewster et al. 2006).


Informed consent vs. informed refusal — and how new it was. Informed consent — disclosure plus free agreement before treatment — was barely a decade old in law in 1973. Canterbury v. Spence, which set the modern “reasonable patient” disclosure standard, had been decided only the year before (United States Court of Appeals, District of Columbia Circuit 1972). The harder idea, informed refusal — a competent patient’s right to decline life-saving care — was almost unrecognized. Many physicians trained in an older paternalistic model treated refusal of life-saving treatment as itself evidence of impaired judgment. That is precisely the reasoning premise 1 of the reply formalizes (see Faden and Beauchamp (1986)).

But the reply has a soft spot. Premise 2 is a statistical generalization — most patients are glad later — used to override this patient. Applying an average to the individual in front of you is a recognizably utilitarian move: maximize good outcomes across the class of burn patients. It invites the objection Cowart spent his life pressing.


Cowart’s counter: a good outcome doesn’t make the choice yours
Cowart’s reply is not “you were wrong about the outcome.” He concedes the outcome. His reply is that a good result does not retroactively transfer the decision to the people who made it.

The Retrospective-Endorsement Objection

The right to make a decision does not depend on the outcome of that decision.
Pointing to a patient’s later gladness is an appeal to the outcome.
Therefore, a patient’s later gladness does not prove that the decision belonged to the medical team.


He sharpened it with an analogy: a surgeon who operates on the wrong patient is not vindicated because the patient happens to recover.
This is where a concept from ethical theory does real work. Cowart’s two claims — I am glad to be alive and I was wronged — are not a contradiction. They are a textbook case of moral residue: even when overriding a duty is, on balance, defensible, the overridden duty does not vanish. Something wrongful remains to be acknowledged. As he said: “I do not feel grateful to anyone for fighting my request to die … no, I’m not glad they forcibly treated me because the pain that I went through was pure hell” (Cowart and Burt 1998). From a Kantian angle, the same point is that treating a competent person merely as a means to a future others judge worthwhile fails to respect him as a person who may author his own life — even badly.


Burt’s relational counter: “I won’t abandon you”
The legal scholar Robert Burt, who debated Cowart publicly for years, accepted almost all of this and still resisted the conclusion that the team should simply have complied. His move changes the frame from a transaction (consent given or withheld) to a relationship.

The Relational Counter

Desperate demands to die are often cries for sustained relationship rather than pure refusals.
If a demand is a cry for relationship, then immediately complying with it is a form of abandonment.
Therefore, immediately honoring a patient’s refusal to live can be a form of abandonment.


This is recognizably an ethics of care position: obligations grow out of relationship and dependency, not only out of rights. It is also where the dialogue gets its most famous phrase. Burt kept asking when comes the end of the day? He conceded that staying cannot be indefinite, because a relationship that never lets the patient’s “no” be final has quietly become the old paternalism in kinder language. Cowart’s answer was blunt: “an hour was an eternity, with the burns and the pain I was going through.” Burt’s honest reply was that he did not always know. That unresolved gap is what the next move tries to close.


The Arnold–Menzel procedure: turning principles into a process
Denis Arnold and Paul Menzel took the Burt–Cowart standoff seriously as an unanswered question: when, exactly, does resistance to a competent refusal have to yield? They proposed a step-by-step procedure to answer it without smuggling paternalism back in. Their guiding question reframes the whole debate away from absolute rights. Not “who holds the trump card?” but “Whose pain is it, anyway?” — a question, they write, about who may decide that agonizing pain now is worth a possible good life later (Arnold and Menzel 1998).
The procedure is, in the vocabulary of principlism, an exercise in specification and balancing. It takes two abstract principles that conflict in the abstract — respect for autonomy and beneficence — and turns them into a concrete sequence a real clinical team can follow at the bedside. The decisive split is whether the patient’s pain is being adequately controlled. Uncontrolled pain both clouds judgment and is itself a harm the patient is being forced to endure. Read the diagram as a single question that forks: first fix the pain or honor a short waiting period. Either way, the patient — not the team and not the family — ends up holding the decision.








G



start

Competent patient refuses
life-sustaining treatment



pain

Is severe pain being
adequately managed?



start->pain





prog

Give an honest (not inflated)
prognosis; acknowledge how hard
it is to believe in recovery



pain->prog


  yes



manage

Set an explicit maximum time
to get pain under control;
defer more to the patient



pain->manage


  no



date

Patient and team set an
agreed decision date;
appoint a patient advocate



prog->date





hold

Refusal may be reasonably
declined for a short period
(about a week)



date->hold





honor

The competent patient
controls the decision;
the refusal is honored



manage->honor


  time elapses,
  pain still uncontrolled



hold->honor


  insistence continues
  & advocate concurs






Figure 1: The Arnold–Menzel “end of the day” procedure. Burt asked when a care team must finally stop resisting a competent patient’s refusal; Arnold and Menzel answer with a process rather than a fixed deadline. The pivotal question (gold diamond) is whether severe pain is controlled. If it is, the team owes the patient an honest prognosis, an agreed decision date, and an advocate, and may decline the refusal only for a short period (about a week). If pain is not controlled, the team may hold only long enough to set and pursue an explicit pain-control deadline. On both paths the endpoint (green) is the same: the competent patient controls the decision. Adapted from Arnold & Menzel (1998).







The argument that hasn’t ended: the empirical turn
Cowart’s case itself never produced a court ruling. The legal architecture grew up around it. Two cases were decisive:

In re Quinlan (1976) recognized that life-sustaining treatment could be withdrawn through a surrogate (Supreme Court of New Jersey 1976).
Cruzan v. Director (1990) treated the refusal of such treatment as a protected liberty, while letting states demand clear evidence of the patient’s wishes (Supreme Court of the United States 1990).

Out of that lineage came living wills, durable powers of attorney, and the federal Patient Self-Determination Act. The law moved Cowart’s way. The bedside did not always follow. In a 2019 remembrance, the bioethicist Greg Pence reported a burn physician saying, of patients who ask to die, “They all want to die, and I just ignore them. I don’t let them give up on themselves” (Parsi and Winslade 2019). Advance directives, when they exist, are quietly overridden a large share of the time. The doctrine settled. The disagreement did not.
If the story stopped with Arnold and Menzel, the lesson would still look tidy: respect autonomy, control pain, hand the patient the decision. The most important recent move complicates exactly that lesson — and it comes not from law but from data. The bioethicist Monica Gerrek argues that building burn ethics almost entirely on Cowart has spread a belief — that burn units are inherently paternalistic — that the wider evidence does not support (Gerrek 2018). She sets beside Dax the case of Andrea Rubin, severely burned in 2014 with comparable injuries. The two cases ran in parallel:

Rubin’s friends, like Dax’s, pleaded that she would not want to live this way.
Her father, like Dax’s mother, consented to treatment.
She was sedated for weeks and could not decide.
After recovery, Rubin did not feel betrayed. She believes she genuinely lacked capacity early on, that the team acted in her interest, and that honoring her friends’ pleas would have been a fatal mistake.

Rubin’s case does not refute Cowart. Two psychiatrists found he had capacity, and hers is also just one story. What it does is reopen premise 2 of the original autonomy argument as a genuinely empirical question that cannot be settled from a single famous case. Capacity in catastrophic injury is harder to read, and more variable across people, than either “burn units are tyrannical” or “patients always come around” allows. Cowart himself, asked how a physician could ever be sure a patient truly wants to die, conceded: “I doubt that there is any way a physician can be absolutely sure.” Fifty years on, that is roughly where the argument rests — not at an answer, but at a sharper question, with Cowart’s autonomy claim intact and the certainty once used against it gone on both sides.

title: Dax Cowart and the Right to Refuse Life-Saving Treatment: Autonomy, Beneficence, and Paternalism
text:
Discussion Questions

Write out The Autonomy Argument and The Beneficence Reply in your own words — premises first, then the conclusion. The two arguments disagree about one thing: whether Cowart was competent to refuse during his treatment. Then say what kind of evidence — clinical, philosophical, or statistical — could actually settle that question.
After he recovered, Cowart said two things: he was glad to be alive, and his rights had been violated. The idea of moral residue says a decision can turn out fine and still leave a moral debt behind. Use that idea to explain why his two statements are not a contradiction. What does the idea tell us about paternalistic decisions that “turned out fine”?
The Beneficence Reply uses a generalization about a group — “most patients in Cowart’s situation are later glad they were treated” — to override one person’s choice. When, if ever, is it fair to apply a true generalization about a group to the specific person in front of you? What principle are you relying on?
You are the nurse assigned to Cowart’s daily tank treatment. He has just told you again that he wants it to stop and wants to die. What do you owe him in that moment that the institution does not? What, concretely, do you do?
Apply the Arnold–Menzel procedure to Andrea Rubin’s case. Does the procedure give the answer Rubin would endorse? If not, what does the mismatch tell you — is the procedure flawed, is Rubin’s case importantly different from Cowart’s, or is the argument Cowart started still open?

title: Dax Cowart and the Right to Refuse Life-Saving Treatment: Autonomy, Beneficence, and Paternalism
text:
Further Reading

Please Let Me Die (1974) and Dax’s Case (1984) — the two documentaries; the second includes interviews with Cowart, his physicians, his nurse, and his mother.
Lonnie D. Kliever, ed., Dax’s Case: Essays in Medical Ethics and Human Meaning (Southern Methodist University Press, 1989) (Kliever 1989).
Keith Burton, “A Chronicle: Dax’s Case as It Happened,” in Kliever, ed. (1989) (Burton 1989).
Daniel E. Slotnik, “Dax Cowart, Who Fought for Patients’ Rights, Dies at 71,” The New York Times, May 15, 2019 (Slotnik 2019).

title: Dax Cowart and the Right to Refuse Life-Saving Treatment: Autonomy, Beneficence, and Paternalism
text:

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
It is 1962. A Seattle hospital has just built the world’s first machine that can keep people with failing kidneys alive — but it has only three treatment slots. About sixty patients in the area will die without it. Every one of them is medically eligible. The machine cannot be shared.
You are on the committee that must decide who gets the three slots.
Write or discuss:

What criteria would you use to choose? Be specific.
Would you consider a patient’s job, family, or character? Should you?
Whatever rule you pick — could you defend it to the families of the people who die?





The committee was real. At Seattle’s Swedish Hospital, seven anonymous citizens — a lawyer, a minister, a homemaker, a banker, a labor leader, a state official, and a surgeon — were handed exactly this choice.

They chose largely by social worth: whether a patient had a steady job, went to church, was married, supported children, seemed likely to “contribute.”
The journalist Shana Alexander watched them for six months and published what she saw in LIFE magazine in 1962, under the title “They Decide Who Lives, Who Dies” (Alexander 1962).
The public was horrified — not because the committee was cruel, but because it had no principle beyond its own values.










Note



The committee’s mistake was not bad intentions. It was deciding life and death with no theory of justice — just an instinct about who “deserved” to live.
The reaction to the Seattle committee helped launch the field of bioethics, and within a decade Congress simply paid for dialysis for everyone who needed it (1972). But the deeper question never went away: when a life-saving resource is scarce, by what principle should it be shared?
Today we meet four serious answers — and test each one against a single donated organ.






By the end of Part A, you will be able to:

Explain what distributive justice is and why a “treat like cases alike” rule is not enough.
State the utilitarian, Rawlsian, libertarian, and capabilities theories of justice in plain language.
Apply all four to a concrete allocation problem — who should receive a scarce organ.
Describe how the US actually allocates organs (UNOS), and the ethics of presumed consent and organ markets.
Reconstruct Daniels’ argument that there is a moral right to health care, and the main objections to it.
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

By the end of Part A, you will be able to:

crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
2.1 Distributive Justice
Distributive justice is the part of ethics that asks how a society should divide up benefits and burdens — here, scarce medical care — fairly.

Almost everyone accepts a starting rule, the formal principle of justice: treat like cases alike, and different cases differently.
The trouble is that the formal principle is empty until you say which differences matter. Need? Merit? Ability to pay? Likelihood of benefit?
The Seattle committee thought “social worth” was a relevant difference. The four theories ahead each name a different feature as the one that counts.
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
section: 2.2 The Problem We Will Keep Returning To
text:
2.2 The Problem We Will Keep Returning To
Every theory will be tested on the same hard case, so the differences between them stay visible.

One organ, many candidates.
Today more than 105,000 people in the US are waiting for an organ transplant. Over 92,000 of them — about 87% — need a kidney (Organ Procurement and Transplantation Network (OPTN) 2025). Most will wait years; some will die waiting.
When one donated kidney becomes available and several patients match it, someone decides who gets it. That decision is distributive justice in its rawest form — and each theory answers it differently.
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
2.3 Four Lenses
Each lens fixes on a different question — and that question, not a tidy formula, is what it brings to the bedside.

Utilitarian: what produces the most total good?
Rawlsian: what rules would we accept before knowing who we’d be?
Libertarian: were the holdings and transfers fair?
Capabilities: what does a person need to function as a human being?

Notice these are not four answers to one question. They are four different questions — which is why, as we’ll see, they rarely hand down a single verdict on their own.
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
3.1 The Principle
Utilitarianism says the just distribution is the one that produces the greatest total well-being. Count everyone equally, add up the benefits, and choose the option with the largest sum.

It is outcome-focused: what matters is results, not who gets what or why.
Applied to scarce care, it becomes a simple instruction: do the most good per unit of a scarce resource.
This is the logic behind cost-effectiveness analysis throughout modern medicine.
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
3.2 Applied to the Organ
A first pass says: give the kidney to whoever gains the most good — usually the most years of healthy life. But “total well-being” is bigger than one patient.

A utilitarian also weighs system-wide effects: rules that keep public trust high, that encourage more people to donate, that don’t frighten families away.
So the lens doesn’t simply crown a recipient. It might favor opt-out, or even a market (more organs, more lives) — or oppose a market if payment crowds out altruistic donation and shrinks the supply.


The QALY. Health economists measure benefit in quality-adjusted life years (QALYs). One year in perfect health = 1 QALY; a year in poor health counts as less.
Britain’s NICE uses roughly this math to decide what the NHS funds — often around £20,000–£30,000 per QALY. It is the utilitarian instinct turned into a spreadsheet. But notice it answers “is this worth it overall?”, not “which patient deserves it?”
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
3.3 Where It Bites
The strength of the utilitarian lens — maximize benefit — is also its danger.

It can sacrifice the few for the many: a patient who would gain only a few good years loses to one who would gain decades, every time.
QALY math can systematically disadvantage the elderly and the disabled, because they are counted as having fewer or “lower-quality” years to gain.
It treats the individual as a site where good is produced, not as someone with a claim. That is exactly what the next lens insists on.
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
4.1 Justice as Fairness
John Rawls asked a different question: not “what maximizes good?” but “what rules would we choose if we were fair?” (Rawls 1971)

The original position. Imagine designing a society’s rules from behind a veil of ignorance: you know nothing about who you will be — your wealth, talents, health, or whether you’ll be the patient who needs the kidney.
Rawls argues that rational people choosing under that veil would not gamble on landing at the bottom. They would choose rules that protect everyone, especially the worst-off — because that worst-off person might be them.
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
4.2 Two Principles, In Order
Behind the veil, Rawls argues, we would choose two principles — and the order matters as much as the content.

Equal basic liberties — the same rights and freedoms for all (speech, conscience, bodily integrity).
(a) Fair equality of opportunity, and (b) the difference principle: inequalities are just only if they help the least advantaged.

Crucially, the first principle is lexically prior: you may not trade away basic liberties for greater welfare, however large the gain. That priority is exactly what divides Rawls from the utilitarian — no one’s liberty is a line item in someone else’s sum.
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
section: 4.3 Applied to the Organ — with a Caveat
text:
4.3 Applied to the Organ — with a Caveat

A caution Rawls himself would press. Rawls’s principles govern the basic structure of society — its constitution and institutions — not who gets the next kidney. Using the difference principle to micro-allocate one organ is an extension of his view, not a verdict it strictly delivers.

Read in a Rawlsian spirit (or its prioritarian cousin — give extra weight to the worse-off), the lens does two things:

It rejects the Seattle committee’s “social worth”: behind the veil, no one would agree to be judged on their job or churchgoing.
It tilts toward the worst-off — the sickest, or those with the least access — even when that isn’t the choice that maximizes total life-years. Whether it picks a specific recipient still depends on how you build the institution.
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
4.4 Thought Question

Two patients match the one available kidney.

Patient A is 30, otherwise healthy, and would likely gain ~40 good years.
Patient B is 55, sicker, and would likely gain ~12 good years — and has already been waiting three years on dialysis.


Who does the utilitarian choose? Who does the Rawlsian lean toward?
Behind the veil of ignorance — not knowing if you’re A or B — which rule would you pick?
Does “years gained” feel like the right measure of a fair claim? What’s missing?
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
5.1 Entitlement, Not Distribution
Robert Nozick rejects the whole question. There is no “just distribution” to engineer — only just transactions (Nozick 1974).

A distribution is just if people acquired what they have fairly (without theft or fraud) and transferred it freely.
On this view, no one is owed health care. Care is a service, produced by others, that you obtain by buying it or being given it.
Forcing some to pay for others’ care is, for Nozick, a kind of coercion — taking what people justly earned.
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
5.2 Applied to the Organ
The libertarian focuses on one fact the other theories skip: the kidney belonged to someone.

An organ is the donor’s to give — so donor wishes, including directed donation (“give it to this person, or people like me”), carry real moral weight.
From self-ownership, the logic runs that what you may give, you may also sell — pointing toward a market.
But libertarians don’t all land there. Nozick’s Lockean proviso (“enough, and as good, left for others”) and the requirement that acquisitions be just can constrain markets that prey on the desperate — and left-libertarians read self-ownership very differently. The lens leans toward markets; it doesn’t compel them.
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
5.3 The Market Made Real: Iran


Since 1988, Iran has run the world’s only legal, regulated kidney market. Living donors are paid — officially about $1,400, plus negotiated payments from recipients.
The result libertarians predict actually happened: Iran eliminated its kidney waiting list by around 1999 (Ghods and Savaj 2006). People who would have died on dialysis received transplants.







Important



The catch
In surveys, about 43% of Iranian donors said their motive was purely financial, and roughly three-quarters said selling kidneys should be banned. The sellers are overwhelmingly poor.
A market clears the list — by routing the burden onto people who feel they have no choice.
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
section: 6.1 What People Are Actually Able to Do
text:
6.1 What People Are Actually Able to Do
Amartya Sen and Martha Nussbaum offer a fourth lens: justice is about securing the real freedoms and functionings people need to live a fully human life (Sen 1999; Nussbaum 2011).

A capability is a genuine opportunity to do or be something a person has reason to value — to be healthy, to move about, to take part in their community.
Money and even “welfare” are only means. What matters is whether a person can actually function.
On Nussbaum’s list, bodily health is a central capability that a just society must secure for everyone above a real threshold.
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
6.2 Applied to the Organ
The capabilities lens asks not “who gains the most life-years?” but “what does this person need to function as a human being?”

Against the utilitarian, it refuses to reduce a person to a QALY score. A disabled candidate is not “worth less”; the question is what they need to flourish.
Against the libertarian, it treats health as something a just society owes people, not merely a good they may buy.
But it, too, under-determines the rule: proponents disagree about what it requires, Sen resists a fixed list, and “secure a threshold for all” doesn’t say who gets this organ when there isn’t enough to go around.
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
6.3 Same Patient, Two Verdicts


The utilitarian counts a candidate with a permanent disability as gaining fewer “quality-adjusted” years — and so ranks them lower for the organ.



The capabilities theorist asks what this person needs to live a full human life, and rejects the idea that a disabled life is worth less. The disability lowers their score in one lens and is irrelevant to their claim in the other.


On the same disabled candidate, the two lenses weigh the very same facts in opposite directions. That is why which considerations you treat as decisive is not academic.
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
7.1 The Theories Head to Head
Each lens foregrounds a different consideration — and that, not a final verdict, is what it contributes.








Lens
What it makes salient
Why it doesn’t settle policy alone




Utilitarian
total benefit, system-wide effects
depends on the empirical facts; can back many rules


Rawlsian
basic liberties, then the worst-off
governs institutions, not one organ


Libertarian
whose it was; consent; fair transfer
the proviso and “just acquisition” cut both ways


Capabilities
what a person needs to function
proponents disagree on what it requires
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
7.2 Theories Don’t Hand Down Policies






Important



The single most common mistake in these debates is to say “utilitarianism requires a market” or “Rawls forbids it.” None of these theories delivers a policy by itself.

Each one tells you what to weigh — total welfare, the worst-off, consent, capabilities.
Turning that into a rule needs empirical facts (does a market grow or shrink supply?) and interpretive choices (what does “fair acquisition,” or “the threshold,” actually demand?).

So the lenses don’t end the argument. They tell you what the argument is about — and that is exactly what the Seattle committee never worked out.
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
7.3 Discussion

Look back at the Seattle “God Committee.”

Which of the four lenses, if any, was the committee using when it judged “social worth”?
Which lens would have most changed its decisions — and how?
If you had to build a real allocation system, which lens would you make the backbone, and which would you keep only as a check?
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
8.1 The UNOS System
In the US, the Organ Procurement and Transplantation Network, run through UNOS, matches donated organs to waiting patients by a published algorithm — not a committee’s gut feeling (Organ Procurement and Transplantation Network (OPTN) 2025).
The algorithm blends factors that, in fact, come straight from our four lenses:

Medical urgency — how sick the patient is (a nod to Rawls’ worst-off).
Expected benefit — how well and how long the graft will work (utilitarian).
Time on the waiting list — first-come fairness.
Matching and proximity — blood type, tissue, distance.
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
8.2 Three Principles in Tension







G



organ

One donated
organ



sick

Sickest first
(urgency / worst-off)



organ->sick





best

Best outcome
(most life-years)



organ->best





fcfs

First-come
(time waiting)



organ->fcfs





conflict



Every allocation rule
picks a trade-off



sick->conflict


who wins?



best->conflict





fcfs->conflict










“Sickest first” and “best outcome” often point at different patients — the sickest patient may do worst with the organ. No rule escapes the trade-off.
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
section: 8.3 Where Do Organs Come From? Opt-In vs. Opt-Out
text:
8.3 Where Do Organs Come From? Opt-In vs. Opt-Out


The US uses opt-in: you are a donor only if you register. About 48 donors per million people.
Spain uses presumed consent (soft opt-out): everyone is a donor unless they say no. Spain leads the world at 52.6 per million (2024) (Matesanz et al. 2017).


Why Spain really wins
The law is not the whole story. Spain pairs opt-out with the ONT — full-time donation coordinators in every hospital and skilled family support. Spanish families consent about 8 times in 10; the UK, also opt-out, gets about 6 in 10.
Infrastructure, not just the default rule, drives the result.
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
section: 8.4 When Money and Choice Bend the System
text:
8.4 When Money and Choice Bend the System
Even a need-based system has seams that wealth and preference can widen.

Directed and conditional donation lets donors steer organs toward specific people or groups — honoring the donor’s liberty, but pulling against equal access.
Multi-listing: a patient can register at several transplant centers in different regions at once. The famous example is Steve Jobs, who registered in Tennessee while living in California and received a liver there.
It is legal. It is also a reminder that the ability to travel and pay can quietly improve your odds in a system meant to run on need.
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
8.5 Philosophers Take Sides
This is a live debate among serious philosophers — and it does not split neatly along left and right.


Allow regulated sales

Radcliffe-Richards and colleagues: if we let people take risks for money in far more dangerous jobs, banning kidney sales is inconsistent paternalism (Radcliffe-Richards et al. 1998).
Cherry: self-ownership and consent make a regulated market legitimate — and it saves lives (Cherry 2005).




Forbid them

Satz: kidney markets are noxious — built on desperation and weak agency, with harmful, unequal outcomes (Satz 2010).
Sandel: pricing organs corrupts how we value the body and one another, regardless of consent (Sandel 2012).



Note both sides include egalitarians and libertarians — the disagreement is about consent, exploitation, and what money does to a thing, not simply about markets.
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
8.6 Thought Question

You are a healthy college student, deep in debt. A patient on dialysis offers you $30,000 for one of your kidneys. You can live a normal life with one. The exchange is voluntary, and it would save the patient’s life and clear your debt.

Is there anything wrong with this trade? If so, what exactly?
Does your answer change if the seller is desperate rather than merely willing?
Would you feel differently about selling blood plasma, or an egg, or a lung? Why?
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
8.7 Make It Contemporary

The US bans organ sales outright — the National Organ Transplant Act of 1984 makes it a federal crime (United States Congress 1984). Iran allows them, and has no kidney waiting list.
So the trade-off is stark and real: the US protects the poor from being pressured to sell, and tens of thousands wait — some die. Iran clears the list, and its sellers are overwhelmingly poor and largely regretful.
Discuss: If a regulated market would save thousands of lives a year, is banning it a moral stand — or a comfortable way of letting people die so we don’t have to watch the poor sell their organs?
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
8.8 After You’ve Discussed






Note



The kidney-market debate turns on three ideas people tend to blur:

Coercion — a threat removes your choice (“sell, or I hurt you”).
Undue inducement — an offer is so large or your need so desperate that you can’t reason well about the risk.
Exploitation — a deal is voluntary and mutually beneficial, yet still unfair because one side takes advantage of the other’s vulnerability.

A kidney sale may involve no coercion at all and still be exploitative. Lurking under all three is commodification — the worry that some things (organs, votes, people) are degraded the moment they carry a price.
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
section: 9.1 From One Organ to the Whole System
text:
9.1 From One Organ to the Whole System
The organ case was a stand-in. The same theories now face the largest distributive question of all: does a person have a right to health care at all?

Two kinds of rights.

A negative right is a right to be left alone — free speech, the right to refuse treatment. It only requires others not to interfere.
A positive right is a right to be provided something — and it requires others to act, and usually to pay.

“A right to health care” is a positive right. That is exactly why it is contested: it places a claim on other people’s resources.
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
9.2 Daniels: Health and Fair Opportunity
Norman Daniels builds the strongest case for a right to care by extending Rawls (Daniels 2008).

Rawls says justice requires fair equality of opportunity: your life chances shouldn’t be set by the lottery of birth.
Disease and disability, Daniels argues, shrink your range of opportunity just as surely as poor schooling does — they shut doors a healthy person can walk through.
So a society already committed to fair opportunity is committed, by its own logic, to the health care that restores normal functioning.


On this view, health care isn’t charity. It’s what fair opportunity requires.
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
9.3 The Counterarguments


The libertarian (Nozick)
There is no positive right to care, because it forces some to fund others. Compassion may be a virtue, but it cannot be coerced through taxation without violating people’s just holdings.



The utilitarian
Whether to guarantee care is not a matter of right at all — it’s an empirical question. Guarantee it if and only if doing so maximizes overall welfare. The answer could change with the budget.


Daniels says opportunity grounds a right; Nozick says rights run the other way; the utilitarian says “right” is the wrong word. Three lenses, three verdicts.
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
9.4 Where the US Stands
The US is the rich-world outlier, and its pattern is revealing.

It treats education as a right (free K–12), food and housing as partial rights (SNAP, vouchers) — floors below which it tries not to let people fall.
But it treats most health care as a market good: something you get mainly through a job, a purchase, or by qualifying for a program.
The result is a patchwork with real gaps — which is the subject of Part B.
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
9.5 The Cancer Drug Test

A new cancer therapy (CAR-T, a real treatment) can cost around $475,000 for a single course. A public insurer must decide whether to cover it.
How does each lens rule?

Utilitarian: is the QALY gain worth $475,000 versus spending it elsewhere?
Rawlsian / Daniels: does fair opportunity require funding it for the worst-off who can’t pay?
Libertarian: is anyone owed this at all — or should those who want it buy it?

Which answer can you live with — and who pays for it?
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
9.6 Mini-Recap

We started with seven citizens in Seattle deciding who lived, and asked what principle could replace their guesswork. Four answered: the utilitarian (maximize good), Rawls (protect the worst-off), the libertarian (free transactions, even markets), and the capabilities view (secure what people need to function). We watched them split over a single kidney, over selling organs, and over whether health care is a right at all — with Daniels arguing it follows from fair opportunity.
We now have the values. Part B brings the facts: how the actual US health care system spends the most in the world and yet leaves Americans sicker and shorter-lived than their peers — and what, by these four lenses, we might owe each other.
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
9.7 Review Questions


Recall
State the difference principle and explain how a Rawlsian and a utilitarian would allocate one kidney differently.


Apply
A city has one ICU bed and two patients: a 40-year-old with a 70% survival chance and a 70-year-old with a 40% chance. Pick a theory of justice and defend who gets the bed.


Debate
Is there a moral right to health care? Defend your answer using Daniels and at least one objection to him.
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
9.8 Key Terms

Distributive justice — how a society fairly divides scarce benefits and burdens.
Utilitarianism — maximize total well-being; outcome-focused.
Veil of ignorance — choose rules not knowing your place (Rawls).
Priority of liberty / difference principle — equal basic liberties come first (lexically prior); then inequalities must help the worst-off (Rawls).
Entitlement theory — justice is fair acquisition and transfer, not a pattern (Nozick).
Capabilities — the real freedoms people need to function (Sen, Nussbaum).
QALY — quality-adjusted life year; a unit of health benefit.
Presumed consent — everyone is an organ donor unless they opt out.
Negative vs. positive rights — a right to non-interference vs. a right to be provided something.
Fair equality of opportunity — the Rawlsian idea Daniels uses to ground a right to care.
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
9.9 Further Reading

Rawls, John. A Theory of Justice. Harvard University Press, 1971.
Daniels, Norman. Just Health: Meeting Health Needs Fairly. Cambridge University Press, 2008.
Nussbaum, Martha. Creating Capabilities. Harvard University Press, 2011.
crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

text:
9.10 References

crumbs: Part A — Theories of Justice & the Right to Care

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
A Case to Sit With

It is 1962. A Seattle hospital has just built the world’s first machine that can keep people with failing kidneys alive — but it has only three treatment slots. About sixty patients in the area will die without it. Every one of them is medically eligible. The machine cannot be shared.
You are on the committee that must decide who gets the three slots.
Write or discuss:

What criteria would you use to choose? Be specific.
Would you consider a patient’s job, family, or character? Should you?
Whatever rule you pick — could you defend it to the families of the people who die?

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
What the Committee Actually Did
The committee was real. At Seattle’s Swedish Hospital, seven anonymous citizens — a lawyer, a minister, a homemaker, a banker, a labor leader, a state official, and a surgeon — were handed exactly this choice.

They chose largely by social worth: whether a patient had a steady job, went to church, was married, supported children, seemed likely to “contribute.”
The journalist Shana Alexander watched them for six months and published what she saw in LIFE magazine in 1962, under the title “They Decide Who Lives, Who Dies” (Alexander 1962).
The public was horrified — not because the committee was cruel, but because it had no principle beyond its own values.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


The committee’s mistake was not bad intentions. It was deciding life and death with no theory of justice — just an instinct about who “deserved” to live.
The reaction to the Seattle committee helped launch the field of bioethics, and within a decade Congress simply paid for dialysis for everyone who needed it (1972). But the deeper question never went away: when a life-saving resource is scarce, by what principle should it be shared?
Today we meet four serious answers — and test each one against a single donated organ.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Today

By the end of Part A, you will be able to:

Explain what distributive justice is and why a “treat like cases alike” rule is not enough.
State the utilitarian, Rawlsian, libertarian, and capabilities theories of justice in plain language.
Apply all four to a concrete allocation problem — who should receive a scarce organ.
Describe how the US actually allocates organs (UNOS), and the ethics of presumed consent and organ markets.
Reconstruct Daniels’ argument that there is a moral right to health care, and the main objections to it.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Distributive Justice
Distributive justice is the part of ethics that asks how a society should divide up benefits and burdens — here, scarce medical care — fairly.

Almost everyone accepts a starting rule, the formal principle of justice: treat like cases alike, and different cases differently.
The trouble is that the formal principle is empty until you say which differences matter. Need? Merit? Ability to pay? Likelihood of benefit?
The Seattle committee thought “social worth” was a relevant difference. The four theories ahead each name a different feature as the one that counts.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
section: The Problem We Will Keep Returning To
text:
The Problem We Will Keep Returning To
Every theory will be tested on the same hard case, so the differences between them stay visible.

One organ, many candidates.
Today more than 105,000 people in the US are waiting for an organ transplant. Over 92,000 of them — about 87% — need a kidney (Organ Procurement and Transplantation Network (OPTN) 2025). Most will wait years; some will die waiting.
When one donated kidney becomes available and several patients match it, someone decides who gets it. That decision is distributive justice in its rawest form — and each theory answers it differently.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Four Lenses
Each lens fixes on a different question — and that question, not a tidy formula, is what it brings to the bedside.

Utilitarian: what produces the most total good?
Rawlsian: what rules would we accept before knowing who we’d be?
Libertarian: were the holdings and transfers fair?
Capabilities: what does a person need to function as a human being?

Notice these are not four answers to one question. They are four different questions — which is why, as we’ll see, they rarely hand down a single verdict on their own.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
The Principle
Utilitarianism says the just distribution is the one that produces the greatest total well-being. Count everyone equally, add up the benefits, and choose the option with the largest sum.

It is outcome-focused: what matters is results, not who gets what or why.
Applied to scarce care, it becomes a simple instruction: do the most good per unit of a scarce resource.
This is the logic behind cost-effectiveness analysis throughout modern medicine.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Applied to the Organ
A first pass says: give the kidney to whoever gains the most good — usually the most years of healthy life. But “total well-being” is bigger than one patient.

A utilitarian also weighs system-wide effects: rules that keep public trust high, that encourage more people to donate, that don’t frighten families away.
So the lens doesn’t simply crown a recipient. It might favor opt-out, or even a market (more organs, more lives) — or oppose a market if payment crowds out altruistic donation and shrinks the supply.


The QALY. Health economists measure benefit in quality-adjusted life years (QALYs). One year in perfect health = 1 QALY; a year in poor health counts as less.
Britain’s NICE uses roughly this math to decide what the NHS funds — often around £20,000–£30,000 per QALY. It is the utilitarian instinct turned into a spreadsheet. But notice it answers “is this worth it overall?”, not “which patient deserves it?”

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Where It Bites
The strength of the utilitarian lens — maximize benefit — is also its danger.

It can sacrifice the few for the many: a patient who would gain only a few good years loses to one who would gain decades, every time.
QALY math can systematically disadvantage the elderly and the disabled, because they are counted as having fewer or “lower-quality” years to gain.
It treats the individual as a site where good is produced, not as someone with a claim. That is exactly what the next lens insists on.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Justice as Fairness
John Rawls asked a different question: not “what maximizes good?” but “what rules would we choose if we were fair?” (Rawls 1971)

The original position. Imagine designing a society’s rules from behind a veil of ignorance: you know nothing about who you will be — your wealth, talents, health, or whether you’ll be the patient who needs the kidney.
Rawls argues that rational people choosing under that veil would not gamble on landing at the bottom. They would choose rules that protect everyone, especially the worst-off — because that worst-off person might be them.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Two Principles, In Order
Behind the veil, Rawls argues, we would choose two principles — and the order matters as much as the content.

Equal basic liberties — the same rights and freedoms for all (speech, conscience, bodily integrity).
(a) Fair equality of opportunity, and (b) the difference principle: inequalities are just only if they help the least advantaged.

Crucially, the first principle is lexically prior: you may not trade away basic liberties for greater welfare, however large the gain. That priority is exactly what divides Rawls from the utilitarian — no one’s liberty is a line item in someone else’s sum.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
section: Applied to the Organ — with a Caveat
text:
Applied to the Organ — with a Caveat

A caution Rawls himself would press. Rawls’s principles govern the basic structure of society — its constitution and institutions — not who gets the next kidney. Using the difference principle to micro-allocate one organ is an extension of his view, not a verdict it strictly delivers.

Read in a Rawlsian spirit (or its prioritarian cousin — give extra weight to the worse-off), the lens does two things:

It rejects the Seattle committee’s “social worth”: behind the veil, no one would agree to be judged on their job or churchgoing.
It tilts toward the worst-off — the sickest, or those with the least access — even when that isn’t the choice that maximizes total life-years. Whether it picks a specific recipient still depends on how you build the institution.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Thought Question

Two patients match the one available kidney.

Patient A is 30, otherwise healthy, and would likely gain ~40 good years.
Patient B is 55, sicker, and would likely gain ~12 good years — and has already been waiting three years on dialysis.


Who does the utilitarian choose? Who does the Rawlsian lean toward?
Behind the veil of ignorance — not knowing if you’re A or B — which rule would you pick?
Does “years gained” feel like the right measure of a fair claim? What’s missing?

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Entitlement, Not Distribution
Robert Nozick rejects the whole question. There is no “just distribution” to engineer — only just transactions (Nozick 1974).

A distribution is just if people acquired what they have fairly (without theft or fraud) and transferred it freely.
On this view, no one is owed health care. Care is a service, produced by others, that you obtain by buying it or being given it.
Forcing some to pay for others’ care is, for Nozick, a kind of coercion — taking what people justly earned.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Applied to the Organ
The libertarian focuses on one fact the other theories skip: the kidney belonged to someone.

An organ is the donor’s to give — so donor wishes, including directed donation (“give it to this person, or people like me”), carry real moral weight.
From self-ownership, the logic runs that what you may give, you may also sell — pointing toward a market.
But libertarians don’t all land there. Nozick’s Lockean proviso (“enough, and as good, left for others”) and the requirement that acquisitions be just can constrain markets that prey on the desperate — and left-libertarians read self-ownership very differently. The lens leans toward markets; it doesn’t compel them.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
The Market Made Real: Iran


Since 1988, Iran has run the world’s only legal, regulated kidney market. Living donors are paid — officially about $1,400, plus negotiated payments from recipients.
The result libertarians predict actually happened: Iran eliminated its kidney waiting list by around 1999 (Ghods and Savaj 2006). People who would have died on dialysis received transplants.







Important


The catch
In surveys, about 43% of Iranian donors said their motive was purely financial, and roughly three-quarters said selling kidneys should be banned. The sellers are overwhelmingly poor.
A market clears the list — by routing the burden onto people who feel they have no choice.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
section: What People Are Actually Able to Do
text:
What People Are Actually Able to Do
Amartya Sen and Martha Nussbaum offer a fourth lens: justice is about securing the real freedoms and functionings people need to live a fully human life (Sen 1999; Nussbaum 2011).

A capability is a genuine opportunity to do or be something a person has reason to value — to be healthy, to move about, to take part in their community.
Money and even “welfare” are only means. What matters is whether a person can actually function.
On Nussbaum’s list, bodily health is a central capability that a just society must secure for everyone above a real threshold.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Applied to the Organ
The capabilities lens asks not “who gains the most life-years?” but “what does this person need to function as a human being?”

Against the utilitarian, it refuses to reduce a person to a QALY score. A disabled candidate is not “worth less”; the question is what they need to flourish.
Against the libertarian, it treats health as something a just society owes people, not merely a good they may buy.
But it, too, under-determines the rule: proponents disagree about what it requires, Sen resists a fixed list, and “secure a threshold for all” doesn’t say who gets this organ when there isn’t enough to go around.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Same Patient, Two Verdicts


The utilitarian counts a candidate with a permanent disability as gaining fewer “quality-adjusted” years — and so ranks them lower for the organ.



The capabilities theorist asks what this person needs to live a full human life, and rejects the idea that a disabled life is worth less. The disability lowers their score in one lens and is irrelevant to their claim in the other.

On the same disabled candidate, the two lenses weigh the very same facts in opposite directions. That is why which considerations you treat as decisive is not academic.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
The Theories Head to Head
Each lens foregrounds a different consideration — and that, not a final verdict, is what it contributes.








Lens
What it makes salient
Why it doesn’t settle policy alone




Utilitarian
total benefit, system-wide effects
depends on the empirical facts; can back many rules


Rawlsian
basic liberties, then the worst-off
governs institutions, not one organ


Libertarian
whose it was; consent; fair transfer
the proviso and “just acquisition” cut both ways


Capabilities
what a person needs to function
proponents disagree on what it requires

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Theories Don’t Hand Down Policies






Important


The single most common mistake in these debates is to say “utilitarianism requires a market” or “Rawls forbids it.” None of these theories delivers a policy by itself.

Each one tells you what to weigh — total welfare, the worst-off, consent, capabilities.
Turning that into a rule needs empirical facts (does a market grow or shrink supply?) and interpretive choices (what does “fair acquisition,” or “the threshold,” actually demand?).

So the lenses don’t end the argument. They tell you what the argument is about — and that is exactly what the Seattle committee never worked out.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Discussion

Look back at the Seattle “God Committee.”

Which of the four lenses, if any, was the committee using when it judged “social worth”?
Which lens would have most changed its decisions — and how?
If you had to build a real allocation system, which lens would you make the backbone, and which would you keep only as a check?

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
The UNOS System
In the US, the Organ Procurement and Transplantation Network, run through UNOS, matches donated organs to waiting patients by a published algorithm — not a committee’s gut feeling (Organ Procurement and Transplantation Network (OPTN) 2025).
The algorithm blends factors that, in fact, come straight from our four lenses:

Medical urgency — how sick the patient is (a nod to Rawls’ worst-off).
Expected benefit — how well and how long the graft will work (utilitarian).
Time on the waiting list — first-come fairness.
Matching and proximity — blood type, tissue, distance.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Three Principles in Tension







G



organ

One donated
organ



sick

Sickest first
(urgency / worst-off)



organ->sick





best

Best outcome
(most life-years)



organ->best





fcfs

First-come
(time waiting)



organ->fcfs





conflict



Every allocation rule
picks a trade-off



sick->conflict


who wins?



best->conflict





fcfs->conflict










“Sickest first” and “best outcome” often point at different patients — the sickest patient may do worst with the organ. No rule escapes the trade-off.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
section: Where Do Organs Come From? Opt-In vs. Opt-Out
text:
Where Do Organs Come From? Opt-In vs. Opt-Out


The US uses opt-in: you are a donor only if you register. About 48 donors per million people.
Spain uses presumed consent (soft opt-out): everyone is a donor unless they say no. Spain leads the world at 52.6 per million (2024) (Matesanz et al. 2017).


Why Spain really wins
The law is not the whole story. Spain pairs opt-out with the ONT — full-time donation coordinators in every hospital and skilled family support. Spanish families consent about 8 times in 10; the UK, also opt-out, gets about 6 in 10.
Infrastructure, not just the default rule, drives the result.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
section: When Money and Choice Bend the System
text:
When Money and Choice Bend the System
Even a need-based system has seams that wealth and preference can widen.

Directed and conditional donation lets donors steer organs toward specific people or groups — honoring the donor’s liberty, but pulling against equal access.
Multi-listing: a patient can register at several transplant centers in different regions at once. The famous example is Steve Jobs, who registered in Tennessee while living in California and received a liver there.
It is legal. It is also a reminder that the ability to travel and pay can quietly improve your odds in a system meant to run on need.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Philosophers Take Sides
This is a live debate among serious philosophers — and it does not split neatly along left and right.


Allow regulated sales

Radcliffe-Richards and colleagues: if we let people take risks for money in far more dangerous jobs, banning kidney sales is inconsistent paternalism (Radcliffe-Richards et al. 1998).
Cherry: self-ownership and consent make a regulated market legitimate — and it saves lives (Cherry 2005).




Forbid them

Satz: kidney markets are noxious — built on desperation and weak agency, with harmful, unequal outcomes (Satz 2010).
Sandel: pricing organs corrupts how we value the body and one another, regardless of consent (Sandel 2012).


Note both sides include egalitarians and libertarians — the disagreement is about consent, exploitation, and what money does to a thing, not simply about markets.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Thought Question

You are a healthy college student, deep in debt. A patient on dialysis offers you $30,000 for one of your kidneys. You can live a normal life with one. The exchange is voluntary, and it would save the patient’s life and clear your debt.

Is there anything wrong with this trade? If so, what exactly?
Does your answer change if the seller is desperate rather than merely willing?
Would you feel differently about selling blood plasma, or an egg, or a lung? Why?

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Make It Contemporary

The US bans organ sales outright — the National Organ Transplant Act of 1984 makes it a federal crime (United States Congress 1984). Iran allows them, and has no kidney waiting list.
So the trade-off is stark and real: the US protects the poor from being pressured to sell, and tens of thousands wait — some die. Iran clears the list, and its sellers are overwhelmingly poor and largely regretful.
Discuss: If a regulated market would save thousands of lives a year, is banning it a moral stand — or a comfortable way of letting people die so we don’t have to watch the poor sell their organs?

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


The kidney-market debate turns on three ideas people tend to blur:

Coercion — a threat removes your choice (“sell, or I hurt you”).
Undue inducement — an offer is so large or your need so desperate that you can’t reason well about the risk.
Exploitation — a deal is voluntary and mutually beneficial, yet still unfair because one side takes advantage of the other’s vulnerability.

A kidney sale may involve no coercion at all and still be exploitative. Lurking under all three is commodification — the worry that some things (organs, votes, people) are degraded the moment they carry a price.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
section: From One Organ to the Whole System
text:
From One Organ to the Whole System
The organ case was a stand-in. The same theories now face the largest distributive question of all: does a person have a right to health care at all?

Two kinds of rights.

A negative right is a right to be left alone — free speech, the right to refuse treatment. It only requires others not to interfere.
A positive right is a right to be provided something — and it requires others to act, and usually to pay.

“A right to health care” is a positive right. That is exactly why it is contested: it places a claim on other people’s resources.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Daniels: Health and Fair Opportunity
Norman Daniels builds the strongest case for a right to care by extending Rawls (Daniels 2008).

Rawls says justice requires fair equality of opportunity: your life chances shouldn’t be set by the lottery of birth.
Disease and disability, Daniels argues, shrink your range of opportunity just as surely as poor schooling does — they shut doors a healthy person can walk through.
So a society already committed to fair opportunity is committed, by its own logic, to the health care that restores normal functioning.


On this view, health care isn’t charity. It’s what fair opportunity requires.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
The Counterarguments


The libertarian (Nozick)
There is no positive right to care, because it forces some to fund others. Compassion may be a virtue, but it cannot be coerced through taxation without violating people’s just holdings.



The utilitarian
Whether to guarantee care is not a matter of right at all — it’s an empirical question. Guarantee it if and only if doing so maximizes overall welfare. The answer could change with the budget.

Daniels says opportunity grounds a right; Nozick says rights run the other way; the utilitarian says “right” is the wrong word. Three lenses, three verdicts.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Where the US Stands
The US is the rich-world outlier, and its pattern is revealing.

It treats education as a right (free K–12), food and housing as partial rights (SNAP, vouchers) — floors below which it tries not to let people fall.
But it treats most health care as a market good: something you get mainly through a job, a purchase, or by qualifying for a program.
The result is a patchwork with real gaps — which is the subject of Part B.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
The Cancer Drug Test

A new cancer therapy (CAR-T, a real treatment) can cost around $475,000 for a single course. A public insurer must decide whether to cover it.
How does each lens rule?

Utilitarian: is the QALY gain worth $475,000 versus spending it elsewhere?
Rawlsian / Daniels: does fair opportunity require funding it for the worst-off who can’t pay?
Libertarian: is anyone owed this at all — or should those who want it buy it?

Which answer can you live with — and who pays for it?

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Mini-Recap

We started with seven citizens in Seattle deciding who lived, and asked what principle could replace their guesswork. Four answered: the utilitarian (maximize good), Rawls (protect the worst-off), the libertarian (free transactions, even markets), and the capabilities view (secure what people need to function). We watched them split over a single kidney, over selling organs, and over whether health care is a right at all — with Daniels arguing it follows from fair opportunity.
We now have the values. Part B brings the facts: how the actual US health care system spends the most in the world and yet leaves Americans sicker and shorter-lived than their peers — and what, by these four lenses, we might owe each other.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Review Questions


Recall
State the difference principle and explain how a Rawlsian and a utilitarian would allocate one kidney differently.


Apply
A city has one ICU bed and two patients: a 40-year-old with a 70% survival chance and a 70-year-old with a 40% chance. Pick a theory of justice and defend who gets the bed.


Debate
Is there a moral right to health care? Defend your answer using Daniels and at least one objection to him.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Key Terms

Distributive justice — how a society fairly divides scarce benefits and burdens.
Utilitarianism — maximize total well-being; outcome-focused.
Veil of ignorance — choose rules not knowing your place (Rawls).
Priority of liberty / difference principle — equal basic liberties come first (lexically prior); then inequalities must help the worst-off (Rawls).
Entitlement theory — justice is fair acquisition and transfer, not a pattern (Nozick).
Capabilities — the real freedoms people need to function (Sen, Nussbaum).
QALY — quality-adjusted life year; a unit of health benefit.
Presumed consent — everyone is an organ donor unless they opt out.
Negative vs. positive rights — a right to non-interference vs. a right to be provided something.
Fair equality of opportunity — the Rawlsian idea Daniels uses to ground a right to care.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Further Reading

Rawls, John. A Theory of Justice. Harvard University Press, 1971.
Daniels, Norman. Just Health: Meeting Health Needs Fairly. Cambridge University Press, 2008.
Nussbaum, Martha. Creating Capabilities. Harvard University Press, 2011.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
References


Alexander, Shana. 1962. “They Decide Who Lives, Who Dies.” Life 53 (19): 102–25.


Cherry, Mark J. 2005. Kidney for Sale by Owner: Human Organs, Transplantation, and the Market. Georgetown University Press.


Daniels, Norman. 2008. Just Health: Meeting Health Needs Fairly. Cambridge University Press.

Matesanz, Rafael, Beatriz Domínguez-Gil, Elisabeth Coll, Beatriz Mahíllo, and Rocío Marazuela. 2017. “How Spain Reached 40 Deceased Organ Donors Per Million Population.” American Journal of Transplantation 17 (6): 1447–54. https://doi.org/10.1111/ajt.14104.


Nozick, Robert. 1974. Anarchy, State, and Utopia. Basic Books.

Radcliffe-Richards, Janet, A. S. Daar, R. D. Guttmann, et al. 1998. “The Case for Allowing Kidney Sales.” The Lancet 351 (9120): 1950–52. https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(97)08211-1.


Rawls, John. 1971. A Theory of Justice. Harvard University Press.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
In January 1983, 25-year-old Nancy Cruzan lost control of her car on an icy Missouri road and was thrown into a ditch. Paramedics restarted her heart, but her brain had gone too long without oxygen. She never woke up.
Years later she still breathes on her own. Her eyes open. She has cycles of sleep and waking. But she never tracks a face, never responds, never shows a sign that anyone is home. Her parents say she would have hated living this way, and they ask the hospital to stop the feeding tube that keeps her alive.
Write or discuss:

Is Nancy alive? Is she still there?
Should her parents’ word be enough to stop the feeding tube?
Who, exactly, should get to decide — and on what basis?











Note



Nancy’s case hides two different questions that people constantly run together.

Is the person gone? This is a clinical question — about brains, breathing, and consciousness.
Who decides what happens next, and on what basis? This is a legal and ethical question — about consent, evidence, and family.

A patient can be clearly gone with no decision left to make (she is simply dead), or fully present but unable to speak for herself, or — like Nancy — somewhere in between, where both questions are live at once. Today we take them one at a time.






By the end of Part A, you will be able to:

Distinguish coma, persistent vegetative state, minimally conscious state, and locked-in syndrome by their clinical signs.
Explain the whole-brain standard of death, where it came from (Harvard 1968), and how the law codified it.
Apply the two surrogate standards — substituted judgment and best interest — to a patient who cannot speak.
Tell a living will, a durable power of attorney, and a POLST apart: what each is, who fills it out, and when it takes effect.
Explain why the Cruzan ruling drove the spread of advance directives.
Say what made the Quinlan, Cruzan, and Schiavo cases different from one another.





The everyday words for this topic are loaded. “Vegetable,” “life support,” and “pulling the plug” all smuggle in judgments before the argument starts.

We will use the clinical terms — vegetative state, mechanical ventilation, withdrawing treatment — not because they are colder, but because they are more precise.
Precision matters here because the words decide real outcomes. Whether a body counts as a living patient or a dead body changes who may touch it, who may stop, and who may grieve.

Watch your own reactions to the vocabulary today. They are often where the ethics is hiding.
crumbs: Part A — Death, Dying & Advance Planning

Her parents say she would have hated living this way, and they ask the hospital to stop the feeding tube that keeps her alive.
Write or discuss:

Note
