title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
You are a parent on the postpartum floor, twelve hours after a healthy delivery. A research nurse offers to read your newborn’s whole genome at no cost. The report, returned in two weeks, will list gene variants linked to several hundred conditions. Some are treatable in childhood. Some show up only in adulthood. Some are of uncertain meaning. Your partner is leaning yes. You want to ask more questions first.
Write or discuss:

Which of those three kinds of result would you want to receive, and which would you rather not know?
Does your answer change if the test is offered at the six-year visit instead?
Whose decision is this — yours, your partner’s, or the child’s own, twenty years from now?



Resist the urge to settle the question. The point is to surface the time-shape of genetic information: a decision the parents make today binds the child for life.










Note



The thought experiment turns on a distinction we will return to all lecture.

Actionable now — a finding the family can act on in childhood (a treatable metabolic condition).
Actionable later — a finding that matters in adulthood, when the child can decide for herself whether to know (BRCA1, late-onset Alzheimer’s risk).
Informative but not actionable — a finding that names a risk no one can act on yet (some Huntington’s variants in a healthy infant).

Today is about what genetic tests can read, what they cannot read, and which decisions get easier or harder once they have read it.






By the end of Part A, you will be able to:

Tell the basic story of the Human Genome Project — the 1990–2003 arc, the public-Celera race, and the gap between what was promised in 1999 and what actually arrived.
Define geneticization and use it to name a pattern that runs through prenatal testing, ancestry kits, and forensic genealogy.
Explain why a “reference genome” built mostly from one or two people is not a neutral starting point, and what an ancestry bias in genetic prediction looks like in practice.
State the NASEM 2023 headline rule on race and genetic variation, and apply it to a real research scenario.
Describe what GINA (2008) covers and what it does not, and name two state-level fixes for the gap.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
You are a parent on the postpartum floor, twelve hours after a healthy delivery. A research nurse offers to read your newborn’s whole genome at no cost. The report, returned in two weeks, will list gene variants linked to several hundred conditions. Some are treatable in childhood. Some show up only in adulthood. Some are of uncertain meaning. Your partner is leaning yes. You want to ask more questions first.
Write or discuss:

Which of those three kinds of result would you want to receive, and which would you rather not know?
Does your answer change if the test is offered at the six-year visit instead?
Whose decision is this — yours, your partner’s, or the child’s own, twenty years from now?



Resist the urge to settle the question. The point is to surface the time-shape of genetic information: a decision the parents make today binds the child for life.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
Note



The thought experiment turns on a distinction we will return to all lecture.

Actionable now — a finding the family can act on in childhood (a treatable metabolic condition).
Actionable later — a finding that matters in adulthood, when the child can decide for herself whether to know (BRCA1, late-onset Alzheimer’s risk).
Informative but not actionable — a finding that names a risk no one can act on yet (some Huntington’s variants in a healthy infant).

Today is about what genetic tests can read, what they cannot read, and which decisions get easier or harder once they have read it.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
By the end of Part A, you will be able to:

Tell the basic story of the Human Genome Project — the 1990–2003 arc, the public-Celera race, and the gap between what was promised in 1999 and what actually arrived.
Define geneticization and use it to name a pattern that runs through prenatal testing, ancestry kits, and forensic genealogy.
Explain why a “reference genome” built mostly from one or two people is not a neutral starting point, and what an ancestry bias in genetic prediction looks like in practice.
State the NASEM 2023 headline rule on race and genetic variation, and apply it to a real research scenario.
Describe what GINA (2008) covers and what it does not, and name two state-level fixes for the gap.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
2.1 A Thirteen-Year Arc







HGP


1990\nHGP launched\n(NIH + DOE)

1990
HGP launched
(NIH + DOE)



1995\nFirst bacterial\ngenome sequenced

1995
First bacterial
genome sequenced



1990\nHGP launched\n(NIH + DOE)->1995\nFirst bacterial\ngenome sequenced





1998\nCelera enters\nthe race

1998
Celera enters
the race



1995\nFirst bacterial\ngenome sequenced->1998\nCelera enters\nthe race





2000\n'Working draft'\nat White House

2000
'Working draft'
at White House



1998\nCelera enters\nthe race->2000\n'Working draft'\nat White House





2003\nCompletion\ndeclared

2003
Completion
declared



2000\n'Working draft'\nat White House->2003\nCompletion\ndeclared










The HGP was a public, international project led by the NIH and the Department of Energy. The UK’s Wellcome Trust Sanger Centre was the largest non-US partner. The project began in 1990 and was meant to finish by 2005. In 1998, a private company — Celera Genomics, run by Craig Venter — said it would finish first using a faster method. The race ended in a tie at a White House press conference in June 2000.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
2.2 The Public-Celera Race


The public effort (HGP)

Funded by NIH, DOE, and the Wellcome Trust.
Clone-by-clone method: cut the genome into mapped chunks, sequence each, reassemble.
Slower, but more careful with repeats.
Data released to public databases each night.




Celera Genomics

Privately funded; planned to sell access to the result.
Whole-genome shotgun method: break the genome into random fragments, sequence in bulk, reassemble by computer.
Faster and cheaper, but leaned on the public data to fill gaps.
Backed off its patent plan under public pressure.



Both finished. The shotgun method won the science fight; the public group won the policy fight.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
2.3 Collins 1999, Verbatim

“By the year 2010, it is expected that predictive genetic tests will be available for as many as a dozen common conditions, allowing individuals who wish to know this information to learn their individual susceptibilities and to take steps to reduce those risks for which interventions are or will be available. Such interventions could take the form of medical surveillance, lifestyle modifications, diet, or drug therapy.”
— Francis S. Collins, “Medical and Societal Consequences of the Human Genome Project,” NEJM 341 (1999): 28.

(Collins 1999)

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
2.4 Promised vs. Delivered







Collins predicted by 2010
What 2026 actually has




Routine cheek-swab risk prediction for common diseases
A small set of strong single-gene tests; broad risk prediction still uneven


Drugs dosed by genotype
A few dozen drug-gene pairs in use; far fewer than promised


Twelve common conditions with usable predictive tests
Several, but most predict only modest risk


Genetic privacy laws “fully in place”
GINA 2008 covers health insurance and employment only


Public catching up with the science
Ancestry-kit marketing still routinely overpromises



The gap is not a failure of the project. It is the lecture’s first lesson about how genetic claims travel.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
2.5 “Missing Heritability”

What GWAS expected and what it found
A genome-wide association study (GWAS) lines up the DNA of people who have a trait — height, diabetes, schizophrenia — against people who don’t, and looks for variants that show up more often in one group. By 2010, GWAS had been run on hundreds of thousands of people.
Twin and family studies had predicted that most variation in these traits was heritable, and that GWAS would find a few moderately strong variants. What it found instead were thousands of variants of tiny effect — which still leave most of the predicted heritability unexplained. That gap is the missing heritability problem. It is part of why “cheek-swab risk prediction” did not arrive on the 1999 schedule.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
3.1 Lippman Names the Tendency

“Geneticization refers to an ongoing process by which differences between individuals are reduced to their DNA codes, with most disorders, behaviours and physiological variations defined, at least in part, as genetic in origin.… It refers as well to the process by which interventions employing genetic technologies are adopted to manage problems of health.”
— Abby Lippman, “Prenatal Genetic Testing and Screening,” American Journal of Law & Medicine 17 (1991): 19.

(Lippman 1991; Gannett 2023)

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
3.2 Three Related Pulls


Geneticization — the tendency to cast human differences, risks, and identities as mostly genetic. A cultural and rhetorical pattern, not a scientific claim.
Genetic reductionism — the working assumption that the right level to explain a trait is the gene. Sometimes useful; often overshoots.



Genetic determinism — the stronger claim that genes fix outcomes — that DNA is destiny. Almost no working geneticist believes this in its strong form. It lives mostly in popular framings and marketing copy.
The three travel together. Catch one and you usually catch the others.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
3.3 Gene × Environment — the Working Picture







GxE


Genotype

Genotype



Phenotype\n(observed trait)

Phenotype
(observed trait)



Genotype->Phenotype\n(observed trait)





Environment\n(diet, exposure,\nstress, care)

Environment
(diet, exposure,
stress, care)



Genotype->Environment\n(diet, exposure,\nstress, care)


  shapes



Environment\n(diet, exposure,\nstress, care)->Phenotype\n(observed trait)










The textbook case is phenylketonuria (PKU). It causes severe intellectual disability — but only if the diet contains a substance called phenylalanine. Find the gene at birth, change the diet, and the disability never appears. Same DNA, different outcome.
Most diseases the HGP was meant to crack — diabetes, heart disease, depression — sit closer to the PKU end than the popular framing suggests. Genes matter. So does what you live next to, eat, and can afford.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
4.1 Four Words







Term
What it means




Gene
A stretch of DNA that codes for a working product (usually a protein). Humans have about 20,000.


Allele
One of the possible versions of a gene at a given spot. You get one from each parent.


Genome
The full set of DNA in an organism’s cells — about 3.2 billion base pairs in humans.


Variant
Any place where one person’s DNA differs from a reference sequence. Most variants do nothing visible. A few do a lot.



The older word mutation is still used, but variant has largely replaced it. “Mutation” carries an unhelpful hint that the difference is always bad.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
4.2 Germline vs. Somatic


Germline

Present in the egg or sperm — and so in every cell of the resulting person.
Heritable: passed to children.
A germline edit changes one person and every descendant.




Somatic

A change in one tissue, in one person, in non-reproductive cells.
Not heritable: dies with the patient.
Most cancers are somatic. So are most approved gene therapies (Casgevy for sickle-cell disease, for example).



This distinction does most of the moral work in Part B. Editing one patient’s bone marrow is not the same as editing every cell in every descendant.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
5.1 The Reference Is a Composite
When a clinical lab says a patient has “a variant,” it means the patient’s DNA differs in one spot from the human reference genome. The reference is a published sequence anyone can look up. Everyone else’s DNA gets compared against it.
The reference is a composite. It is stitched together from cell lines that came from several anonymous donors. It is not the genome of any one person.
That choice matters, because most of the reference is from very few of those donors. (Gannett 2023)

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
section: 5.2 The Buffalo Man and Craig Venter
text:
5.2 The Buffalo Man and Craig Venter






Important



Where the reference actually comes from
The public HGP reference is roughly 74% from one anonymous male donor. He was known internally as “RP11” because his cells became the most-used cell line at Roswell Park Cancer Institute in Buffalo, New York. He was paid $50, signed an anonymous consent form, and never knew he was the main donor.
The Celera reference is roughly 71% from Craig Venter himself. Venter quietly put his own DNA into the donor pool and confirmed it in public in 2002.
For about twenty years, the standard against which billions of human genomes have been compared was therefore mostly two men.


(Gannett 2023)

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
5.3 Why It Matters: Ancestry Bias
GWAS finds variants by comparing thousands of cases to thousands of controls. If those controls are mostly of one ancestry, the variants the study finds are the ones that matter in that group. The predictions built from them work best in that group too.


Where GWAS participants come from (cumulative, mid-2010s):

~80% European ancestry
~10% Asian ancestry
~3% African ancestry
~1% Hispanic / Latin American
Remainder: everyone else




What that means in the clinic:
A polygenic risk score for coronary disease trained on this data is about 4–5× more accurate in people of European ancestry than in people of African ancestry.
The score is not biased because the math is bad. It is biased because the training data was.


(Popejoy and Fullerton 2016)

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
5.4 Toward a Pangenome
The fix is not to “patch the reference.” It is to replace one reference with many. A pangenome reference is a set of high-quality genomes from people around the world, queried as a group rather than as one string.
The first draft human pangenome was published in 2023. Its 47 starting genomes are still only a fraction of human variation. The shift — from one reference to many — closes the bias gap at the root rather than patching it downstream.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
6.1 Three Kinds of Genetic Testing








Type
Who is tested
What it tells you




Diagnostic
A patient with symptoms
Whether their disease has a known genetic cause


Predictive
A healthy person at risk
Their chance of developing a disease later


Reproductive
A future parent, fetus, or embryo
Whether a future person is likely to be affected



The same lab test can be used for any of the three. What changes is who is asking, on whose behalf, and what they will do with the answer.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
6.2 NIPT — How It Works







NIPT


Maternal\nblood draw\n(~10 weeks)

Maternal
blood draw
(~10 weeks)



Cell-free DNA\n(mother + fetus)

Cell-free DNA
(mother + fetus)



Maternal\nblood draw\n(~10 weeks)->Cell-free DNA\n(mother + fetus)





Sequencing

Sequencing



Cell-free DNA\n(mother + fetus)->Sequencing





Count chromosome\nfragments

Count chromosome
fragments



Sequencing->Count chromosome\nfragments





Probability\nof trisomy 21,\n18, 13, etc.

Probability
of trisomy 21,
18, 13, etc.



Count chromosome\nfragments->Probability\nof trisomy 21,\n18, 13, etc.










Non-invasive prenatal testing (NIPT) reads bits of fetal DNA that float in the mother’s blood. It is available from about ten weeks of pregnancy. NIPT is screening, not diagnosis. A positive result raises the chance the fetus has Down syndrome but does not confirm it.
A confirming test — usually amniocentesis at 15–18 weeks — is needed before any permanent decision.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
section: 6.3 NIPT, Down Syndrome, and the >90% Question
text:
6.3 NIPT, Down Syndrome, and the >90% Question
NIPT has been offered to nearly all pregnant patients in the US since the mid-2010s, regardless of age. After a confirmed Down syndrome diagnosis, the US termination rate is above 60%. In Denmark and Iceland, where first-trimester screening is universal, the rate is close to 95%.

A question that returns later in the course
The disability scholar Adrienne Asch argued that aborting a fetus diagnosed with Down syndrome is not the same as the general right to abortion. The grounds matter, she said. “We don’t want a child like that” sends a message to people already living with the condition.
This lecture does not settle the argument. The disability-rights treatment belongs to a later lecture. The point here is that NIPT is the technology that made the question scale — from rare diagnosis to a routine offer.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
7.1 BRCA in Adults
The genes BRCA1 and BRCA2 are tumor-suppressor genes. Certain variants in either raise lifetime risk of breast and ovarian cancer by a lot. Among Ashkenazi Jewish women, three specific variants account for most of the inherited risk. Predictive testing in healthy adult women is, by now, routine.
What a positive result changes for an adult woman is concrete: more frequent screening, the option of preventive surgery in her thirties or forties, sometimes drug therapy. The decision is hers, with a genetic counselor.
The adult case is largely a settled use of informed consent. The child case is not.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
section: 7.2 Testing Healthy Children for Adult-Onset Conditions
text:
7.2 Testing Healthy Children for Adult-Onset Conditions


BRCA in a healthy eight-year-old

The risk is real, but does not become actionable until her twenties or thirties.
The result will follow her through every insurance form and school record for two decades before she can act on it.
The American Society of Human Genetics has long advised against testing children for adult-onset conditions with no childhood treatment.




Huntington’s in a healthy eight-year-old

A near-certain prediction of a fatal brain disease in midlife.
No treatment. No prevention.
The professional view is even firmer: do not test.
Yet some adult Huntington’s patients say they wish they had been told earlier. Others say the opposite.

The same rule, applied twice, gives the same answer for different reasons.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
7.3 Feinberg’s “Open Future,” Applied to Information

Feinberg’s right to an open future
The legal philosopher Joel Feinberg argued that children hold a class of “rights in trust.” These are autonomy rights they cannot yet use, but which adults must keep open on their behalf. Parental choices that close off a future the child might have chosen are, on this view, a distinct kind of wrong.
The first uses were about things like religion and schooling. Applied to genetic information, the rule is simple: don’t fix the meaning of an adult-onset risk before the future adult can decide whether to know it.
The same adult, twenty years later, can walk into a clinic and ask.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
8.1 BabySeq and GUARDIAN
Two long-running US studies are quietly normalizing the choice we opened with.

BabySeq (Boston, since 2015) randomly assigned newborns to whole-genome reading plus normal care, or normal care alone. It tracked what families and pediatricians did with the results.
GUARDIAN (New York, since 2022) is enrolling a much larger group. It returns about 150–250 conditions, all chosen for childhood treatability.

Neither study has reported major harm. Both report that families want the results — even for adult-onset conditions the researchers were nervous about returning.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
8.2 ACMG vs. AAP — Two Positions









American College of Medical Genetics
American Academy of Pediatrics




Default frame
Sequencing is cheap; expanding the panel of conditions returned is the responsible move
The longstanding rule against testing healthy children should not erode with technology


What to report
A regularly updated list of “medically actionable” findings, including some adult-onset
Conditions with a childhood treatment, by preference


Why
Information delayed is treatments missed
Information returned is futures narrowed


Direction of pressure
Expand the panel
Hold the line



Both groups are firmly mainstream. They disagree, in public, about what to do with the next four million newborns.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
section: 8.3 Thought Question — Would You Sign Your Newborn Up?
text:
8.3 Thought Question — Would You Sign Your Newborn Up?

You are the parent we opened with. The hospital is enrolling for a study that returns about 200 conditions: most treatable in childhood, about 30 only relevant in adulthood, a handful neither but informative. Your partner wants to enroll. You are unsure.
Write or discuss.

Which of the three kinds of result would you tell the study you want back, and which not?
The consent form says the data will be kept for thirty years and may be re-read as the science improves. Does that change your answer?
The nurse on the consent team says “most families take everything.” Is that helpful information — or a form of subtle pressure?

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
8.4 After You’ve Discussed






Note



A distinction worth carrying forward.
Actionability does not mean a single thing. It can mean:

Actionable now — there is a treatment we can start tomorrow (PKU diet, congenital hypothyroidism).
Actionable later — there is a screening or prevention plan that begins in adulthood (BRCA, Lynch syndrome).
Behaviorally actionable — no medical treatment, but the family changes plans (career, when to have kids).
Not actionable — the information is interesting but changes nothing concrete.

The ACMG and AAP do not disagree about the science. They disagree about which of these counts as “actionable enough” to override a child’s right to decide for herself.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
9.1 23andMe by the Numbers

Founded 2006; cleared by the FDA as a home health test in 2017.
About 14 million customers by 2023, most for ancestry rather than health.
A saliva tube; about $100; results in six weeks.
Customers can opt their DNA into research; about 80% historically have.
Filed for bankruptcy in 2025; the data itself is still a corporate asset under sale.

The product is two products in one box: a recreational ancestry report, and a clinical-grade test for a small set of disease variants.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
9.2 The 2023 Breach
In October 2023, attackers got into 23andMe accounts by reusing passwords leaked from other sites. Because many customers had opted into a feature called “DNA Relatives,” each broken account also exposed information about that user’s relatives — relatives who had never been hacked themselves.
About 6.9 million users were affected. Lists targeting Ashkenazi Jewish and Chinese-ancestry customers were posted on hacker forums. The company settled in late 2024 for about $30 million.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
section: 9.3 One Relative’s Test Is Everyone’s Test
text:
9.3 One Relative’s Test Is Everyone’s Test

The consent externality
If your second cousin uploads her saliva to a genealogy site, she has not just given consent for herself. With no notice to you, she has shared a lot about your DNA too.
You cannot really opt out of your relatives’ choices. The same feature of DNA that makes ancestry inference work makes one-person consent an incomplete protection.
The next section runs on the same fact. Shared DNA is what exposed millions in the 23andMe breach. It is also what makes forensic genetic genealogy work.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
10.1 Why This Comes Up Here
Race is not the topic of this lecture. The technology puts it on the table all the same, in two places.

Ancestry kits: a customer is told she is “23% Italian” with a precision the science does not support. The reference panels behind those percentages are social-political artifacts, not natural categories.
Genetic research: studies often report results by self-reported race, treating race as a stand-in for genetic variation. The 2023 National Academies report says that is a mistake.

The field has argued about race and genetics since the HGP itself. The latest professional consensus is short and specific.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
10.2 NASEM 2023 — The Headline Rule






Important



Recommendation 1. “Researchers should not use race as a proxy for human genetic variation. In particular, researchers should not assign genetic ancestry group labels to individuals or sets of individuals based on their race, whether self-identified or not.”
— National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine, Using Population Descriptors in Genetics and Genomics Research (2023), Summary, Rec. 1.


The reasoning, in one line: race is a social-political category. It shifts by country and by era. Human genetic variation is continuous, not discrete. Mapping the first onto the second produces errors that go in predictable directions. (National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine 2023)

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
10.3 Population Descriptors at a Glance








Descriptor
What it captures
When to use it




Race
A social-political category about perceived ancestry; shifts by country and era
Not as a stand-in for genetic variation; sometimes as a stand-in for social exposure (with a stated reason)


Ethnicity
A social-political category about shared cultural heritage
For cultural features, not genetic ones


Geography
Where a person’s recent ancestors lived
Useful to describe sampling; not a stand-in for ancestry


Genetic ancestry
The paths through a family tree by which DNA was passed down
When the study question is about ancestry as such


Genetic similarity
A number measuring how genetically alike two people are
The preferred tool for most population comparisons



Adapted from NASEM 2023, Table S-1.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
10.4 “Caucasian” — a Word with a History

Where the word came from
The term Caucasian was coined in 1795 by the German anatomist Johann Friedrich Blumenbach. He split humans into five “varieties” and declared the Caucasus region home to the most beautiful skull in his collection. He used the word to convey a racial hierarchy, with Europeans on top.
NASEM 2023 says the word should be retired from genetic research. It carries no scientific information. It never did. Its continued use in medical forms gives a sheen of legitimacy to a category that started as a judgment about a skull.

(National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine 2023)

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
section: 10.5 Thought Question — A Study Lands on Your Desk
text:
10.5 Thought Question — A Study Lands on Your Desk

You are a primary-care clinician reading the morning’s literature alerts. A new paper reports: “African American patients in our cohort have a 1.8× higher rate of variant X, a variant linked to poor response to drug Y.” You prescribe drug Y often.
Write or discuss.

Before you change anything, what would you want to know about how the authors defined “African American”?
Two of your patients fit the label as the authors used it: one whose grandparents emigrated from Nigeria in the 1990s, one whose family has been in rural Mississippi for six generations. Should the finding change their care in the same way?
If the answer to (2) is “no,” what should the study have been reporting instead?

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
10.6 After You’ve Discussed






Note



A clinician will, this week, read a study saying “African American patients have a higher rate of variant X.” Three readings sit underneath that sentence. They do not commit to the same things.

Race as ancestry-proxy: the variant is more common in people whose recent ancestors came from certain regions of Africa. Replace the label with the geographic ancestry and the claim becomes precise.
Race as social-exposure proxy: the variant looks more common because what activates it (lead, redlining, segregated air) tracks race in the US.
Race as the actual variable: a claim about a biological category called “African American” — which NASEM says doesn’t exist.

The clinician’s job is to ask which one is being claimed before acting on it.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
section: 11.1 GEDmatch and the Golden State Killer
text:
11.1 GEDmatch and the Golden State Killer






Important



Joseph James DeAngelo, 2018
Between 1973 and 1986, one offender committed at least 13 murders and dozens of rapes across California. The case went cold for thirty years. In 2018, a Sacramento team uploaded a DNA profile from a 1980 crime scene to GEDmatch, an open genealogy site used mostly by hobbyist family-tree researchers.
The profile matched several of DeAngelo’s distant cousins. None of them had any idea their saliva tubes could finger a relative for murder. Investigators built family trees from the matches, narrowed to DeAngelo, picked up DNA from his car door handle, and arrested him.
He pleaded guilty in 2020.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
11.2 How Long-Range Familial Search Works







IGG


Crime-scene\nDNA sample

Crime-scene
DNA sample



SNP profile\n(~500k markers)

SNP profile
(~500k markers)



Crime-scene\nDNA sample->SNP profile\n(~500k markers)





Upload to\nGEDmatch /\nFamilyTreeDNA

Upload to
GEDmatch /
FamilyTreeDNA



SNP profile\n(~500k markers)->Upload to\nGEDmatch /\nFamilyTreeDNA





Identify shared\nDNA segments

Identify shared
DNA segments



Upload to\nGEDmatch /\nFamilyTreeDNA->Identify shared\nDNA segments





Build family\ntrees from matches

Build family
trees from matches



Identify shared\nDNA segments->Build family\ntrees from matches





Narrow to one\ncandidate suspect

Narrow to one
candidate suspect



Build family\ntrees from matches->Narrow to one\ncandidate suspect





Confirm with\nrecovered DNA

Confirm with
recovered DNA



Narrow to one\ncandidate suspect->Confirm with\nrecovered DNA










The method works because of one fact about DNA. A third cousin shares about 0.8% of their DNA with you, in long, readable chunks. With a database of a few million users, some third or fourth cousin of any given American is almost certainly in it.
Your choice to stay out of the database does not, by itself, keep you out of searches it enables.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
11.3 The Law Catches Up







Year
Action




2018
DeAngelo arrest brings the method to public attention


2019
US Department of Justice issues an interim policy for federal use — only with prosecutor approval, only for violent crimes


2021
Maryland becomes the first US state to regulate forensic genealogy by statute — court approval required, limited crimes


2021
Montana passes a tighter law requiring a search warrant


2023–
Other state legislatures consider similar bills; most US states still have no law



The federal courts have not yet ruled on whether a Fourth Amendment “search” happens when investigators query a database your cousin uploaded.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
11.4 Thought Question — Whose Consent?

Your second cousin — someone you have met twice in your life — uploads her saliva to a genealogy site. Six years later, investigators use her upload to identify you as a suspect in a thirty-year-old assault case. They are right; you did it. You are arrested, charged, and convicted.
Write or discuss.

Did you have a privacy interest that was violated, given that your own DNA was never tested?
Does the answer change if you are innocent and were briefly a suspect before being cleared?
Does it change if the crime is a serial murder vs. a thirty-year-old simple assault?

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
12.1 GINA 2008 — The Federal Floor
The Genetic Information Nondiscrimination Act, signed in May 2008, was the first US federal law aimed at genetic discrimination. It does two things, and only two.

Health insurance: insurers may not use a person’s genetic information to set eligibility, premiums, or coverage. They may not request or require it.
Employment: employers with 15 or more workers may not use genetic information in hiring, firing, or promotion.

That is the federal floor. Everyone in the US is covered by it. The areas it does not reach are large.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
12.2 The Four-Cell Gap



Insurance line
Covered by GINA?




Health insurance
Yes — cannot use genetic information


Life insurance
No — may request it and price on it


Disability insurance
No


Long-term-care insurance
No










Employer category
Covered by GINA?




Civilian employer, ≥15 employees
Yes


Civilian employer, <15 employees
No


US military
No — service members may be reassigned or separated on genetic findings



Anyone who tells a patient “you are protected against genetic discrimination” is, strictly, telling them about half the truth.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
12.3 Florida 2020 and the State-Level Patchwork
In 2020, Florida became the first US state to extend protection to life, disability, and long-term-care insurance — partly closing the GINA gap inside its own borders. A few other states have passed narrower versions. Many more have tried and failed.
Federal law has not moved since 2008. For now, the action is in state legislatures and in the actuarial departments of life insurers. Both know that a whole-genome reading for under a thousand dollars is on the near horizon for every American applicant.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
13.1 Mail-Order DNA Synthesis
For about $0.10 per base pair, a researcher can order a custom stretch of DNA online and get it in the mail in days. The technology is what makes modern molecular biology workable. It also made it possible, in 2002, for a Stony Brook lab to build a working poliovirus from scratch, using only the published genome and mail-order reagents.
The dual-use risk is not hypothetical. The 1918 flu virus has been rebuilt in a high-containment lab. A 2016 Canadian team used a published horsepox genome to assemble a close relative of the virus, for under $100,000.
The question is not whether the technology should exist. It does. The question is who is checking what gets ordered.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
13.2 The 2023 White House Framework
In April 2023, the White House issued a framework asking providers of synthetic DNA to screen orders against lists of dangerous sequences, and to verify the legitimacy of customers placing large or unusual orders.


What the framework does

Sets a uniform federal screening standard.
Targets orders touching sequences on the federal Select Agent list.
Ties some federal funding to using compliant providers.




What it doesn’t do

Cover benchtop DNA synthesizers, which sit outside the screening regime.
Cover non-US providers, several of which sell into US labs.
Catch fragments small enough to be assembled into a dangerous sequence later.



The risk is real. The controls are partial. The regulatory pace has not matched the technological one.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
section: 14.1 ELSI — A Bioethics Program Built Into the Science
text:
14.1 ELSI — A Bioethics Program Built Into the Science

The 1988 promise
When James Watson was named to lead the new National Center for Human Genome Research, a reporter asked him about the social effects of sequencing the human genome. More or less off the cuff, Watson said a portion of the funding would be set aside to study those effects.
That promise became the Ethical, Legal, and Social Implications (ELSI) research program. The 1993 NIH Revitalization Act made it a legal requirement: at least 5% of the NIH budget for the genome project goes to ELSI research. That has meant tens of millions of dollars a year, every year, for over three decades.
ELSI is why genomics has more bioethics infrastructure than other parts of biomedicine. The model was later copied for the BRAIN Initiative, the All of Us program, and parts of federal AI research funding.

(McEwen et al. 2014)

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
15.1 Scoreboard — Geneticization Across the Lecture








Where it shows up
What gets read as genetic
What gets read out




NIPT + the >90% question
Fetal trisomy
What life with Down syndrome actually looks like


23andMe ancestry
“23% Italian” — discrete national identities
The continuous geographic gradients beneath the labels


Polygenic risk scores
Disease risk as a number
The training-data ancestry that set how accurate the number is


Race in research
Genetic variation as racial-group difference
The environment and history that track race in the US


Forensic genealogy
A suspect’s identity from distant-cousin matches
The non-suspect relatives whose DNA was searched without consent


Dual-use synthesis
A pathogen’s sequence as a copyable recipe
The conditions under which the recipe gets ordered



The same pull, six times. Name it once; spot it everywhere.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
15.2 Review Questions
Write a brief answer to each, or use them as discussion prompts.


Recall
Define geneticization in your own words. Then explain why polygenic risk scores work better for people of European ancestry than for everyone else.


Apply
A 34-year-old whose mother died of ovarian cancer brings a 23andMe report flagging two BRCA1 variants. What should her care team do before any discussion of preventive surgery? Which of those steps is the one a 23andMe report cannot replace?


Debate
Should police need a warrant before searching genealogy databases for a suspect’s relatives? Take a side, then name the strongest argument on the other side.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
15.3 Key Terms



Geneticization
Genetic determinism
Germline vs. somatic
Genome-wide association study (GWAS)
Polygenic risk score





NIPT
Open future (Feinberg)
Population descriptor (NASEM)
Investigative genetic genealogy
GINA

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
15.4 Further Reading

James Tabery, Tyranny of the Gene: Personalized Medicine and Its Threat to Public Health (Knopf, 2023).
Lisa Gannett, “The Human Genome Project,” Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy (2023 revision).
National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine, Using Population Descriptors in Genetics and Genomics Research (2023) — read the summary at minimum.
Alice Popejoy and Stephanie Fullerton, “Genomics is Failing on Diversity,” Nature 538 (2016).
Jean McEwen et al., “The ELSI Program: Reflections on an Ongoing Experiment,” Annual Review of Genomics and Human Genetics 15 (2014).

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
section: 15.5 What We’re Doing in Part B
text:
15.5 What We’re Doing in Part B
Part A asked what genetic tests can read, what they cannot, and which decisions get easier or harder once they have read.
Part B asks what happens when the technology starts to write — CRISPR-Cas9 in the clinic, the He Jiankui twins, the non-identity problem, and the live US market for polygenic embryo selection. Same spine — geneticization — applied to a sharper edge.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
15.6 References


Collins, Francis S. 1999. “Shattuck Lecture — Medical and Societal Consequences of the Human Genome Project.” New England Journal of Medicine 341 (1): 28–37. https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJM199907013410106.


Gannett, Lisa. 2023. The Human Genome Project. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy (Fall 2023 Edition), Edward N. Zalta and Uri Nodelman (eds.). https://plato.stanford.edu/archives/fall2023/entries/human-genome/.

Guyer. 2014. “The Ethical, Legal, and Social Implications Program of the National Human Genome Research Institute: Reflections on an Ongoing Experiment.” Annual Review of Genomics and Human Genetics 15: 481–505. https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-genom-090413-025327.
