title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
Would You Sign?

You are a parent on the postpartum floor, twelve hours after a healthy delivery. A research nurse offers to read your newborn’s whole genome at no cost. The report, returned in two weeks, will list gene variants linked to several hundred conditions. Some are treatable in childhood. Some show up only in adulthood. Some are of uncertain meaning. Your partner is leaning yes. You want to ask more questions first.
Write or discuss:

Which of those three kinds of result would you want to receive, and which would you rather not know?
Does your answer change if the test is offered at the six-year visit instead?
Whose decision is this — yours, your partner’s, or the child’s own, twenty years from now?



Resist the urge to settle the question. The point is to surface the time-shape of genetic information: a decision the parents make today binds the child for life.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


The thought experiment turns on a distinction we will return to all lecture.

Actionable now — a finding the family can act on in childhood (a treatable metabolic condition).
Actionable later — a finding that matters in adulthood, when the child can decide for herself whether to know (BRCA1, late-onset Alzheimer’s risk).
Informative but not actionable — a finding that names a risk no one can act on yet (some Huntington’s variants in a healthy infant).

Today is about what genetic tests can read, what they cannot read, and which decisions get easier or harder once they have read it.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
Today

By the end of Part A, you will be able to:

Tell the basic story of the Human Genome Project — the 1990–2003 arc, the public-Celera race, and the gap between what was promised in 1999 and what actually arrived.
Define geneticization and use it to name a pattern that runs through prenatal testing, ancestry kits, and forensic genealogy.
Explain why a “reference genome” built mostly from one or two people is not a neutral starting point, and what an ancestry bias in genetic prediction looks like in practice.
State the NASEM 2023 headline rule on race and genetic variation, and apply it to a real research scenario.
Describe what GINA (2008) covers and what it does not, and name two state-level fixes for the gap.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
A Thirteen-Year Arc







HGP


1990\nHGP launched\n(NIH + DOE)

1990
HGP launched
(NIH + DOE)



1995\nFirst bacterial\ngenome sequenced

1995
First bacterial
genome sequenced



1990\nHGP launched\n(NIH + DOE)->1995\nFirst bacterial\ngenome sequenced





1998\nCelera enters\nthe race

1998
Celera enters
the race



1995\nFirst bacterial\ngenome sequenced->1998\nCelera enters\nthe race





2000\n'Working draft'\nat White House

2000
'Working draft'
at White House



1998\nCelera enters\nthe race->2000\n'Working draft'\nat White House





2003\nCompletion\ndeclared

2003
Completion
declared



2000\n'Working draft'\nat White House->2003\nCompletion\ndeclared










The HGP was a public, international project led by the NIH and the Department of Energy. The UK’s Wellcome Trust Sanger Centre was the largest non-US partner. The project began in 1990 and was meant to finish by 2005. In 1998, a private company — Celera Genomics, run by Craig Venter — said it would finish first using a faster method. The race ended in a tie at a White House press conference in June 2000.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
The Public-Celera Race


The public effort (HGP)

Funded by NIH, DOE, and the Wellcome Trust.
Clone-by-clone method: cut the genome into mapped chunks, sequence each, reassemble.
Slower, but more careful with repeats.
Data released to public databases each night.




Celera Genomics

Privately funded; planned to sell access to the result.
Whole-genome shotgun method: break the genome into random fragments, sequence in bulk, reassemble by computer.
Faster and cheaper, but leaned on the public data to fill gaps.
Backed off its patent plan under public pressure.


Both finished. The shotgun method won the science fight; the public group won the policy fight.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
Collins 1999, Verbatim

“By the year 2010, it is expected that predictive genetic tests will be available for as many as a dozen common conditions, allowing individuals who wish to know this information to learn their individual susceptibilities and to take steps to reduce those risks for which interventions are or will be available. Such interventions could take the form of medical surveillance, lifestyle modifications, diet, or drug therapy.”
— Francis S. Collins, “Medical and Societal Consequences of the Human Genome Project,” NEJM 341 (1999): 28.

(Collins 1999)

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
Promised vs. Delivered







Collins predicted by 2010
What 2026 actually has




Routine cheek-swab risk prediction for common diseases
A small set of strong single-gene tests; broad risk prediction still uneven


Drugs dosed by genotype
A few dozen drug-gene pairs in use; far fewer than promised


Twelve common conditions with usable predictive tests
Several, but most predict only modest risk


Genetic privacy laws “fully in place”
GINA 2008 covers health insurance and employment only


Public catching up with the science
Ancestry-kit marketing still routinely overpromises



The gap is not a failure of the project. It is the lecture’s first lesson about how genetic claims travel.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
“Missing Heritability”

What GWAS expected and what it found
A genome-wide association study (GWAS) lines up the DNA of people who have a trait — height, diabetes, schizophrenia — against people who don’t, and looks for variants that show up more often in one group. By 2010, GWAS had been run on hundreds of thousands of people.
Twin and family studies had predicted that most variation in these traits was heritable, and that GWAS would find a few moderately strong variants. What it found instead were thousands of variants of tiny effect — which still leave most of the predicted heritability unexplained. That gap is the missing heritability problem. It is part of why “cheek-swab risk prediction” did not arrive on the 1999 schedule.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
Lippman Names the Tendency

“Geneticization refers to an ongoing process by which differences between individuals are reduced to their DNA codes, with most disorders, behaviours and physiological variations defined, at least in part, as genetic in origin.… It refers as well to the process by which interventions employing genetic technologies are adopted to manage problems of health.”
— Abby Lippman, “Prenatal Genetic Testing and Screening,” American Journal of Law & Medicine 17 (1991): 19.

(Lippman 1991; Gannett 2023)

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
Three Related Pulls


Geneticization — the tendency to cast human differences, risks, and identities as mostly genetic. A cultural and rhetorical pattern, not a scientific claim.
Genetic reductionism — the working assumption that the right level to explain a trait is the gene. Sometimes useful; often overshoots.



Genetic determinism — the stronger claim that genes fix outcomes — that DNA is destiny. Almost no working geneticist believes this in its strong form. It lives mostly in popular framings and marketing copy.
The three travel together. Catch one and you usually catch the others.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
Gene × Environment — the Working Picture







GxE


Genotype

Genotype



Phenotype\n(observed trait)

Phenotype
(observed trait)



Genotype->Phenotype\n(observed trait)





Environment\n(diet, exposure,\nstress, care)

Environment
(diet, exposure,
stress, care)



Genotype->Environment\n(diet, exposure,\nstress, care)


  shapes



Environment\n(diet, exposure,\nstress, care)->Phenotype\n(observed trait)










The textbook case is phenylketonuria (PKU). It causes severe intellectual disability — but only if the diet contains a substance called phenylalanine. Find the gene at birth, change the diet, and the disability never appears. Same DNA, different outcome.
Most diseases the HGP was meant to crack — diabetes, heart disease, depression — sit closer to the PKU end than the popular framing suggests. Genes matter. So does what you live next to, eat, and can afford.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
Four Words







Term
What it means




Gene
A stretch of DNA that codes for a working product (usually a protein). Humans have about 20,000.


Allele
One of the possible versions of a gene at a given spot. You get one from each parent.


Genome
The full set of DNA in an organism’s cells — about 3.2 billion base pairs in humans.


Variant
Any place where one person’s DNA differs from a reference sequence. Most variants do nothing visible. A few do a lot.



The older word mutation is still used, but variant has largely replaced it. “Mutation” carries an unhelpful hint that the difference is always bad.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
Germline vs. Somatic


Germline

Present in the egg or sperm — and so in every cell of the resulting person.
Heritable: passed to children.
A germline edit changes one person and every descendant.




Somatic

A change in one tissue, in one person, in non-reproductive cells.
Not heritable: dies with the patient.
Most cancers are somatic. So are most approved gene therapies (Casgevy for sickle-cell disease, for example).


This distinction does most of the moral work in Part B. Editing one patient’s bone marrow is not the same as editing every cell in every descendant.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
The Reference Is a Composite
When a clinical lab says a patient has “a variant,” it means the patient’s DNA differs in one spot from the human reference genome. The reference is a published sequence anyone can look up. Everyone else’s DNA gets compared against it.
The reference is a composite. It is stitched together from cell lines that came from several anonymous donors. It is not the genome of any one person.
That choice matters, because most of the reference is from very few of those donors. (Gannett 2023)

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
section: The Buffalo Man and Craig Venter
text:
The Buffalo Man and Craig Venter






Important


Where the reference actually comes from
The public HGP reference is roughly 74% from one anonymous male donor. He was known internally as “RP11” because his cells became the most-used cell line at Roswell Park Cancer Institute in Buffalo, New York. He was paid $50, signed an anonymous consent form, and never knew he was the main donor.
The Celera reference is roughly 71% from Craig Venter himself. Venter quietly put his own DNA into the donor pool and confirmed it in public in 2002.
For about twenty years, the standard against which billions of human genomes have been compared was therefore mostly two men.



(Gannett 2023)

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
Why It Matters: Ancestry Bias
GWAS finds variants by comparing thousands of cases to thousands of controls. If those controls are mostly of one ancestry, the variants the study finds are the ones that matter in that group. The predictions built from them work best in that group too.


Where GWAS participants come from (cumulative, mid-2010s):

~80% European ancestry
~10% Asian ancestry
~3% African ancestry
~1% Hispanic / Latin American
Remainder: everyone else




What that means in the clinic:
A polygenic risk score for coronary disease trained on this data is about 4–5× more accurate in people of European ancestry than in people of African ancestry.
The score is not biased because the math is bad. It is biased because the training data was.

(Popejoy and Fullerton 2016)

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
Toward a Pangenome
The fix is not to “patch the reference.” It is to replace one reference with many. A pangenome reference is a set of high-quality genomes from people around the world, queried as a group rather than as one string.
The first draft human pangenome was published in 2023. Its 47 starting genomes are still only a fraction of human variation. The shift — from one reference to many — closes the bias gap at the root rather than patching it downstream.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
Three Kinds of Genetic Testing








Type
Who is tested
What it tells you




Diagnostic
A patient with symptoms
Whether their disease has a known genetic cause


Predictive
A healthy person at risk
Their chance of developing a disease later


Reproductive
A future parent, fetus, or embryo
Whether a future person is likely to be affected



The same lab test can be used for any of the three. What changes is who is asking, on whose behalf, and what they will do with the answer.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
NIPT — How It Works







NIPT


Maternal\nblood draw\n(~10 weeks)

Maternal
blood draw
(~10 weeks)



Cell-free DNA\n(mother + fetus)

Cell-free DNA
(mother + fetus)



Maternal\nblood draw\n(~10 weeks)->Cell-free DNA\n(mother + fetus)





Sequencing

Sequencing



Cell-free DNA\n(mother + fetus)->Sequencing





Count chromosome\nfragments

Count chromosome
fragments



Sequencing->Count chromosome\nfragments





Probability\nof trisomy 21,\n18, 13, etc.

Probability
of trisomy 21,
18, 13, etc.



Count chromosome\nfragments->Probability\nof trisomy 21,\n18, 13, etc.










Non-invasive prenatal testing (NIPT) reads bits of fetal DNA that float in the mother’s blood. It is available from about ten weeks of pregnancy. NIPT is screening, not diagnosis. A positive result raises the chance the fetus has Down syndrome but does not confirm it.
A confirming test — usually amniocentesis at 15–18 weeks — is needed before any permanent decision.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
section: NIPT, Down Syndrome, and the >90% Question
text:
NIPT, Down Syndrome, and the >90% Question
NIPT has been offered to nearly all pregnant patients in the US since the mid-2010s, regardless of age. After a confirmed Down syndrome diagnosis, the US termination rate is above 60%. In Denmark and Iceland, where first-trimester screening is universal, the rate is close to 95%.

A question that returns later in the course
The disability scholar Adrienne Asch argued that aborting a fetus diagnosed with Down syndrome is not the same as the general right to abortion. The grounds matter, she said. “We don’t want a child like that” sends a message to people already living with the condition.
This lecture does not settle the argument. The disability-rights treatment belongs to a later lecture. The point here is that NIPT is the technology that made the question scale — from rare diagnosis to a routine offer.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
BRCA in Adults
The genes BRCA1 and BRCA2 are tumor-suppressor genes. Certain variants in either raise lifetime risk of breast and ovarian cancer by a lot. Among Ashkenazi Jewish women, three specific variants account for most of the inherited risk. Predictive testing in healthy adult women is, by now, routine.
What a positive result changes for an adult woman is concrete: more frequent screening, the option of preventive surgery in her thirties or forties, sometimes drug therapy. The decision is hers, with a genetic counselor.
The adult case is largely a settled use of informed consent. The child case is not.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
section: Testing Healthy Children for Adult-Onset Conditions
text:
Testing Healthy Children for Adult-Onset Conditions


BRCA in a healthy eight-year-old

The risk is real, but does not become actionable until her twenties or thirties.
The result will follow her through every insurance form and school record for two decades before she can act on it.
The American Society of Human Genetics has long advised against testing children for adult-onset conditions with no childhood treatment.




Huntington’s in a healthy eight-year-old

A near-certain prediction of a fatal brain disease in midlife.
No treatment. No prevention.
The professional view is even firmer: do not test.
Yet some adult Huntington’s patients say they wish they had been told earlier. Others say the opposite.

The same rule, applied twice, gives the same answer for different reasons.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
Feinberg’s “Open Future,” Applied to Information

Feinberg’s right to an open future
The legal philosopher Joel Feinberg argued that children hold a class of “rights in trust.” These are autonomy rights they cannot yet use, but which adults must keep open on their behalf. Parental choices that close off a future the child might have chosen are, on this view, a distinct kind of wrong.
The first uses were about things like religion and schooling. Applied to genetic information, the rule is simple: don’t fix the meaning of an adult-onset risk before the future adult can decide whether to know it.
The same adult, twenty years later, can walk into a clinic and ask.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
BabySeq and GUARDIAN
Two long-running US studies are quietly normalizing the choice we opened with.

BabySeq (Boston, since 2015) randomly assigned newborns to whole-genome reading plus normal care, or normal care alone. It tracked what families and pediatricians did with the results.
GUARDIAN (New York, since 2022) is enrolling a much larger group. It returns about 150–250 conditions, all chosen for childhood treatability.

Neither study has reported major harm. Both report that families want the results — even for adult-onset conditions the researchers were nervous about returning.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
ACMG vs. AAP — Two Positions









American College of Medical Genetics
American Academy of Pediatrics




Default frame
Sequencing is cheap; expanding the panel of conditions returned is the responsible move
The longstanding rule against testing healthy children should not erode with technology


What to report
A regularly updated list of “medically actionable” findings, including some adult-onset
Conditions with a childhood treatment, by preference


Why
Information delayed is treatments missed
Information returned is futures narrowed


Direction of pressure
Expand the panel
Hold the line



Both groups are firmly mainstream. They disagree, in public, about what to do with the next four million newborns.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
section: Thought Question — Would You Sign Your Newborn Up?
text:
Thought Question — Would You Sign Your Newborn Up?

You are the parent we opened with. The hospital is enrolling for a study that returns about 200 conditions: most treatable in childhood, about 30 only relevant in adulthood, a handful neither but informative. Your partner wants to enroll. You are unsure.
Write or discuss.

Which of the three kinds of result would you tell the study you want back, and which not?
The consent form says the data will be kept for thirty years and may be re-read as the science improves. Does that change your answer?
The nurse on the consent team says “most families take everything.” Is that helpful information — or a form of subtle pressure?

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


A distinction worth carrying forward.
Actionability does not mean a single thing. It can mean:

Actionable now — there is a treatment we can start tomorrow (PKU diet, congenital hypothyroidism).
Actionable later — there is a screening or prevention plan that begins in adulthood (BRCA, Lynch syndrome).
Behaviorally actionable — no medical treatment, but the family changes plans (career, when to have kids).
Not actionable — the information is interesting but changes nothing concrete.

The ACMG and AAP do not disagree about the science. They disagree about which of these counts as “actionable enough” to override a child’s right to decide for herself.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
23andMe by the Numbers

Founded 2006; cleared by the FDA as a home health test in 2017.
About 14 million customers by 2023, most for ancestry rather than health.
A saliva tube; about $100; results in six weeks.
Customers can opt their DNA into research; about 80% historically have.
Filed for bankruptcy in 2025; the data itself is still a corporate asset under sale.

The product is two products in one box: a recreational ancestry report, and a clinical-grade test for a small set of disease variants.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
The 2023 Breach
In October 2023, attackers got into 23andMe accounts by reusing passwords leaked from other sites. Because many customers had opted into a feature called “DNA Relatives,” each broken account also exposed information about that user’s relatives — relatives who had never been hacked themselves.
About 6.9 million users were affected. Lists targeting Ashkenazi Jewish and Chinese-ancestry customers were posted on hacker forums. The company settled in late 2024 for about $30 million.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
section: One Relative’s Test Is Everyone’s Test
text:
One Relative’s Test Is Everyone’s Test

The consent externality
If your second cousin uploads her saliva to a genealogy site, she has not just given consent for herself. With no notice to you, she has shared a lot about your DNA too.
You cannot really opt out of your relatives’ choices. The same feature of DNA that makes ancestry inference work makes one-person consent an incomplete protection.
The next section runs on the same fact. Shared DNA is what exposed millions in the 23andMe breach. It is also what makes forensic genetic genealogy work.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
Why This Comes Up Here
Race is not the topic of this lecture. The technology puts it on the table all the same, in two places.

Ancestry kits: a customer is told she is “23% Italian” with a precision the science does not support. The reference panels behind those percentages are social-political artifacts, not natural categories.
Genetic research: studies often report results by self-reported race, treating race as a stand-in for genetic variation. The 2023 National Academies report says that is a mistake.

The field has argued about race and genetics since the HGP itself. The latest professional consensus is short and specific.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
NASEM 2023 — The Headline Rule






Important


Recommendation 1. “Researchers should not use race as a proxy for human genetic variation. In particular, researchers should not assign genetic ancestry group labels to individuals or sets of individuals based on their race, whether self-identified or not.”
— National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine, Using Population Descriptors in Genetics and Genomics Research (2023), Summary, Rec. 1.



The reasoning, in one line: race is a social-political category. It shifts by country and by era. Human genetic variation is continuous, not discrete. Mapping the first onto the second produces errors that go in predictable directions. (National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine 2023)

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
Population Descriptors at a Glance








Descriptor
What it captures
When to use it




Race
A social-political category about perceived ancestry; shifts by country and era
Not as a stand-in for genetic variation; sometimes as a stand-in for social exposure (with a stated reason)


Ethnicity
A social-political category about shared cultural heritage
For cultural features, not genetic ones


Geography
Where a person’s recent ancestors lived
Useful to describe sampling; not a stand-in for ancestry


Genetic ancestry
The paths through a family tree by which DNA was passed down
When the study question is about ancestry as such


Genetic similarity
A number measuring how genetically alike two people are
The preferred tool for most population comparisons



Adapted from NASEM 2023, Table S-1.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
“Caucasian” — a Word with a History

Where the word came from
The term Caucasian was coined in 1795 by the German anatomist Johann Friedrich Blumenbach. He split humans into five “varieties” and declared the Caucasus region home to the most beautiful skull in his collection. He used the word to convey a racial hierarchy, with Europeans on top.
NASEM 2023 says the word should be retired from genetic research. It carries no scientific information. It never did. Its continued use in medical forms gives a sheen of legitimacy to a category that started as a judgment about a skull.

(National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine 2023)

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
section: Thought Question — A Study Lands on Your Desk
text:
Thought Question — A Study Lands on Your Desk

You are a primary-care clinician reading the morning’s literature alerts. A new paper reports: “African American patients in our cohort have a 1.8× higher rate of variant X, a variant linked to poor response to drug Y.” You prescribe drug Y often.
Write or discuss.

Before you change anything, what would you want to know about how the authors defined “African American”?
Two of your patients fit the label as the authors used it: one whose grandparents emigrated from Nigeria in the 1990s, one whose family has been in rural Mississippi for six generations. Should the finding change their care in the same way?
If the answer to (2) is “no,” what should the study have been reporting instead?

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


A clinician will, this week, read a study saying “African American patients have a higher rate of variant X.” Three readings sit underneath that sentence. They do not commit to the same things.

Race as ancestry-proxy: the variant is more common in people whose recent ancestors came from certain regions of Africa. Replace the label with the geographic ancestry and the claim becomes precise.
Race as social-exposure proxy: the variant looks more common because what activates it (lead, redlining, segregated air) tracks race in the US.
Race as the actual variable: a claim about a biological category called “African American” — which NASEM says doesn’t exist.

The clinician’s job is to ask which one is being claimed before acting on it.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
section: GEDmatch and the Golden State Killer
text:
GEDmatch and the Golden State Killer






Important


Joseph James DeAngelo, 2018
Between 1973 and 1986, one offender committed at least 13 murders and dozens of rapes across California. The case went cold for thirty years. In 2018, a Sacramento team uploaded a DNA profile from a 1980 crime scene to GEDmatch, an open genealogy site used mostly by hobbyist family-tree researchers.
The profile matched several of DeAngelo’s distant cousins. None of them had any idea their saliva tubes could finger a relative for murder. Investigators built family trees from the matches, narrowed to DeAngelo, picked up DNA from his car door handle, and arrested him.
He pleaded guilty in 2020.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
How Long-Range Familial Search Works







IGG


Crime-scene\nDNA sample

Crime-scene
DNA sample



SNP profile\n(~500k markers)

SNP profile
(~500k markers)



Crime-scene\nDNA sample->SNP profile\n(~500k markers)





Upload to\nGEDmatch /\nFamilyTreeDNA

Upload to
GEDmatch /
FamilyTreeDNA



SNP profile\n(~500k markers)->Upload to\nGEDmatch /\nFamilyTreeDNA





Identify shared\nDNA segments

Identify shared
DNA segments



Upload to\nGEDmatch /\nFamilyTreeDNA->Identify shared\nDNA segments





Build family\ntrees from matches

Build family
trees from matches



Identify shared\nDNA segments->Build family\ntrees from matches





Narrow to one\ncandidate suspect

Narrow to one
candidate suspect



Build family\ntrees from matches->Narrow to one\ncandidate suspect





Confirm with\nrecovered DNA

Confirm with
recovered DNA



Narrow to one\ncandidate suspect->Confirm with\nrecovered DNA










The method works because of one fact about DNA. A third cousin shares about 0.8% of their DNA with you, in long, readable chunks. With a database of a few million users, some third or fourth cousin of any given American is almost certainly in it.
Your choice to stay out of the database does not, by itself, keep you out of searches it enables.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
The Law Catches Up







Year
Action




2018
DeAngelo arrest brings the method to public attention


2019
US Department of Justice issues an interim policy for federal use — only with prosecutor approval, only for violent crimes


2021
Maryland becomes the first US state to regulate forensic genealogy by statute — court approval required, limited crimes


2021
Montana passes a tighter law requiring a search warrant


2023–
Other state legislatures consider similar bills; most US states still have no law



The federal courts have not yet ruled on whether a Fourth Amendment “search” happens when investigators query a database your cousin uploaded.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
Thought Question — Whose Consent?

Your second cousin — someone you have met twice in your life — uploads her saliva to a genealogy site. Six years later, investigators use her upload to identify you as a suspect in a thirty-year-old assault case. They are right; you did it. You are arrested, charged, and convicted.
Write or discuss.

Did you have a privacy interest that was violated, given that your own DNA was never tested?
Does the answer change if you are innocent and were briefly a suspect before being cleared?
Does it change if the crime is a serial murder vs. a thirty-year-old simple assault?

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
GINA 2008 — The Federal Floor
The Genetic Information Nondiscrimination Act, signed in May 2008, was the first US federal law aimed at genetic discrimination. It does two things, and only two.

Health insurance: insurers may not use a person’s genetic information to set eligibility, premiums, or coverage. They may not request or require it.
Employment: employers with 15 or more workers may not use genetic information in hiring, firing, or promotion.

That is the federal floor. Everyone in the US is covered by it. The areas it does not reach are large.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
The Four-Cell Gap



Insurance line
Covered by GINA?




Health insurance
Yes — cannot use genetic information


Life insurance
No — may request it and price on it


Disability insurance
No


Long-term-care insurance
No










Employer category
Covered by GINA?




Civilian employer, ≥15 employees
Yes


Civilian employer, <15 employees
No


US military
No — service members may be reassigned or separated on genetic findings



Anyone who tells a patient “you are protected against genetic discrimination” is, strictly, telling them about half the truth.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
Florida 2020 and the State-Level Patchwork
In 2020, Florida became the first US state to extend protection to life, disability, and long-term-care insurance — partly closing the GINA gap inside its own borders. A few other states have passed narrower versions. Many more have tried and failed.
Federal law has not moved since 2008. For now, the action is in state legislatures and in the actuarial departments of life insurers. Both know that a whole-genome reading for under a thousand dollars is on the near horizon for every American applicant.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
Mail-Order DNA Synthesis
For about $0.10 per base pair, a researcher can order a custom stretch of DNA online and get it in the mail in days. The technology is what makes modern molecular biology workable. It also made it possible, in 2002, for a Stony Brook lab to build a working poliovirus from scratch, using only the published genome and mail-order reagents.
The dual-use risk is not hypothetical. The 1918 flu virus has been rebuilt in a high-containment lab. A 2016 Canadian team used a published horsepox genome to assemble a close relative of the virus, for under $100,000.
The question is not whether the technology should exist. It does. The question is who is checking what gets ordered.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
The 2023 White House Framework
In April 2023, the White House issued a framework asking providers of synthetic DNA to screen orders against lists of dangerous sequences, and to verify the legitimacy of customers placing large or unusual orders.


What the framework does

Sets a uniform federal screening standard.
Targets orders touching sequences on the federal Select Agent list.
Ties some federal funding to using compliant providers.




What it doesn’t do

Cover benchtop DNA synthesizers, which sit outside the screening regime.
Cover non-US providers, several of which sell into US labs.
Catch fragments small enough to be assembled into a dangerous sequence later.


The risk is real. The controls are partial. The regulatory pace has not matched the technological one.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
section: ELSI — A Bioethics Program Built Into the Science
text:
ELSI — A Bioethics Program Built Into the Science

The 1988 promise
When James Watson was named to lead the new National Center for Human Genome Research, a reporter asked him about the social effects of sequencing the human genome. More or less off the cuff, Watson said a portion of the funding would be set aside to study those effects.
That promise became the Ethical, Legal, and Social Implications (ELSI) research program. The 1993 NIH Revitalization Act made it a legal requirement: at least 5% of the NIH budget for the genome project goes to ELSI research. That has meant tens of millions of dollars a year, every year, for over three decades.
ELSI is why genomics has more bioethics infrastructure than other parts of biomedicine. The model was later copied for the BRAIN Initiative, the All of Us program, and parts of federal AI research funding.

(McEwen et al. 2014)

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
Scoreboard — Geneticization Across the Lecture








Where it shows up
What gets read as genetic
What gets read out




NIPT + the >90% question
Fetal trisomy
What life with Down syndrome actually looks like


23andMe ancestry
“23% Italian” — discrete national identities
The continuous geographic gradients beneath the labels


Polygenic risk scores
Disease risk as a number
The training-data ancestry that set how accurate the number is


Race in research
Genetic variation as racial-group difference
The environment and history that track race in the US


Forensic genealogy
A suspect’s identity from distant-cousin matches
The non-suspect relatives whose DNA was searched without consent


Dual-use synthesis
A pathogen’s sequence as a copyable recipe
The conditions under which the recipe gets ordered



The same pull, six times. Name it once; spot it everywhere.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
Review Questions
Write a brief answer to each, or use them as discussion prompts.


Recall
Define geneticization in your own words. Then explain why polygenic risk scores work better for people of European ancestry than for everyone else.


Apply
A 34-year-old whose mother died of ovarian cancer brings a 23andMe report flagging two BRCA1 variants. What should her care team do before any discussion of preventive surgery? Which of those steps is the one a 23andMe report cannot replace?


Debate
Should police need a warrant before searching genealogy databases for a suspect’s relatives? Take a side, then name the strongest argument on the other side.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
Key Terms



Geneticization
Genetic determinism
Germline vs. somatic
Genome-wide association study (GWAS)
Polygenic risk score





NIPT
Open future (Feinberg)
Population descriptor (NASEM)
Investigative genetic genealogy
GINA

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
Further Reading

James Tabery, Tyranny of the Gene: Personalized Medicine and Its Threat to Public Health (Knopf, 2023).
Lisa Gannett, “The Human Genome Project,” Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy (2023 revision).
National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine, Using Population Descriptors in Genetics and Genomics Research (2023) — read the summary at minimum.
Alice Popejoy and Stephanie Fullerton, “Genomics is Failing on Diversity,” Nature 538 (2016).
Jean McEwen et al., “The ELSI Program: Reflections on an Ongoing Experiment,” Annual Review of Genomics and Human Genetics 15 (2014).

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
section: What We’re Doing in Part B
text:
What We’re Doing in Part B
Part A asked what genetic tests can read, what they cannot, and which decisions get easier or harder once they have read.
Part B asks what happens when the technology starts to write — CRISPR-Cas9 in the clinic, the He Jiankui twins, the non-identity problem, and the live US market for polygenic embryo selection. Same spine — geneticization — applied to a sharper edge.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
References


Collins, Francis S. 1999. “Shattuck Lecture — Medical and Societal Consequences of the Human Genome Project.” New England Journal of Medicine 341 (1): 28–37. https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJM199907013410106.


Gannett, Lisa. 2023. The Human Genome Project. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy (Fall 2023 Edition), Edward N. Zalta and Uri Nodelman (eds.). https://plato.stanford.edu/archives/fall2023/entries/human-genome/.


Lippman, Abby. 1991. “Prenatal Genetic Testing and Screening: Constructing Needs and Reinforcing Inequities.” American Journal of Law & Medicine 17 (1–2): 15–50.


McEwen, Jean E., Joy T. Boyer, Kathie Y. Sun, Karen R. Rothenberg, Nicole C. Lockhart, and Mark S. Guyer. 2014. “The Ethical, Legal, and Social Implications Program of the National Human Genome Research Institute: Reflections on an Ongoing Experiment.” Annual Review of Genomics and Human Genetics 15: 481–505. https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-genom-090413-025327.


National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine. 2023. Using Population Descriptors in Genetics and Genomics Research: A New Framework for an Evolving Field. The National Academies Press. https://doi.org/10.17226/26902.


Popejoy, Alice B., and Stephanie M. Fullerton. 2016. “Genomics Is Failing on Diversity.” Nature 538 (7624): 161–64. https://doi.org/10.1038/538161a.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
A 22-week-pregnant woman is admitted to the ICU with severe bacterial pneumonia. She is awake and oriented. The team recommends placing her on a ventilator and starting IV antibiotics; without the ventilator, she will likely die within seventy-two hours. She refuses, citing fetal effects of the sedation, and says she will take her chances.
Write or discuss:

Is her refusal morally identical to the refusal of a non-pregnant patient in the same clinical state?
Does the answer change if she becomes briefly delirious from oxygen deprivation overnight?
If the team disagrees with her decision, what — short of going to court — are the team’s legitimate options?



Don’t formalize. Let students react. We’ll come back to the legal contours of cases like this when we discuss In re A.C. and Burton v. State.










Note




The standard idea of autonomy was built for one patient. Pregnancy is the first case where some say there is more than one — and even that claim is disputed.
The instinct to override a refusing pregnant patient has a long, bad history. It often hurts both the patient and the future baby the override was meant to protect.
Today’s job is to learn a framework sharp enough to tell three kinds of refusal apart: a refusal we should accept, a refusal we should keep trying to talk through, and a refusal the law may override.







By the end of Part A, you will be able to:

Distinguish liberal and relational autonomy as two complementary models of what it means to “make one’s own decision,” and say which model fits which kind of case.
State Mill’s harm principle, identify its limits, and apply a standard three-question test for justified paternalism to a contemporary drug-policy case.
Explain why some drug bans have backfired while some forms of soft paternalism (taxes, age limits, harm-reduction services) have worked.
Describe what Ferguson v. City of Charleston (2001) and In re A.C. (1990) held, and what each refused to decide.
Explain how mandatory reporting, child-protective services, and the federal Plan of Safe Care apply when a newborn has been exposed to substances in utero.
Explain why pregnant women were historically excluded from clinical trials, and why the 2018 PRGLAC report reframed that exclusion as itself a harm.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics

A 22-week-pregnant woman is admitted to the ICU with severe bacterial pneumonia. She is awake and oriented. The team recommends placing her on a ventilator and starting IV antibiotics; without the ventilator, she will likely die within seventy-two hours. She refuses, citing fetal effects of the sedation, and says she will take her chances.
Write or discuss:

Is her refusal morally identical to the refusal of a non-pregnant patient in the same clinical state?
Does the answer change if she becomes briefly delirious from oxygen deprivation overnight?
If the team disagrees with her decision, what — short of going to court — are the team’s legitimate options?



Don’t formalize. Let students react. We’ll come back to the legal contours of cases like this when we discuss In re A.C. and Burton v. State.

Note

The instinct to override a refusing pregnant patient has a long, bad history. It often hurts both the patient and the future baby the override was meant to protect.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
2.1 “Making Your Own Decision” Is Vague
Almost everyone in bioethics says they respect autonomy. The word covers more ground than people notice.

A cancer patient declining chemo to spend her remaining months at home.
A teenager being pressured by family into an unwanted abortion.
A homeless patient asked to consent to a research trial while waiting for a bed.
A pregnant woman refusing a recommended cesarean because she fears surgery.

All four involve “the person deciding for herself.” None of them are the same kind of case. Two models of autonomy — liberal and relational — sharpen what we are protecting in each.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
2.2 Liberal Autonomy
The standard model rests on a few claims that are easy to state and hard to shake.

The patient is the author of her own care.
Valid informed consent has four parts: disclosure, understanding, voluntariness, and capacity.
A competent adult may refuse the treatment her doctors recommend, for any reason or no reason.
When a patient refuses, the clinician’s job is to persuade, not to coerce.

This model has done huge work since Nuremberg. It is the right lens when the worry is interference — somebody pushing against the patient’s own choice.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
2.3 Relational Autonomy
Relational autonomy is the view, most linked to Susan Sherwin, that real autonomy is shaped through our relationships, social position, and material life — not in spite of them. (Sherwin 1992)
The view is not anti-autonomy. It is a fuller picture of what autonomy is.

A choice made under crushing pressure is still a “choice” in the liberal sense. But calling it free, without further comment, misses something.
The clinician’s job runs past the consent form. It also means tending to the conditions under which the patient is choosing.

Relational autonomy is the right lens when the worry is constraint — when background conditions narrow what choices are even on the table.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
2.4 Two Models, Two Jobs









Liberal autonomy
Relational autonomy




The self
Bounded, prior to relationships
Constituted through relationships


The threat it watches for
Interference by an identifiable agent
Constraint from background conditions


What it tells the clinician
Document consent and step back
Attend to the conditions of choice


Where it shines
Refusal of unwanted treatment; the cancer patient choosing hospice
Choices shaped by poverty, racism, coercive partners, addiction


Failure mode if used alone
Treats coerced consent as real consent
Slides toward paternalism by second-guessing choices



The competent clinician carries both and switches between them.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
section: 2.5 Why Pregnancy Strains Either Model Alone
text:
2.5 Why Pregnancy Strains Either Model Alone
Pregnancy is the paradigm case where each lens shows something the other misses.


What the liberal model is good at:

Refusal of a cesarean by a competent adult is a refusal — not a special case.
The body in front of you is one patient’s body, and her body is not a public resource.
The professional consensus (A.C., ACOG 664) is built on this lens.




What the relational model is good at:

A 19-year-old in active addiction, with no childcare and an abusive partner, “consenting” to an opioid-free birth plan is not, by itself, evidence the plan is freely chosen.
Most US pregnancies happen to women whose economic and social circumstances do real work shaping what a “free” choice looks like.
Harm reduction (next section) is the relational lens applied to policy.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
3.1 Paternalism, Defined
Paternalism is acting on someone’s behalf, against their stated wishes, for what you take to be their own good. The metaphor is exact: a parent overriding a child for the child’s sake.
Almost no one thinks all paternalism is wrong. We accept it toward small children, toward people in psychotic crisis, toward an unconscious patient in the ED. The hard question is where it stops.
The framework below is not just about pregnancy. It is what we need before the pregnancy cases can be judged fairly.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
3.2 Mill’s Harm Principle

“The only purpose for which power can be rightfully exercised over any member of a civilised community, against his will, is to prevent harm to others. His own good, either physical or moral, is not a sufficient warrant.… Over himself, over his own body and mind, the individual is sovereign.”
— John Stuart Mill, On Liberty (1859), ch. 1

(Mill 1863)

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
section: 3.3 Mill’s Limits — In His Own Words
text:
3.3 Mill’s Limits — In His Own Words
The harm principle is not the all-purpose shield it is sometimes treated as. In the same chapter, Mill carved out exceptions.


Mill himself excluded:

Children — “those who are still in a state to require being taken care of by others.”
People who cannot benefit from “free and equal discussion.”
“Backward states of society” — a 19th-century blind spot the modern reader should mark and reject.




What this means for us:
Liberal autonomy is a position with edges, not a default. The case for non-interference rests on working assumptions: that the person is competent, choosing freely, and well-informed. Those are exactly the assumptions that fail in the cases relational autonomy is built to catch.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
3.4 Soft vs. Hard Paternalism









Soft paternalism
Hard paternalism




What it overrides
Choices that are not fully voluntary — made under coercion, ignorance, or incapacity
Choices that are fully voluntary, competent, and informed


Example
Detaining a delirious post-op patient who tries to leave the hospital pulling out her IV
Banning the sale of cigarettes to a competent adult who has read every warning label


Mill’s view
Permitted — not a real violation of autonomy, because the person isn’t really choosing
Not permitted — overriding sovereignty


Where it gets hard
Determining when a choice is “not voluntary enough”
Almost every legitimate public-health intervention sits in this zone

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
section: 3.5 The Standard Test for Justified Paternalism
text:
3.5 The Standard Test for Justified Paternalism
Three questions, taken together, set a strong default against stepping in.

Is the person competent — able to understand and weigh the facts?
Is the choice voluntary — free from pressure, manipulation, or false information?
Does the harm fall on the chooser alone, or also on others?

If the answers are yes, yes, and chooser alone, the liberal answer is: leave the person be. Most real cases turn on honest disagreement about each question.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
4.1 Prohibition, 1920–1933


The Eighteenth Amendment (1919) banned making, selling, and shipping alcohol in the US. The Volstead Act gave it teeth. It was repealed in 1933 — the only constitutional amendment ever fully overturned.
In between, drinking fell briefly and then climbed back. Illegal liquor became a major industry. Federal prison populations roughly tripled. The murder rate hit a peak it would not match for sixty years.



Three lessons that recur in modern drug policy:

Banning a widely-used substance does not erase the demand for it.
The black market that replaces a legal market is more violent.
Selective enforcement falls hardest on the communities with the least power to push back.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
section: 4.2 What Has Worked: The Soft-Paternalism Ledger
text:
4.2 What Has Worked: The Soft-Paternalism Ledger
Bans are not the only tool. Some of the biggest public-health wins of the past sixty years used softer tools — and worked.

Tobacco taxes. US adult smoking fell from ~42% in 1965 to ~11% in 2022. About half of that drop is due to price (taxes). The rest comes from ad limits, age limits, and indoor-smoking bans. None of it required a ban on tobacco itself.
The drinking age. When the national age was raised to 21 in 1984, alcohol-related traffic deaths among 18–20 year olds fell by about a third over the next decade.
Seat belts and motorcycle helmets. These are rules for how you drive, not bans on driving. They save tens of thousands of lives a year.
Naloxone (Narcan) distribution. No ban — just a widely shared antidote. It is the single step with the clearest effect on the modern opioid death rate.

The pattern: changing the price, availability, age of access, or downstream harm of a behavior can shift outcomes without ever charging the user.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
section: 4.3 The War on Drugs at Fifty
text:
4.3 The War on Drugs at Fifty








Indicator
1971 (Nixon declares “war on drugs”)
2023




Federal drug-control budget
~$100 million
~$40 billion


U.S. incarcerated population
~200,000
~1.9 million


Share of state prisoners held for drug offenses
<10%
~14%


Black–White disparity in drug-arrest rates
not systematically tracked
~2.5× higher arrest rate for Black Americans, despite near-identical use rates


U.S. overdose deaths (annual)
~7,000
~107,000



The numbers do not settle the policy question on their own. They do force one question: after fifty years and forty billion dollars a year, what does the policy claim to have done?

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
4.4 Oregon Measure 110 — The Decriminalization Experiment


2020. Oregon voters passed Measure 110. It made possession of all drugs — including heroin, meth, and fentanyl — a $100 ticket rather than a crime. Cannabis-tax money was sent to treatment.
2021–23. Few people went into treatment. The ticket system was almost never enforced. Fentanyl arrived in volume in the same years. Overdose deaths rose sharply. Street drug use became more visible. Public support for the law fell apart.



2024. The Oregon legislature passed HB 4002. It repealed Measure 110 and made possession a misdemeanor again — but with a treatment-track built in.
The honest reading. Decriminalizing drugs without paying for real treatment is not “the relational autonomy answer.” It is a half-built bridge. Portugal — the original model — paired decriminalization with big investments in treatment and outreach.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
4.5 Marijuana Legalization — A Live Ledger


What looks fine:

No clear spike in teen use in most legal states.
Race gaps in marijuana arrests have fallen sharply.
Tax revenue is large, and some of it pays for treatment and schools.




What is real and uncomfortable:

Daily and near-daily adult use has risen. In some surveys it now beats daily drinking.
ER visits for cannabis-induced vomiting and for cannabis-induced psychosis have climbed in legal states.
Today’s cannabis flower averages ~20% THC, vs. ~4% in the 1990s. Concentrates run far higher.



Wide legalization may still be the right call. It is not the cost-free reform some of its early backers suggested.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
section: 4.6 Harm Reduction as a Third Frame
text:
4.6 Harm Reduction as a Third Frame
Harm reduction accepts that some people will keep using drugs. It tries to cut the medical and social damage — without making sobriety a condition for help.


Concrete examples:

Naloxone (Narcan) distribution to opioid users and those around them.
Syringe-services programs reducing HIV and hepatitis C transmission.
Methadone and buprenorphine for opioid use disorder during pregnancy.
Supervised consumption sites (legal in Canada; piloted in NYC since 2021).




The philosophical signature:
Harm reduction does not need to settle whether drug use is morally wrong. It asks a different question: what step, in this person, this week, cuts harm the most?
That is soft paternalism in public-health clothes — and relational autonomy turned into policy.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
section: 4.7 Thought Question — Should the State Ban Tobacco?
text:
4.7 Thought Question — Should the State Ban Tobacco?

Tobacco kills about 480,000 Americans a year — more than all illegal drugs combined. It is sold legally to anyone over twenty-one. The FDA has the power to cut nicotine to non-addictive levels. It has proposed doing so, but has not done it.
Write or discuss.

Apply the three-question test (competent? voluntary? harm to chooser alone?) to a thirty-year-old smoker.
What does secondhand smoke do to the third question, and what does that commit you to about other secondhand harms?
If you would support a steep federal tobacco tax but not a ban, what is doing the work in your view — and would that same reasoning apply to alcohol? To methamphetamine?

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
4.8 Make It Contemporary


Three live policies that turn on these questions:

The FDA’s proposed menthol cigarette ban, delayed in 2024 over fears of unequal enforcement.
State and federal limits on flavored e-cigarettes, aimed at teen uptake.
Australia’s prescription-only nicotine rule for vaping, in force since 2024.




What each is doing under the hood:

Menthol is about secondhand harms — uptake patterns in communities with fewer choices.
Vape limits are about competence — the choice is made by teens whose ability to choose freely is in dispute.
Australia is soft paternalism dressed up as a clinical pathway. You can have it. You just have to go through a doctor.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
4.9 After You’ve Discussed






Note



A distinction worth carrying into the pregnancy block.
Deterrence-of-care names a failure mode. An enforcement policy can drive the people it claims to protect away from the very system that was meant to help them.
When tobacco rules target the user, smokers do not stop smoking. They hide it from their doctor. When drug enforcement reaches into pregnancy, women do not stop using. They skip prenatal visits. The next forty minutes is about how that pattern plays out when the patient is pregnant.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
5.1 The Question, Stated Sharply

May the state criminalize, in a pregnant woman, conduct that — were she not pregnant — would be unprosecutable?

That is the question. Every case in this section turns on it. Notice that it takes no position on the moral status of the fetus. It asks instead about the enforcement practice and what it does to the people it touches.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
section: 5.2 Ferguson v. City of Charleston (2001) — Facts
text:
5.2 Ferguson v. City of Charleston (2001) — Facts


In the late 1980s, the Medical University of South Carolina in Charleston started a secret urine drug-testing program on some pregnant patients in its prenatal clinic. Positive results were sent to police. Hospital staff wrote the rules for who got tested; the local prosecutor signed off. Triggers included things like “no prenatal care.”
Ten of the eleven women arrested under the program were Black. The eleventh was a white woman whose chart noted that she “lives with her boyfriend, who is a Negro.”



The legal question:
Were these searches Fourth Amendment searches that needed patient consent? Or were they “special-needs” searches a hospital may do without consent, because the main goal is public health rather than law enforcement?
The Supreme Court decided in 2001.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
5.3 Ferguson — Holding






Important



Held, 6–3 (Stevens, J.): When the main goal of the searches was to gather evidence for police, the special-needs exception did not apply. The secret drug-testing program violated the Fourth Amendment.
What the Court did not decide: Whether drug testing tied to treatment referral — with no police — would be allowed. Whether the prosecutions themselves were lawful (a separate state-law question). Whether civil steps short of arrest (custody removal, civil commitment) raised the same concern.


(Supreme Court of the United States 2001)

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
5.4 State Criminalization at a Glance









State / Statute
What triggers it
Notable feature
Status




Wisconsin Act 292 (1997)
Probable cause of severe substance use in pregnancy; refusal of treatment
Civil detention; no appointed counsel for 48 hours; fetus gets a guardian ad litem immediately
In force


Alabama chemical-endangerment statute (2006)
Newborn positive for any controlled substance
Written for meth-lab operators; reapplied to pregnant women by prosecutorial choice; affirmed in Ex parte Ankrom (2013)
In force; several hundred convictions


Tennessee Chapter 820 (2014–16)
Newborn born “addicted to or harmed by” a narcotic
First explicit “fetal assault” statute; allowed to sunset after two years
Repealed


Oklahoma — Poolaw prosecution (2021)
Pregnancy loss with any drug metabolite present
First-degree manslaughter charge brought under existing homicide law
Conviction affirmed 2023



Five states use the criminal law directly. About twenty more reach the same outcome through child-welfare law.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
section: 5.5 Tennessee’s Fetal Assault Law — Rise and Fall
text:
5.5 Tennessee’s Fetal Assault Law — Rise and Fall


2014. Tennessee enacts Public Chapter 820, making it a misdemeanor for a woman to take a narcotic during pregnancy “if her child is born addicted to or harmed by the narcotic.”
2014–16. About one hundred women are arrested. Most cases are in poor, rural counties. Drug-use rates in pregnancy do not fall. Women report avoiding prenatal care. Some give birth at home, alone, to avoid the hospital’s required drug test.



2016. The law is allowed to sunset. The legislature does not renew it.
What the data showed. Faherty et al. (2019) looked at state-level data across the country. States with punitive policies on substance use in pregnancy had higher rates of newborn drug withdrawal — the very outcome the laws were sold as preventing.
The deterrence-of-care idea from earlier in the lecture has a body count.



Neonatal abstinence syndrome (NAS) — also called newborn opioid withdrawal — is the set of symptoms a baby exposed to opioids in the womb shows after birth: tremors, feeding problems, fussiness, sometimes seizures. It is treatable, and most babies do well with the right care. It is also the diagnosis punitive reporting laws are sold as cutting down.

(Faherty et al. 2019)

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
5.6 The Scale


Paltrow and Flavin counted 1,396 arrests and forced interventions on pregnant women in the US between 1973 and 2005. Their 2013 study in JHPPL is the landmark count. Pregnancy Justice has kept tracking. The pace has sped up since Dobbs.
Of the cases they documented:

71% of the women were low-income.
59% were women of color.
The most common charge was drug-related. The second was refusing care.

Dorothy Roberts’s Killing the Black Body (1997) is the classic account of why these patterns track race and class.



Read this number carefully.
1,396 is not the number of pregnant women who used drugs. It is the number who were charged, locked up, or forced to undergo a medical procedure by the state.


(Paltrow and Flavin 2013; Roberts 1997)

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
5.7 Brittney Poolaw, Oklahoma, 2021

In October 2021, a Comanche County jury found Brittney Poolaw, twenty-one, guilty of first-degree manslaughter for a pregnancy loss at about seventeen weeks. The medical examiner could not find a cause. Trace amounts of methamphetamine were found in fetal tissue. Even the state’s own toxicologist testified the levels were not clearly the cause. About one in five pregnancies at this stage is lost on its own. Poolaw was sentenced to four years.
— Pregnancy Justice case summary; State v. Poolaw, Comanche Co. CF-2020-114

The conviction was affirmed on appeal in 2023.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
section: 5.8 ACOG’s Position — And Its Empirical Backing
text:
5.8 ACOG’s Position — And Its Empirical Backing


ACOG Committee Opinion 473 / 664:

Substance use in pregnancy is “a medical condition that should be managed with the patient.”
Criminal charges and forced detention are “not effective in reducing the incidence of alcohol or drug abuse” and are “contrary to the welfare of the mother and the fetus.”
Drug testing without consent is rejected. Reporting to child-protective services is “controversial” and depends on state law.




Faherty et al. (2019), JAMA Network Open:
A state-level study of 4.5 million births. States with punitive policies (criminal charges, civil commitment, or reporting as abuse) had a 27% higher rate of newborn withdrawal than states without.
ACOG’s ethical claim and the population data point the same way. That kind of agreement is rare enough to matter.


(American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists 2011, 2016; Faherty et al. 2019)

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
section: 5.9 Mandatory Reporting, CPS, and Plan of Safe Care
text:
5.9 Mandatory Reporting, CPS, and Plan of Safe Care
The fight about criminal charges can hide a more common question that comes up in actual delivery rooms. When a baby has been exposed to drugs in the womb — legal or illegal — what is supposed to happen next?

CAPTA and the Plan of Safe Care. The federal Child Abuse Prevention and Treatment Act (CAPTA) was updated in 2016 and 2018. It tells states to set up a system in which every baby found to be substance-exposed at birth is referred to child-protective services. The family must also get a written Plan of Safe Care that addresses the health and recovery needs of the baby, the mother, and the household.
CAPTA says clearly that a CAPTA referral is not a finding of child abuse. The goal is to connect families to services, not to take the child away. In practice, states do this very unevenly.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
section: 5.10 What That Looks Like, Across States
text:
5.10 What That Looks Like, Across States









State approach
What gets reported
What gets called abuse/neglect
Typical outcome




Service-led (e.g., Vermont, Washington)
All substance-exposed infants → home-visiting and treatment services
Only if other risk factors present
Most infants stay with parent


Investigative (most states)
Positive newborn toxicology → CPS investigation
State-by-state; some treat exposure alone as neglect
Wide variation; removal rates track race


Punitive (Alabama, parts of TN/WI)
Positive newborn toxicology → CPS and law enforcement
Exposure can itself ground prosecution
Removal is common; criminal charges possible



The clinician’s job is the same across all three columns: identify, document, and connect the family with services. The legal system that takes over from there is not in the clinician’s hands. But the clinician should be honest with patients about what to expect.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
section: 5.11 Thought Question — A Patient on Methamphetamine
text:
5.11 Thought Question — A Patient on Methamphetamine

A 32-week-pregnant patient comes to a community hospital in early labor. Her urine tests positive for methamphetamine. She has had three prenatal visits, no other problems, and tells the nurse she used “yesterday, maybe the day before.” State law requires the hospital to report drug exposure to CPS. The hospital can choose whether to involve police.
Write or discuss.

The patient asks what happens next. What does the care team owe her in honest information? Is anything fair to hold back until after delivery?
The attending asks whether to call CPS now or after delivery. What is the right answer, and why?
Does your answer change if the drug is alcohol? Fentanyl? Cannabis that is legal in this state? Be honest about why.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
5.12 Make It Contemporary


Three live trends, 2022–2025:

Pregnancy Justice reports a sharp jump in arrests for pregnancy outcomes since Dobbs — from about fifty a year nationally to more than two hundred in 2023.
Since 2022, at least nine states have introduced fetal-personhood bills that would widen the legal basis for charges like Poolaw’s.
The Alabama Supreme Court’s LePage ruling (2024) called frozen embryos “extrauterine children.” We will look at it in detail in Part B. It is the most aggressive recent move in this direction.




What unifies these:
Each treats the fetus or embryo as a legal subject with claims that limit what the pregnant person (or the patient seeking IVF) may do.
The medical position has not changed. The legal world it works in has changed sharply.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
5.13 After You’ve Discussed






Note



A distinction the body of the lecture has been circling.
When a pregnant patient and her team disagree, there are three kinds of disagreement. Each calls for a different response.

About the facts. (“That medicine will harm my fetus.” It won’t, or it will less than the disease itself.) The remedy is better information.
About the values. (“Even if the medicine is safer, I want to take my chances.”) The remedy is respect for refusal.
About whose interests count. (“Your refusal will harm your fetus, who cannot speak.”) This is where the lawsuits live. And here ACOG’s modern position lands: fetal interests exist, but forcing the patient is almost never the right response to them.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
section: 6.1 When the Doctor Says Yes and the Patient Says No
text:
6.1 When the Doctor Says Yes and the Patient Says No
Three kinds of refusal cases recur. They look similar but are different in ethics and law.

Refusal of cesarean. The clinician thinks a surgical delivery is needed. The patient prefers a vaginal delivery, or no delivery at all.
Refusal of blood transfusion. Most often a Jehovah’s Witness with heavy bleeding around delivery. The standard model honors the refusal. Pregnancy has long been used as a reason to override it.
Refusal of bed-rest or hospital stay for a high-risk pregnancy. The burdens fall almost all on the patient. The benefits are statistical and indirect.

The starting case for all three is In re A.C. (1990).

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
6.2 In re A.C. (1990) — Angela Carder


Angela Carder was twenty-seven, twenty-six weeks pregnant, and dying of widespread cancer when she was admitted to George Washington University Hospital in 1987. Her cancer doctor had treated her since she was a teenager. The agreed plan was comfort care.
Hospital leaders asked a court for an order to perform a cesarean against her will, on the grounds that the fetus might survive. The hearing was held in the hospital hallway. The order was granted. The cesarean was performed. The infant lived two hours. Carder died two days later. Her death certificate listed the cesarean as a contributing cause.



The appeals court’s job was not to say whether the trial judge had been merciful or cruel.
It was to decide whether the test the judge had used would stand. That test balanced the patient’s right to refuse against the state’s interest in potential life.
The DC Court of Appeals, sitting en banc, gave its answer about two and a half years later.


(District of Columbia Court of Appeals 1990)

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
6.3 The 1990 Appellate Repudiation






Important



Held (DC Court of Appeals, en banc, April 1990): “In virtually all cases the question of what is to be done is to be decided by the patient — the pregnant woman — on behalf of herself and the fetus. The trial court erred in subordinating A.C.’s right of bodily integrity in favor of the state’s interest in potential life.”
The court did not decide what should happen in a different case — say, a patient who was not competent and a fetus that was clearly viable. But it set the bar for that case very high. In the thirty-five years since, no American appeals court has upheld a similar order.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
section: 6.4 ACOG Committee Opinion 664 — The Modern Consensus
text:
6.4 ACOG Committee Opinion 664 — The Modern Consensus



“Pregnancy is not an exception to the principle that a decisionally capable patient has the right to refuse treatment, even treatment needed to maintain life. … Coercive and punitive legal approaches to pregnant women who refuse medical advice fail to recognize that all competent adults are entitled to informed consent and bodily integrity.”

— ACOG, Committee Opinion 664 (2016, reaffirmed 2022) (American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists 2016)



Why it works as a primary source.
It is short. Most students can read it in fifteen minutes. It is the cleanest single statement of where the medical profession lands today. The A.C. court’s holding maps onto it almost line for line.
Burton v. State (Florida, 2010) reached the same result for forced hospital stays. It threw out an order that had forced a woman with ruptured membranes to stay in the hospital against her will. (District Court of Appeal of Florida, First District 2010)

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
6.5 Thought Question — A Refused Cesarean

A patient near her due date has had one prior cesarean. Her obstetrician now recommends a repeat cesarean. He gives two reasons: the baby is bigger than average, and the fetal heart monitor has looked worrying for the last forty minutes. The patient — competent, alert, and afraid of surgery — refuses. She asks to keep laboring.
The obstetrician turns to the nurse and says: “Help her understand.”
Write or discuss.

What does the care team owe the patient in the next ten minutes? What would not be okay to do, even with the obstetrician’s push?
When would it be right to call a hospital ethics committee? When would it be right to go to court?
Apply both models of autonomy to the case. Does either lens push you toward a different answer?

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
7.1 Marlise Muñoz, Fort Worth, 2013–14


On November 26, 2013, Marlise Muñoz collapsed in her kitchen from a blood clot in her lungs. She was 33 and fourteen weeks pregnant. Paramedics restarted her heart. By the next morning, she met the criteria for whole-brain death. Her entire brain, including the brainstem, had stopped working.
Her husband Erick — a paramedic — and her parents asked the hospital to stop life support. They said this matched her stated wishes. The hospital refused, pointing to the Texas Advance Directives Act. The family sued. In January 2014, a trial court ordered the hospital to disconnect support. By then the fetus had been growing for ten more weeks. Imaging showed severe abnormalities.
The case has been re-litigated, in one form or another, in twelve states since.



Texas Health and Safety Code § 166.049:

“A person may not withdraw or withhold life-sustaining treatment under this subchapter from a pregnant patient.”

No exception for brain-dead patients. No exception for non-viable pregnancies. No way for an advance directive to override it. About a dozen US states have similar wording as of 2025.
The Adriana Smith case (Atlanta, 2025) is the latest version. A brain-dead pregnant woman was kept on body support for four months under a disputed reading of Georgia’s LIFE Act.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
7.2 Bridge


The case study for this week is on Adriana Smith and Georgia’s LIFE Act. It picks up where this slide leaves off — what the family fought for, how the argument was built, what changed after, and what did not.
Read it before our next meeting.



Later in the course, when we turn to brain death and advance directives directly, these cases come back. The questions of pregnancy and the questions of end-of-life care overlap right here — at the body the law has decided is partly its own and partly something else’s.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
8.1 The Other Side of “Vulnerability”
The same word that for fifty years protected pregnant women from risky trials has, for fifty years, kept them out of the evidence base for the drugs they take.
Earlier in the course, “vulnerability” was a reason to be careful. It has a second face. Keeping a group out can itself be a harm, when it leaves them without good data on their own care. The next three slides walk through that second face.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
8.2 Why Pregnant Women Were Excluded








Period
What happened
What it triggered




1957–62
Thalidomide. Prescribed for morning sickness in Europe and (off-label) in the US; produced ~10,000 cases of severe limb malformation in babies
International revision of drug-approval standards; FDA Kefauver-Harris Amendment (1962)


1940s–70s
Diethylstilbestrol (DES). Prescribed to prevent miscarriage; later linked to a rare vaginal cancer in the daughters of women who took it
Reinforced perception that drugs in pregnancy were uniquely dangerous


1977
FDA guidance excluding women of reproductive age from most early-phase trials, regardless of whether they were pregnant
A near-total absence of pregnancy-specific safety data for the next forty years


1993
FDA reversal — women of reproductive age permitted in trials, but pregnant women remained largely excluded
The current gap




Teratogenic means a drug that causes birth defects when taken during pregnancy. Thalidomide is the textbook example. Fear of another thalidomide drove the exclusion policy — and made sure almost no drug developed later had pregnancy-specific data either.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
8.3 PRGLAC (2018) and the FDA’s Reframing






Important



PRGLAC Task Force, 2018 Report to Congress.
About 95% of prescription drugs in common use during pregnancy lack good pregnancy-specific safety and dosing data. The task force concluded that “protection by exclusion” had failed both pregnant women (who take the drugs anyway) and the doctors who prescribe them anyway.
The FDA’s 2018 draft guidance, issued at the same time, made including pregnant participants the default. Excluding them now needs a reason.


(Task Force on Research Specific to Pregnant Women and Lactating Women 2018)

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
section: 8.4 What the Evidence Gap Looks Like in Practice
text:
8.4 What the Evidence Gap Looks Like in Practice








Drug class
Pregnancy use
Pregnancy-specific safety/dosing data




SSRIs (sertraline, fluoxetine) — antidepressants
~6% of US pregnancies
Observational only; no RCTs; debate ongoing about neonatal effects


Antihypertensives — blood pressure medications
~9% of US pregnancies
Some have decades of use without trial data; ACE inhibitors known to cause birth defects


Anti-seizure medications
~0.5% of US pregnancies
Most pregnancy data come from voluntary registries, not trials


Anticoagulants — blood thinners
Common in high-risk pregnancies
Dosing largely extrapolated from non-pregnant adults


COVID-19 vaccines and antivirals
Recommended in pregnancy
Pregnant women excluded from pivotal trials; post-marketing data only



The same justice principle that warns against wrongful exploitation — putting the burdens on pregnant women while others get the benefits — also warns against wrongful exclusion: keeping them out of the evidence base their own care depends on.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
9.1 The Scoreboard











Liberal lens
Relational lens
Modern professional (ACOG)
Empirical record




Substance use
Presumption against coercion if competent and voluntary
Attend to conditions — addiction, poverty, partner violence
Public-health framing; criminalization rejected
Punitive policies → worse outcomes (Faherty 2019)


Refusal of care
Refusal is absolute for competent adults
Same conclusion, with attention to whose voice is silenced
A.C. / Burton / Opinion 664: refusal stands
Forced interventions track race and class


Brain death
The decedent’s prior wishes govern
The dead cannot consent to gestational labor
No professional support for indefinite somatic support against family wishes
Statutes still in place in ~12 states


Research exclusion
Voluntary participation should be available
Exclusion shaped by background paternalism
Inclusion as default (PRGLAC, FDA 2018)
95% of drugs in pregnancy lack adequate data

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
9.2 Three Faces of Pregnancy Autonomy


One question, three terrains:

What may the state do to a pregnant person on the grounds that her conduct affects a future person? (Substance use)
What may a medical team do to a pregnant patient against her stated preferences? (Refusal of care)
What may either do to a pregnant body when the person herself is no longer there? (Brain death)




And the inversion:

What may the state and the medical profession do to pregnant people by leaving them out of the evidence base?

The same word — autonomy — does different work in each terrain. The two-models framework you have today is what tells you which work it is doing in each case.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
9.3 Review Questions
Write a brief answer to each, or use them as discussion prompts.


Recall
In your own words: what’s the difference between liberal and relational autonomy, and between soft and hard paternalism? Give one example of each.


Apply
A 30-week pregnant patient with opioid addiction refuses inpatient rehab — she has two other children at home — but agrees to outpatient medication treatment (buprenorphine) and weekly visits.
