title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
section: 2.1 Why This Line Matters in Practice
text:
2.1 Why This Line Matters in Practice
The treatment-enhancement distinction is not just an academic puzzle. It does work in three concrete places.

Insurance. Health insurers pay for treatment. They do not pay for enhancement. Whether a procedure is one or the other decides who can afford it.
Professional duty. Doctors have an obligation to treat. They do not have an obligation to enhance. A patient cannot demand enhancement the way she can demand treatment.
Regulation. The FDA approves drugs as treatments for specific conditions. Off-label and enhancement uses fall outside its main review.

Move a procedure from one side of the line to the other, and three different systems respond.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
2.2 A Working Definition
The standard definition in the philosophical literature is precise.
Enhancement = biomedical interventions used to improve human form or functioning beyond what is necessary to restore or sustain health.
A few implications travel with that definition.

It is the use, not the technology, that makes something an enhancement. The same drug can be treatment for one patient and enhancement for the next.
Most enhancement-capable drugs and procedures enter medicine first as treatments. They show up as enhancements later, off-label.
The definition rests on what counts as “health” — which is exactly the unstable concept the next three slides will probe. (Moseley and Juengst 2025)

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
2.3 Three Accounts at a Glance









Account
What treatment is
What enhancement is
Who advocates




Professional-domain
What the profession says it is
What the profession refuses to do
Practitioners; medical societies


Normal-function
Restores species-typical function
Pushes beyond the typical range
Boorse 1977; Daniels


Disease-based
Addresses a diagnosable malady
Acts on a healthy system
Gert, Culver, Clouser 2006



Each account does real work in clinical practice. Each one also keeps collapsing under pressure.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
2.4 The Professional-Domain Account

The idea. Treatment is whatever doctors agree they should do. Enhancement is whatever they refuse. Medical societies set the line by professional consensus.
Why it collapses. Medicine has a track record of medicalization — absorbing new conditions into its scope as cultural demand grows. Shyness became social anxiety disorder. Restlessness in boys became ADHD. Aging in men became “low T.” Each was once outside medicine. Each is now inside.
If the profession’s own boundaries shift with demand, the profession’s word for what counts as treatment cannot be the bedrock of the distinction.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
2.5 The Normal-Function Account


The idea (Boorse, Daniels)
Health is the ability to do what people of your age and sex can typically do. Treatment restores you to that range. Enhancement pushes you above it.
This account anchors the distinction in physiology, not professional opinion.



Where it collapses

Prevention. A vaccine pushes your immune response above normal on purpose. Statins lower cholesterol below the average for your age. Both look like enhancement under this account. Both are clearly treatment in practice.
Disability. A powered wheelchair that travels faster than a healthy walker does counts as enhancement here. Asking why is asking what is wrong with the account.



(Boorse 1977)

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
2.6 The Disease-Based Account

The idea (Gert, Culver, Clouser). Treatment addresses a malady — a condition that involves real harm and has no good external cause. Enhancement acts on a healthy system that has no malady to address.
Why it collapses. The list of recognized maladies keeps growing — and often grows to match what the profession (and the market) want to provide. This is nosological elasticity: coining new diseases to license new interventions.
The clearest current example is the next block of slides. ADHD diagnosis rates have moved in lockstep with stimulant prescriptions, which have moved in lockstep with classroom and workplace demand for focus. Each step was inside the disease-based account. The aggregate looks more like enhancement.

(Gert et al. 2006)

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
2.7 Why the Line Keeps Collapsing
Each account does something the other two cannot.

The professional-domain account is honest about who actually decides. It gives no principled answer.
The normal-function account is honest about biology. It collapses on prevention.
The disease-based account is honest about clinical practice. It collapses under medicalization.

The distinction does not vanish. It just stops being settle-able from the outside. Every concrete case in this lecture — Casgevy, Adderall, He Jiankui, polygenic IQ — runs the same argument again, on different ground.
That is the lecture’s spine. Watch for it. (Moseley and Juengst 2025)

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
3.1 Somatic vs. Germline


Somatic changes

In one tissue, in one person.
Not in sperm or egg cells.
Not inherited.
The change dies with the patient.
All currently approved gene therapies are somatic.




Germline changes

In every cell of the resulting person, including reproductive cells.
Heritable. Passed to children.
Each descendant inherits it.
Banned for clinical use in nearly every country.
The He Jiankui case is the only known human germline edit to date.



The same molecular tool can do either. The moral weight is in which cells it touches.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
3.2 CRISPR-Cas9 — How It Works







CRISPR


Design a short\nguide RNA

Design a short
guide RNA



Guide RNA + Cas9\nenter the cell

Guide RNA + Cas9
enter the cell



Design a short\nguide RNA->Guide RNA + Cas9\nenter the cell





Guide finds matching\nDNA sequence

Guide finds matching
DNA sequence



Guide RNA + Cas9\nenter the cell->Guide finds matching\nDNA sequence





Cas9 cuts\nthe DNA

Cas9 cuts
the DNA



Guide finds matching\nDNA sequence->Cas9 cuts\nthe DNA





Cell repairs the cut\n(edit takes effect)

Cell repairs the cut
(edit takes effect)



Cas9 cuts\nthe DNA->Cell repairs the cut\n(edit takes effect)










CRISPR-Cas9 is, in plain terms, a programmable find-and-replace for DNA. You design a short guide molecule that matches the sequence you want to change. The guide drags Cas9 (a cutting enzyme) to that spot. Cas9 cuts. The cell tries to repair the cut, and the edit ends up in place.
Casgevy and the He Jiankui case both use this technology. The difference between them is somatic vs. germline.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
3.3 Casgevy (FDA December 2023)
The first CRISPR-based therapy approved for clinical use in the US treats sickle-cell disease, an inherited blood disorder that causes severe pain crises and organ damage.
The procedure works like this. Doctors harvest the patient’s own stem cells. In the lab, they use CRISPR to switch on a gene that produces a different form of hemoglobin. The edited cells are then reinfused. The patient’s bone marrow makes the new hemoglobin from then on.
This is a somatic edit. It changes the patient. It does not change her future children.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
3.4 Casgevy by the Numbers

Approved by the FDA in December 2023.
About 100,000 Americans live with sickle-cell disease. Most are Black.
The list price is roughly $2.2 million per patient.
Insurance coverage in early 2026 is uneven. Medicaid coverage varies by state.
The procedure requires weeks of chemotherapy to clear the marrow before the edited cells go back in. Most patients can only be treated at specialized centers.

The science is a triumph. The access question is a justice question. We will come back to it in the health-care lecture.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
3.5 Luxturna and the Broader Picture
Luxturna (FDA 2017) treats an inherited form of blindness caused by a mutation in the RPE65 gene. A working copy of the gene is injected directly into the retina. About $425,000 per eye.
Several other somatic gene therapies are now approved or in late-stage trials — for hemophilia, for spinal muscular atrophy, for some inherited immune disorders.
All of them are clearly treatment under all three accounts of the line. None of the moral arguments later in this lecture apply to them.
The hard cases start when the same tools are used in embryos — or in healthy people.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
3.6 He Jiankui — Briefly

What he did. In November 2018, Chinese scientist He Jiankui announced he had used CRISPR to edit the CCR5 gene in human embryos. He had implanted the embryos. Twin girls — Lulu and Nana — had been born. A third edited child was born shortly after.
His stated goal was to confer resistance to HIV. The edit was performed without serious clinical justification, with deeply flawed consent, and outside any sanctioned oversight.
He was sentenced to three years in prison in 2019. He was released in 2022 and has since attempted to return to research. The international scientific consensus against germline editing has held — for now.


Paired case: He Jiankui’s CRISPR Twins — the full case study, including the consent failures and the scientific community’s response.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
4.1 Off-Label Stimulants in Healthy Adults
The most common form of enhancement actually happening today does not involve embryos or CRISPR. It involves a prescription pad.

Roughly 1 in 6 US college students reports nonmedical use of stimulants like Adderall.
Rates are higher in pre-professional programs — nursing, medical, and law students above the average.
Modafinil, prescribed for narcolepsy, is now widely used off-label by shift workers, surgical residents, and military personnel.
Most users get their pills from a friend with a prescription. A growing number get them from telehealth services.

This is enhancement in plain sight. It is also enhancement that looks a lot like treatment from the outside.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
4.2 What the Drugs Actually Do
The marketing implies a cognitive transformation. The evidence is more modest.

In diagnosed ADHD, stimulants improve attention and reduce disruptive symptoms in a clear, well-replicated way.
In healthy adults, the effects are small. Recent meta-analyses find a modest boost to simple attention tasks and a near-zero effect on complex problem-solving.
Side effects — insomnia, anxiety, raised heart rate, dependence — are real and dose-dependent.
The largest effect for healthy users is on perceived productivity. Subjects feel sharper, even when their actual performance does not move much.

This matters for the ethics. The drugs are not nothing. They are also not what the campus rumor says they are.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
4.3 ADHD Diagnosis Over Time








Year
US children diagnosed with ADHD
Adult stimulant prescriptions




1997
~3%
uncommon


2007
~7%
rising


2016
~10%
common in adults


2022
~11%
sharply higher; telehealth-driven



The diagnosis rate has roughly tripled in twenty-five years. The criteria have broadened in each DSM revision since 1994.
Some of that increase is real recognition of a real condition. Some of it is the disease-based account collapsing in slow motion: as demand for the drug grew, the criteria for the diagnosis that justifies the drug expanded to match. Nosological elasticity in real time.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
section: 4.4 Thought Question — A Roommate’s NCLEX Request
text:
4.4 Thought Question — A Roommate’s NCLEX Request

Your roommate, a healthy nursing student with no ADHD diagnosis, asks to borrow one of your Adderall pills the night before NCLEX. She is anxious. She has heard from upperclassmen that “everyone uses it.” You are prescribed it for a diagnosis you have had since high school.
Write or discuss.

Apply the three accounts of the treatment-enhancement line to her request. Which one makes the strongest case for “no”?
Does the answer change if she says she just wants to try one pill at a normal dose?
If you would say no to your roommate, would you say no to yourself using the same drug to gain an edge on the same test?

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
4.5 After You’ve Discussed






Note



The three accounts give three different reasons for “no” — and they cut at different angles.

Professional-domain: prescribing is a professional act. Sharing a prescription with someone undiagnosed is outside that act. The clinical relationship is what makes the use legitimate; outside that relationship, the same pill is something else.
Normal-function: the drug pushes attention above the normal range in a healthy user. That is enhancement, not treatment.
Disease-based: she has no malady. There is nothing to treat. The pill is acting on a healthy system to give her a competitive edge.

A fourth angle is equity. If passing NCLEX increasingly requires stimulants, the test starts measuring access to a diagnosis, not nursing knowledge.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
5.1 Parfit’s 14-Year-Old Girl






Important



The thought experiment
Imagine a 14-year-old girl who is thinking about having a child. If she has the child now, the child will have a difficult start — limited resources, an inexperienced parent, predictable hardships. If she waits until she is 22, she will be more prepared, more stable, and the child she has then will have a much better life.
It seems obvious that waiting is the right thing to do.
But here is the puzzle. The child she would have at 14 is not the same person as the child she would have at 22. Different egg, different sperm, different person. If she waits, the first child never exists. He doesn’t get a better life; he simply does not get a life at all.
— Derek Parfit, Reasons and Persons (1984)

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
5.2 The Puzzle Stated Sharply
So who was wronged by the early pregnancy?

Not the actual child. His alternative was nonexistence. He is glad to be alive. He has no complaint.
Not the never-existing child. That child has no complaints, because that child never existed.

The puzzle is real. We feel a strong intuition that the early pregnancy was wrong. The standard person-affecting reasoning cannot say who was wronged. (Parfit 1984)
This matters everywhere we choose the conditions of someone’s existence — embryo selection, germline editing, even decisions about when to conceive.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
section: 5.3 He Jiankui’s Twins Through This Lens
text:
5.3 He Jiankui’s Twins Through This Lens
Lulu and Nana exist only because their parents took part in He Jiankui’s experiment. A different embryo would have meant a different child — or no child at all.
So on the simplest reading: the twins were not wronged by being edited. The alternative was that they not exist.
That cannot be where the analysis ends. Most people share a strong sense that what He did was wrong. The non-identity problem forces us to locate the wrong somewhere other than “harm to the specific child who exists.”
Two candidate locations show up later in the lecture: a wrong to the kind of relationship parents and children should have (Sandel, Habermas), and a wrong to the future adult who was set up without consent (Feinberg, Habermas).

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
6.1 The Central Question
If safe, accurate gene editing or embryo selection were available — and many forms already are — should parents use them to give their children advantages they would not otherwise have?
Three serious answers. Each is held by careful philosophers. None is fringe.

Savulescu: yes, and parents have a positive duty to do so.
Sandel: no, because doing so corrodes something valuable in the parent-child relationship.
Habermas: no, because the child cannot consent to being someone else’s project.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
6.2 Savulescu — Procreative Beneficence

The argument. If you are going to bring a child into existence, you have a moral reason to bring the child with the best expected life. The reasoning is straightforward consequentialism: more well-being is better than less well-being, all else equal.
The argument applies the same way to disease risk and to traits. If a parent should pick the embryo less likely to develop cancer, the parent should also pick the embryo more likely to be tall, intelligent, or cheerful — to the extent those traits track well-being.
Savulescu calls this procreative beneficence. It is the most permissive position in the contemporary literature.

(Savulescu 2001)

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
6.3 Sandel — Verbatim

“To appreciate children as gifts is to accept them as they come, not as objects of our design, or products of our will, or instruments of our ambition. Parental love is not contingent on the talents and attributes the child happens to have.”
— Michael Sandel, The Case Against Perfection: Ethics in the Age of Genetic Engineering (2007), p. 45.

(Sandel 2007)

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
6.4 Sandel’s Giftedness Argument
The argument is not that enhancement makes children worse off. It often does not. The argument is about what enhancement does to the parent and to the practice of parenting.

A parent who accepts the child as a gift can love the child unconditionally. Effort goes into the child’s flourishing on the child’s own terms.
A parent who designs the child treats the child as a project. Love starts to track how well the project is going.
Beyond the family, a society of designed children loses what Sandel calls solidarity — the sense that we are all subject to the same lottery and owe each other something for it.

The argument is not against medicine. It is against the idea that parenting is a manufacturing problem.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
6.5 Habermas — Making vs. Growing
For Habermas, the wrong of enhancement is not in the child’s outcomes. It is in the kind of being a designed child is.

A child who grew — under nature’s contingency — can come to understand her life as her own. The traits she has are not someone else’s choice.
A child who was made — selected or edited toward a specific design — is, in a real sense, the project of her parents’ lives. She cannot retroactively consent to that. She cannot un-design herself.

This is not a religious argument. It is an argument about the structure of autonomy. The future adult is owed a life that is hers, not an extension of someone else’s plan.
The He Jiankui twins are the sharpest test case. They exist only because of the edit. They were also, in Habermas’s terms, made in a way no child should be. (Habermas 2003)

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
6.6 Three Positions at a Glance










Savulescu
Sandel
Habermas




Enhancement is
A duty, where it improves the child’s life
A corrosion of the parent-child relationship
A wrong to the future autonomous adult


What’s lost without it
Well-being the child could have had
Nothing — the giftedness is the point
Nothing — the autonomy is the point


What’s lost with it
Nothing intrinsic; only access concerns
Unconditional love; social solidarity
The child’s authorship of her own life


Polygenic IQ scoring is
Permissible; perhaps obligatory if affordable
The textbook example of the drive to mastery
The clearest case of making a person



All three can agree that Casgevy is fine. They split sharply on embryo selection.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
section: 7.1 Open Future, Now for the Body
text:
7.1 Open Future, Now for the Body
Part A used Feinberg’s argument about a child’s right to an open future for the case of genetic information — testing a healthy child for an adult-onset disease.
Part B applies the same principle to genetic engineering. The principle is the same: do not foreclose, in advance, choices the future adult should be free to make on her own.

The informational version says: don’t tell the eight-year-old about her BRCA risk before she can decide whether she wants to know.
The engineering version says: don’t build the eight-year-old toward a specific future before she can decide what kind of future she wants.

The argument is consistent with letting the same person, at 25, walk into a clinic and choose. (Feinberg 1980)

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
section: 7.2 The Future Person Who Never Consented
text:
7.2 The Future Person Who Never Consented
Habermas and Feinberg meet at the He Jiankui twins.

Habermas: they were made, in a way that makes their lives the project of someone else’s plan.
Feinberg: a future they might have chosen for themselves was foreclosed in the embryo dish.
Non-identity problem: they cannot complain that they were harmed, because the alternative was nonexistence.

The wrong, then, is not to the twins-vs-no-twins comparison. It is to the kind of relationship He Jiankui’s experiment set up between him, the parents, and two future adults who could never have agreed to be designed this way.
That is the cleanest formulation in the literature. It is also why germline editing remains banned in clinical use almost everywhere.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
8.1 What Polygenic Embryo Selection Is







PGTPRS


IVF\n(6–10 embryos)

IVF
(6–10 embryos)



Biopsy a few cells\nfrom each embryo

Biopsy a few cells
from each embryo



IVF\n(6–10 embryos)->Biopsy a few cells\nfrom each embryo





Sequence the DNA

Sequence the DNA



Biopsy a few cells\nfrom each embryo->Sequence the DNA





Calculate polygenic\nrisk scores

Calculate polygenic
risk scores



Sequence the DNA->Calculate polygenic\nrisk scores





Rank embryos

Rank embryos



Calculate polygenic\nrisk scores->Rank embryos





Implant the chosen\nembryo

Implant the chosen
embryo



Rank embryos->Implant the chosen\nembryo










No editing. No CRISPR. Just selection.
The clinic creates embryos through IVF, tests each one, calculates a polygenic risk score for whatever traits the parents are paying to assess, and lets the parents choose which embryo to implant.
The technology has been used to screen out single-gene diseases (Tay-Sachs, cystic fibrosis) for decades. What is new is selecting on risk of common disease — and on traits like height or predicted IQ.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
section: 8.2 From Disease Screening to Trait Selection
text:
8.2 From Disease Screening to Trait Selection
The standard preimplantation test screens for things almost no one wants — a fatal childhood disease, a chromosomal abnormality. The new market screens for things that look more like preferences.

Risk of adult heart disease.
Risk of type 2 diabetes.
Predicted height.
Predicted educational attainment.
Predicted IQ.

Each step looks like a small extension of the last. The aggregate has crossed a line the field used to police more carefully. There is no US federal law against it.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
8.3 Orchid Health and Heliospect
Two US companies are the live edge of the field.

Orchid Health (San Francisco) offers what it calls “whole-genome embryo screening” — sequencing every embryo and reporting risk scores across hundreds of conditions, including some adult-onset ones. Prices range from a few thousand dollars per embryo upward.
Heliospect Genomics offers polygenic embryo selection that explicitly includes scoring for IQ. Reporting by The Guardian in 2024 documented the service in detail.

Both operate legally in the US. The market is small but growing. Neither product is available in most European countries.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
section: 8.4 How Accurate Is a Polygenic IQ Score?
text:
8.4 How Accurate Is a Polygenic IQ Score?
The marketing implies precision. The science is humbler.

Current polygenic scores for IQ explain about 10–15% of the variance in intelligence-test performance. The rest is environment, gene-environment interaction, and noise.
For a couple selecting among 6–10 sibling embryos, the expected IQ gain from picking the top-ranked one is roughly 2–4 points. Within the noise of the test itself.
Predictions work best in the population the score was trained on — overwhelmingly people of European ancestry, as Part A noted.
Almost everything that makes a person’s life go well — temperament, motivation, the household they grow up in — is not in the score.

The product on offer is not a designer baby. It is a small statistical nudge sold with the language of choice.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
8.5 Thought Question — IQ at $50,000

You and your partner can afford the $50,000 selection package. The clinic shows you six embryos ranked by predicted IQ. The top embryo is 2.3 points higher than the bottom one. The clinic counselor says “most of our families choose the top.”
Write or discuss.

Do you select on IQ? Why or why not?
Suppose the service became free tomorrow. Does your answer change?
Suppose the gap were 10 points instead of 2.3. Does your answer change?
If you would not select for IQ, would you select for low risk of type 2 diabetes? What is different about the two choices?

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
8.6 After You’ve Discussed






Note



A few distinctions worth carrying away.

What is actually selected. The “best” embryo is best only in expectation, and the expectation is small. The choice is a probability bet, not a guarantee.
What is left out. Most of what shapes a child’s life is environment and chance — neither of which is in the score. Selection promises agency over a slice of the outcome and quiet about the rest.
Where the three positions land. Savulescu says select. Sandel says you have just defined the parent-child relationship in terms of a 2-point statistical edge. Habermas says you have committed a future adult to a project she cannot un-author.
Equity. The service is currently sold at price points that exclude most American families. If it works at all, it works to widen advantage that money already buys.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
9.1 Scoreboard — The Treatment-Enhancement Collapse








Where it shows up
Looks like treatment because
Looks like enhancement because




Casgevy for sickle cell
Treats a clear malady; restores normal hemoglobin
Permanent edit; price puts it out of reach for most patients


Adderall off-label
Same molecule as the prescription drug
Acts on a healthy brain; gives a competitive edge


He Jiankui’s CCR5 edit
Framed as HIV prevention
Healthy embryos; no clear medical need; heritable


Polygenic disease screening
Selects against future illness
Treats embryos as products to be ranked


Polygenic IQ selection
Sold as “best chance for your child”
No malady; pure trait selection


Future germline editing
Could prevent inherited disease
Could redesign human capacities



Six cases. One unstable line. That is what the lecture has been arguing.

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
9.2 Review Questions
Write a brief answer to each, or use them as discussion prompts.


Recall
State the three accounts of the treatment-enhancement distinction in your own words. For each, name one concrete example where it gives an unhelpful answer.


Apply
A young woman with sickle-cell disease is considering Casgevy, but her state’s Medicaid program may not cover it. Name two things she needs to understand about the treatment, and one ethical issue that goes beyond her individual case.


Debate
A US state legislature is considering a bill that would ban polygenic embryo selection for non-disease traits in fertility clinics. Take a position. Identify the strongest argument against your position and respond to it.

text:
9.3 Key Terms

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
Where We Left Off
In Part A every hard case was about whether to let dying happen — to stop or withhold treatment for a patient who could no longer decide. We ended on a puzzle: the law treats withholding and withdrawing treatment as the same, yet many caregivers feel that stopping a machine causes the death in a way that never starting it does not.
Part B walks straight into that feeling. We now ask about a sharper act: not letting a patient die, but actively helping them die — on their own clear request. This is where medicine, law, and philosophy disagree most deeply.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
A Case to Sit With

In early 2014, Brittany Maynard, 29, was diagnosed with an aggressive brain cancer (glioblastoma) and given about six months to live. Her doctors described the death she could expect: seizures, escalating pain, loss of speech and self.
She and her husband moved from California to Oregon, one of the few states where a terminally ill, mentally competent adult can ask a doctor for a prescription to end their own life. She set a date, spoke publicly about her choice, and on November 1, 2014, took the medication and died at home. (Maynard 2014)
Write or discuss:

Is what Brittany did different, morally, from a patient who refuses a ventilator and dies?
Her doctor wrote the prescription; she took it herself. Does who performs the final act matter?
Should this option be legal everywhere — or are there people it would put at risk?

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


Notice how Brittany’s case differs from Nancy Cruzan’s in Part A.

Cruzan could not speak for herself; others decided whether to let her die.
Maynard was fully competent and decided for herself; she asked for help to actively end her life.

Part A was about who decides for the silent. Part B is about what a person may ask for — and what others may do — when the patient is the one asking. Almost everything today turns on one disputed line: the line between killing and letting die.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
Today

By the end of Part B, you will be able to:

Sort the key terms — active/passive euthanasia, voluntary/non-voluntary/involuntary, and physician-assisted death — and say why the distinctions matter.
Reconstruct Rachels’ argument that killing and letting die carry no moral weight in themselves, and Foot’s reply.
Explain the positive case for assisted death from Brock (autonomy) and Singer (quality of life).
Explain Velleman’s secular argument against a right to die.
State the disability-rights critique and how Harriet McBryde Johnson pressed it against Singer.
Apply the doctrine of double effect to palliative sedation, and describe where assisted death is legal and what the data show.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
Why Vocabulary Comes First
This is the one topic where people argue past each other because they are using the same words for different acts. Before any ethics, we need a clean map.
Three distinctions do all the work:

Active vs. passive — doing something to cause death vs. withholding something.
Voluntary vs. non-voluntary vs. involuntary — who wanted it.
Assisted death vs. euthanasia — who performs the final act.

Get these straight and most of the confusion disappears.

Voluntary
(patient asks)



who->vol





non

(patient can't say)

(against patient's

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
Active vs. Passive Euthanasia
Euthanasia means bringing about a death for the sake of the person who dies — a “good death.” It splits in two:

Active euthanasia — someone administers something that causes death, such as a lethal injection.
Passive euthanasia — death is allowed to happen by withholding or withdrawing treatment, such as stopping a ventilator.

Almost every legal system treats these very differently: passive euthanasia is widely accepted, while active euthanasia is illegal in most of the world. The whole philosophical fight is about whether that difference is justified.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
section: “Withdrawal Is Not Killing” — The Standard View
text:
“Withdrawal Is Not Killing” — The Standard View
Most clinicians and courts insist that stopping treatment is not the same as killing.

When a ventilator is withdrawn and the patient dies, the standard view says the disease kills her; the doctor merely steps out of the way.
When a doctor injects a lethal drug, the doctor kills her.

On this view, a patient has a strong right to refuse treatment (so passive euthanasia is permissible), but no one has a right to be killed (so active euthanasia is not). Hold onto this claim — it is exactly what Rachels will attack.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
Assisted Death vs. Euthanasia


Physician-assisted death (PAD / MAID)
The doctor prescribes a lethal medication, but the patient takes it themselves. This is what Brittany Maynard did. The final act is the patient’s.



(Active, voluntary) euthanasia
The doctor administers the medication directly — for example, a lethal injection at the patient’s request. The final act is the doctor’s.

This line is legally decisive. US states that permit assisted death allow only the prescribing model; Canada and several European countries allow the doctor to administer as well. “MAID” (Medical Assistance in Dying) is the Canadian term and covers both.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
The Map of Terms









Term
Who acts last

Typical legal status

Patient (refuses)

Passive euthanasia
Doctor (withholds)
Usually voluntary/non-vol.
Widely accepted

Doctor (administers)
Voluntary

Doctor

Involuntary euthanasia
Doctor

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
The View Rachels Attacks
In 1975 the American Medical Association’s policy captured the standard view exactly: a doctor may stop “extraordinary means” of prolonging life (passive), but the “intentional termination of the life of one human being by another — mercy killing” (active) is forbidden. (Rachels 1975)
The assumption: passive euthanasia is sometimes permissible, active euthanasia never is — because killing is in itself worse than letting die.
James Rachels set out to show that this assumption is confused.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
Rachels’ Challenge (1975)
Rachels argues that if we have already decided a patient should die — say, a newborn with a condition so severe that withholding treatment is judged acceptable — then passive euthanasia is often the crueler option.

Withholding treatment can mean a slow death over days, by dehydration or infection.
A quick lethal injection would cause less suffering.

So if anything, the act we permit (passive) can be worse for the patient than the act we forbid (active). Something has gone wrong with the distinction.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
Smith and Jones

Rachels’ thought experiment

Smith will inherit a fortune if his young cousin dies. He sneaks into the bathroom and drowns the boy in the bath, making it look like an accident.
Jones stands to inherit the same fortune. He sneaks in planning to drown the boy — but the child slips, hits his head, and slides face-down into the water. Jones watches, ready to push him under if needed, and lets him drown.

Smith killed; Jones merely let die. Is Jones any less monstrous? Almost no one thinks so. (Rachels 1975)

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
The Bare Difference Argument
The Smith and Jones case is built so that everything is the same except killing vs. letting die — same motive, same outcome, same intention. Rachels calls what is left the bare difference.

If killing were worse than letting die in itself, Jones would be at least a little less guilty than Smith.
He is not. So the bare difference between killing and letting die carries no moral weight on its own.

If that is right, then “but that would be killing” cannot, by itself, settle the euthanasia debate.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
section: What Rachels Does and Doesn’t Show
text:
What Rachels Does and Doesn’t Show






Important


Be precise about the conclusion.

Rachels does not show that active euthanasia is right, or that killing is usually fine.
He shows only that the killing/letting-die distinction itself does not carry the moral weight people lean on it for.

Other things — consent, motive, consequences, risk of abuse — may still make killing usually worse. Rachels just denies that the bare act-type does the work. Whether anything else can rescue the distinction is Foot’s question.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
Foot’s Reply: Duties, Not Acts
Philippa Foot agrees the bare physical difference is thin — but argues the distinction survives once we ground it in duties and rights. (Foot 1977)

A negative duty not to harm people (not to kill) is generally more stringent than a positive duty to aid people (to save).
I may not murder a stranger to use his organs, even to save five others — my duty not to kill him outweighs my duty to save them.

So “killing vs. letting die” is a stand-in for “violating a strong negative duty vs. a weaker positive one.” The distinction was tracking something real after all.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
Why Foot’s Move Matters


On Foot’s view, the reason Jones is as bad as Smith is not that letting die equals killing in general. It is that Jones had no right to let the boy drown — he had a duty to save him and a murderous intent besides.
Change the case to a doctor who may permissibly stop a treatment the patient refused, and the negative/positive distinction does real work: the doctor violates no duty by stopping, but would violate one by injecting.


The upshot
Rachels and Foot agree the bare act-type is morally inert. They split on whether the duties behind the act-type — strong duties not to harm, weaker duties to aid — revive a defensible line between euthanasia’s forms.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
Scoreboard: Rachels vs. Foot









Rachels (1975)
Foot (1977)




Bare act-type matters?
No
No


So the distinction is empty?
Largely, yes
No — re-ground it


What does the real work?
Consequences, motive, consent
Negative vs. positive duties


Implication for euthanasia
Removes a key objection
Leaves a principled brake



The debate does not end here — it relocates. If the line between active and passive is not bedrock, the next question is whether the values underneath it actually support assisted death.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
Brock’s Positive Case
Dan Brock turns the discussion around. Instead of asking whether killing is special, he asks: what values justify letting patients refuse treatment in the first place? (Brock 1992)
He finds two:

Self-determination — competent people have the right to shape their own lives, including their dying.
Well-being — we let people judge for themselves when continued life is a benefit or a burden.

These are the same two values, Brock argues, that support voluntary active euthanasia.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
Brock: The Parity Argument
If self-determination and well-being are weighty enough to let a patient refuse a ventilator and die, Brock asks, why are they not weighty enough to let a patient request a lethal drug and die?

In both, a competent person judges that continued life is worse than death.
In both, a doctor helps carry out that judgment.

Brock’s challenge to the standard view: you cannot consistently celebrate the right to refuse treatment and flatly forbid voluntary euthanasia, because the same moral foundation underlies both. He takes the worries seriously, but locates them in consequences and safeguards, not in the act itself.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
Singer: Rejecting Sanctity of Life
Peter Singer pushes further, attacking the premise he thinks blocks clear thinking: the sanctity-of-life doctrine — the idea that human life is always equally precious simply because it is human. (Singer 1994)

Singer replaces it with a quality-of-life ethic: what matters morally is the character of a life for the one living it.
For a preference utilitarian like Singer, killing a self-aware being is wrong mainly because it thwarts that being’s preferences — so when a person prefers death, voluntary euthanasia can respect rather than violate them.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
section: Singer’s Edge — and the Backlash It Invites
text:
Singer’s Edge — and the Backlash It Invites






Important


Singer’s view has a sharp edge that will matter for the rest of the lecture.
If what makes killing wrong is thwarting a being’s preferences about its own future, then beings without such preferences — Singer’s examples include some severely impaired newborns — do not have the same protection. Singer has defended non-voluntary euthanasia in such cases. (Singer 2011)
This is exactly the reasoning that the disability-rights movement regards as dangerous — and it is why a quality-of-life ethic, meant to expand compassion, draws its fiercest critics from disabled people themselves.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
Thought Question

A patient with ALS is on a ventilator and fully competent. She asks her physician to turn it off, knowing she will die within minutes. The physician does, giving sedation for comfort. The patient dies. Everyone agrees this was lawful and ethical.
The next patient, with the same disease but not yet ventilator-dependent, asks the physician for a lethal medication to take at home.

Is the physician’s role really different in the two cases?
If the first is permitted, on what grounds would you forbid the second?
Does it change your answer that, in the first case, the patient could not have lived long without the machine?

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
Make It Contemporary

This is not hypothetical. After Brittany Maynard’s death, California passed the End of Life Option Act (2015), joining Oregon (1997), Washington, Colorado, and others. By the mid-2020s, assisted death was legal in eleven US jurisdictions, covering roughly a fifth of Americans.
Every US law allows only the prescribing model, requires a terminal diagnosis (usually six months), and demands the patient be competent and act voluntarily.
Discuss: Are those three safeguards — terminal, competent, voluntary — the right lines? Which would you tighten or loosen?

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


A distinction the safeguards quietly assume:

In assisted death, the patient performs the final act. This builds in a last check — the person must still, at the end, choose to act.
In euthanasia, someone else performs it. This is available to patients too disabled to act for themselves, but it removes that final self-check.

US law has chosen the first model precisely because the patient’s last act is itself a safeguard. Canada and the Netherlands accept the second — and inherit a harder problem of verifying consent at the very end.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
section: Velleman: Against the Right to Die
text:
Velleman: Against the Right to Die
J. David Velleman offers the most interesting secular objection — one that grants almost everything to the other side. (Velleman 1992)

He concedes that death can genuinely benefit some patients, and that they may be entitled to it.
He still argues against establishing a right or institution of assisted death.

His worry is not about the act. It is about what offering the option to everyone does to everyone.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
section: Velleman: The Cost of Having a Choice
text:
Velleman: The Cost of Having a Choice

Why an option can harm you
Right now, a gravely ill person who keeps living needs no justification — there was simply no choice. But once death becomes an accepted option, the situation changes for everyone, including those who never wanted it.
Now a patient must, in effect, justify continuing to live — to family, to caregivers, even to herself. The frail and dependent may feel they ought to “stop being a burden.” Merely making the choice available can strip away the protection of having no choice at all. (Velleman 1992)

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
Velleman: A Careful Conclusion






Important


Velleman’s argument is easy to misread, so be exact.

He is not claiming euthanasia is intrinsically wrong, or that no one should ever have it.
He argues that a general right or public policy is dangerous, because the option’s mere existence reshapes the pressures on the vulnerable.

This is why his view cuts across the usual lines: a secular philosopher, granting that death can be a benefit, still resisting a right to it. It also sets up the critics who say the vulnerable are not hypothetical.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
The Disability-Rights Critique
For disability-rights groups such as Not Dead Yet, Velleman’s abstract worry is daily reality. Their objection is not religious; it is about who is actually at risk.

“Better dead than disabled.” Assisted-death laws rest on the judgment that some lives are not worth living — and the lives singled out are usually disabled ones.
People often request death not because of pain but because of dependence, lost dignity, and being a burden — conditions that better support could change.
The same society that offers suicide prevention to the able-bodied may offer suicide assistance to the disabled. That asymmetry is the heart of the critique.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
McBryde Johnson Meets Singer


Harriet McBryde Johnson was a lawyer and disability-rights activist who had a progressive neuromuscular condition and used a wheelchair. In 2002 she debated Peter Singer at Princeton and wrote about it in “Unspeakable Conversations.” (Johnson 2003)
Her core reply to Singer: his confidence that disability makes a life worse is an outsider’s assumption. Disabled people consistently rate their own quality of life far higher than the non-disabled imagine. The premise of the quality-of-life argument is, she said, simply mistaken.


The unsettling part
Johnson found Singer personable and his logic internally consistent — which is what frightened her. A coherent, well-meaning argument can still license treating some lives as expendable. The danger is not cartoon villainy; it is reasonable-sounding premises.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
Coercion, Duty, and Access
The critics converge on three concrete risks that safeguards must answer.

Coercion — subtle pressure from family, cost, or exhausted caregivers, not held at gunpoint but nudged.
The “duty to die” — once dying is an option, the dependent may feel obligated to take it (Velleman’s worry, lived).
Access disparities — those with the least social support may find death the most available form of “care,” while the well-resourced get treatment.

These are empirical questions. Which is why both sides now argue about data — and we turn to it after one more conceptual tool.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
The Doctrine of Double Effect
The doctrine of double effect tries to explain how an act with a foreseen bad effect can still be permissible — if the bad effect is not intended. (McIntyre 2023)
A classic four-part test: the act is permissible only if

the act itself is good or neutral (giving pain medication);
the bad effect is not the means to the good (relief doesn’t work by killing);
the bad effect is foreseen but not intended; and
there is a proportionate reason (the suffering is grave enough).

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
Double Effect in Practice
The standard medical example: a dying patient in severe pain is given escalating morphine. The dose needed for comfort may also depress breathing and hasten death.

Under double effect, this is permissible: the doctor intends relief and merely foresees the risk to breathing.
The same physical act — giving the drug — would be wrong if the doctor intended the death and used pain relief as a cover.

Double effect makes intention do the moral work. Critics (including Rachels) ask whether a distinction that turns on a doctor’s private intention can really bear that much weight.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
Palliative Sedation
Palliative sedation is the practice of sedating a dying patient — sometimes to unconsciousness — to relieve symptoms that nothing else can touch (intractable pain, agitation, breathlessness).

It is legal and widely accepted, and is usually justified by double effect: the aim is relief of suffering, not death.
In its deepest form — continuous sedation to unconsciousness, often while withholding artificial nutrition and hydration — the patient never wakes and dies within days.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
Is Palliative Sedation “Slow Euthanasia”?


The defense
The intention is to relieve refractory suffering, not to kill. Studies suggest properly titrated sedation does not clearly shorten life. By double effect, it stays on the right side of the line.



The worry
If you sedate someone to permanent unconsciousness and stop feeding them, the outcome is certain death. Critics call this “slow euthanasia” — euthanasia that keeps the form of double effect while abandoning its substance.

This is the same fault line as Rachels vs. Foot, in a new place: does the intention behind an act change what the act is?

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
Where It Is Legal

Who is eligible

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
Oregon After 25+ Years
Oregon’s law is the most studied in the world, because the state publishes annual data. The findings are consistent year after year. (Oregon Health Authority, Public Health Division 2024)

The top reasons patients give are loss of autonomy, being less able to enjoy activities, and loss of dignity — each cited by most patients.
Inadequate pain control (or fear of it) is cited by a minority — roughly a quarter.
About a third of patients who get the prescription never take it; having the option is itself a comfort.

The data complicate both sides: little evidence of the predicted “carnage,” but confirmation that the drivers are existential, not just physical pain.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
Canada: The Fastest Expansion
Canada moved further and faster than anywhere else.

2015: In Carter v. Canada, the Supreme Court struck down the ban on assisted death. (Supreme Court of Canada 2015)
2016: Parliament legalized MAID for adults whose natural death was “reasonably foreseeable.”
2021: A new law removed the terminal requirement, allowing MAID for serious, incurable conditions that are not life-threatening.

By 2023, MAID accounted for about 1 in 20 deaths in Canada — among the highest rates in the world, and the speed of expansion is itself now part of the debate.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
The Psychiatric MAID Controversy
The hardest frontier is assisted death for psychiatric suffering — where the illness is mental, not terminal.

The Netherlands, Belgium, and Spain permit assisted death for non-terminal suffering, including conditions that are partly or wholly psychiatric.
The dispute is sharp because two of the law’s safeguards strain here: Is the suffering truly irremediable? Can a condition that distorts judgment leave the patient fully competent to consent?

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
section: A Case at the Frontier: Noelia Castillo
text:
A Case at the Frontier: Noelia Castillo


In 2022, Noelia Castillo, a young Spanish woman with OCD and borderline personality disorder, attempted suicide and was left paraplegic and in constant pain. In 2024 she asked to die under Spain’s euthanasia law.
Her father fought her in court for 601 days, arguing her mental illness made her wish to die a symptom, not a choice. Every court — up to the European Court of Human Rights — found she had capacity. She died in March 2026, age 25.







Important


Why it’s the hard case
Her death was approved for physical pain, but fought over psychiatric capacity. She was not dying — she was disabled. So the case fuses both critiques: Is this autonomy honored, or a vulnerable woman society failed first and offered death second?

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
section: Castillo: Two Readings of the Same Facts
text:
Castillo: Two Readings of the Same Facts


Autonomy honored
Refusing a capable woman’s request only because her suffering is psychiatric applies a double standard we never use for physical illness. Every court agreed she could choose.



Vulnerability abandoned
Safeguards checked whether she qualified, not why she got there: lost home, institutional care, untreated trauma, assaults never investigated. A pianist offered to fund her care. Was death simply more available than a livable life?

The paired case study works this through in full. It is the live form of every argument in this lecture.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
Canada Hits the Brakes

A pause worth noticing
Canada planned to extend MAID to people whose sole underlying condition is a mental illness. The start date was set for March 2023 — then delayed to 2024 — then delayed again to March 2027.
The reasons cited were exactly the critics’ worries: clinicians were not confident they could reliably tell an irremediable mental illness from a treatable one, or a settled wish from a symptom. Even a system committed to expansion found a line it was not ready to cross.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
section: After You’ve Discussed: Two Kinds of Slope
text:
After You’ve Discussed: Two Kinds of Slope






Note


“Slippery slope” is often dismissed as a fallacy, but separate two versions:

A logical slope says permitting A commits you to B. This is usually weak — you can permit voluntary, terminal cases and still forbid the rest, as Oregon does.
An empirical slope says that in practice, categories drift: eligibility widens, safeguards loosen, numbers climb. Canada’s trajectory is evidence this can happen — not as logic, but as politics and habit.

The honest question is not “is there a slope?” but “which slope, and is this society good at stopping on it?”

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
Wrap-Up

We began with one disputed line — killing vs. letting die — and watched Rachels argue it carries no weight in itself while Foot rebuilt it from duties. Brock and Singer pressed the case for assisted death from autonomy and quality of life; Velleman and the disability-rights critics, led here by Harriet McBryde Johnson, pressed back — not from religion, but from concern for the vulnerable and for what a mere option does to them.
The doctrine of double effect let us see palliative sedation as the same fight in new clothing: does intention change what an act is? And the law on the ground — Oregon’s steady data, Canada’s rapid expansion and its sudden pause on psychiatric cases — shows a society still arguing, in real time, about where the brakes belong. The line between mercy and danger is exactly what no one has finished drawing.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
Review Questions


Recall
Explain the Smith and Jones case and state precisely what Rachels concludes from it — and what he does not conclude.


Apply
A hospice patient with no pain asks for assisted death, citing loss of dignity and fear of being a burden. Using Velleman and the disability critique, what would worry you about granting it?


Debate
Should assisted death ever be available for psychiatric suffering alone, as in Belgium and the Netherlands — or was Canada right to pause?

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
Key Terms

Active euthanasia — administering something that causes death.
Passive euthanasia — allowing death by withholding or withdrawing treatment.
Voluntary / non-voluntary / involuntary — with the patient’s request / without their input / against their wish.
Physician-assisted death (PAD/MAID) — the doctor prescribes; the patient performs the final act.
Bare difference argument (Rachels) — killing vs. letting die carries no moral weight in itself.
Negative vs. positive duties (Foot) — the duty not to harm is more stringent than the duty to aid.
Doctrine of double effect — a foreseen bad effect can be permissible if unintended and proportionate.
Palliative sedation — sedating a dying patient to relieve refractory suffering.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
Paired Case
Work through the paired case study on Noelia Castillo — a woman left disabled by a suicide attempt who won a two-year legal battle for euthanasia in Spain. It tests every argument in this lecture against the hardest case: psychiatric suffering, contested capacity, and a family that fought to keep her alive.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
In Part A every hard case was about whether to let dying happen — to stop or withhold treatment for a patient who could no longer decide. We ended on a puzzle: the law treats withholding and withdrawing treatment as the same, yet many caregivers feel that stopping a machine causes the death in a way that never starting it does not.
Part B walks straight into that feeling. We now ask about a sharper act: not letting a patient die, but actively helping them die — on their own clear request. This is where medicine, law, and philosophy disagree most deeply.




In early 2014, Brittany Maynard, 29, was diagnosed with an aggressive brain cancer (glioblastoma) and given about six months to live. Her doctors described the death she could expect: seizures, escalating pain, loss of speech and self.
She and her husband moved from California to Oregon, one of the few states where a terminally ill, mentally competent adult can ask a doctor for a prescription to end their own life. She set a date, spoke publicly about her choice, and on November 1, 2014, took the medication and died at home. (Maynard 2014)
Write or discuss:

Is what Brittany did different, morally, from a patient who refuses a ventilator and dies?
Her doctor wrote the prescription; she took it herself. Does who performs the final act matter?
Should this option be legal everywhere — or are there people it would put at risk?











Note



Notice how Brittany’s case differs from Nancy Cruzan’s in Part A.

Cruzan could not speak for herself; others decided whether to let her die.
Maynard was fully competent and decided for herself; she asked for help to actively end her life.

Part A was about who decides for the silent. Part B is about what a person may ask for — and what others may do — when the patient is the one asking. Almost everything today turns on one disputed line: the line between killing and letting die.






By the end of Part B, you will be able to:

Sort the key terms — active/passive euthanasia, voluntary/non-voluntary/involuntary, and physician-assisted death — and say why the distinctions matter.
Reconstruct Rachels’ argument that killing and letting die carry no moral weight in themselves, and Foot’s reply.
Explain the positive case for assisted death from Brock (autonomy) and Singer (quality of life).
Explain Velleman’s secular argument against a right to die.
State the disability-rights critique and how Harriet McBryde Johnson pressed it against Singer.
Apply the doctrine of double effect to palliative sedation, and describe where assisted death is legal and what the data show.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
In Part A every hard case was about whether to let dying happen — to stop or withhold treatment for a patient who could no longer decide. We ended on a puzzle: the law treats withholding and withdrawing treatment as the same, yet many caregivers feel that stopping a machine causes the death in a way that never starting it does not.
Part B walks straight into that feeling. We now ask about a sharper act: not letting a patient die, but actively helping them die — on their own clear request. This is where medicine, law, and philosophy disagree most deeply.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
In early 2014, Brittany Maynard, 29, was diagnosed with an aggressive brain cancer (glioblastoma) and given about six months to live. Her doctors described the death she could expect: seizures, escalating pain, loss of speech and self.
She and her husband moved from California to Oregon, one of the few states where a terminally ill, mentally competent adult can ask a doctor for a prescription to end their own life. She set a date, spoke publicly about her choice, and on November 1, 2014, took the medication and died at home. (Maynard 2014)
Write or discuss:

Is what Brittany did different, morally, from a patient who refuses a ventilator and dies?
Her doctor wrote the prescription; she took it herself. Does who performs the final act matter?
Should this option be legal everywhere — or are there people it would put at risk?

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
Note



Notice how Brittany’s case differs from Nancy Cruzan’s in Part A.

Cruzan could not speak for herself; others decided whether to let her die.
Maynard was fully competent and decided for herself; she asked for help to actively end her life.

Part A was about who decides for the silent. Part B is about what a person may ask for — and what others may do — when the patient is the one asking. Almost everything today turns on one disputed line: the line between killing and letting die.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
By the end of Part B, you will be able to:

Sort the key terms — active/passive euthanasia, voluntary/non-voluntary/involuntary, and physician-assisted death — and say why the distinctions matter.
Reconstruct Rachels’ argument that killing and letting die carry no moral weight in themselves, and Foot’s reply.
Explain the positive case for assisted death from Brock (autonomy) and Singer (quality of life).
Explain Velleman’s secular argument against a right to die.
State the disability-rights critique and how Harriet McBryde Johnson pressed it against Singer.
Apply the doctrine of double effect to palliative sedation, and describe where assisted death is legal and what the data show.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
2.1 Why Vocabulary Comes First
This is the one topic where people argue past each other because they are using the same words for different acts. Before any ethics, we need a clean map.
Three distinctions do all the work:

Active vs. passive — doing something to cause death vs. withholding something.
Voluntary vs. non-voluntary vs. involuntary — who wanted it.
Assisted death vs. euthanasia — who performs the final act.

Get these straight and most of the confusion disappears.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
2.2 The Concept Map







G



cause

Causing a patient's death

lethal drug)

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
2.3 Active vs. Passive Euthanasia
Euthanasia means bringing about a death for the sake of the person who dies — a “good death.” It splits in two:

Active euthanasia — someone administers something that causes death, such as a lethal injection.
Passive euthanasia — death is allowed to happen by withholding or withdrawing treatment, such as stopping a ventilator.

Almost every legal system treats these very differently: passive euthanasia is widely accepted, while active euthanasia is illegal in most of the world. The whole philosophical fight is about whether that difference is justified.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
section: 2.4 “Withdrawal Is Not Killing” — The Standard View
text:
2.4 “Withdrawal Is Not Killing” — The Standard View
Most clinicians and courts insist that stopping treatment is not the same as killing.

When a ventilator is withdrawn and the patient dies, the standard view says the disease kills her; the doctor merely steps out of the way.
When a doctor injects a lethal drug, the doctor kills her.

On this view, a patient has a strong right to refuse treatment (so passive euthanasia is permissible), but no one has a right to be killed (so active euthanasia is not). Hold onto this claim — it is exactly what Rachels will attack.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
2.5 Assisted Death vs. Euthanasia


Physician-assisted death (PAD / MAID)
The doctor prescribes a lethal medication, but the patient takes it themselves. This is what Brittany Maynard did. The final act is the patient’s.



(Active, voluntary) euthanasia
The doctor administers the medication directly — for example, a lethal injection at the patient’s request. The final act is the doctor’s.


This line is legally decisive. US states that permit assisted death allow only the prescribing model; Canada and several European countries allow the doctor to administer as well. “MAID” (Medical Assistance in Dying) is the Canadian term and covers both.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
2.6 The Map of Terms
