title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
3.1 The View Rachels Attacks
In 1975 the American Medical Association’s policy captured the standard view exactly: a doctor may stop “extraordinary means” of prolonging life (passive), but the “intentional termination of the life of one human being by another — mercy killing” (active) is forbidden. (Rachels 1975)
The assumption: passive euthanasia is sometimes permissible, active euthanasia never is — because killing is in itself worse than letting die.
James Rachels set out to show that this assumption is confused.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
3.2 Rachels’ Challenge (1975)
Rachels argues that if we have already decided a patient should die — say, a newborn with a condition so severe that withholding treatment is judged acceptable — then passive euthanasia is often the crueler option.

Withholding treatment can mean a slow death over days, by dehydration or infection.
A quick lethal injection would cause less suffering.

So if anything, the act we permit (passive) can be worse for the patient than the act we forbid (active). Something has gone wrong with the distinction.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
3.3 Smith and Jones

Rachels’ thought experiment

Smith will inherit a fortune if his young cousin dies. He sneaks into the bathroom and drowns the boy in the bath, making it look like an accident.
Jones stands to inherit the same fortune. He sneaks in planning to drown the boy — but the child slips, hits his head, and slides face-down into the water. Jones watches, ready to push him under if needed, and lets him drown.

Smith killed; Jones merely let die. Is Jones any less monstrous? Almost no one thinks so. (Rachels 1975)

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
3.4 The Bare Difference Argument
The Smith and Jones case is built so that everything is the same except killing vs. letting die — same motive, same outcome, same intention. Rachels calls what is left the bare difference.

If killing were worse than letting die in itself, Jones would be at least a little less guilty than Smith.
He is not. So the bare difference between killing and letting die carries no moral weight on its own.

If that is right, then “but that would be killing” cannot, by itself, settle the euthanasia debate.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
section: 3.5 What Rachels Does and Doesn’t Show
text:
3.5 What Rachels Does and Doesn’t Show






Important



Be precise about the conclusion.

Rachels does not show that active euthanasia is right, or that killing is usually fine.
He shows only that the killing/letting-die distinction itself does not carry the moral weight people lean on it for.

Other things — consent, motive, consequences, risk of abuse — may still make killing usually worse. Rachels just denies that the bare act-type does the work. Whether anything else can rescue the distinction is Foot’s question.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
3.6 Foot’s Reply: Duties, Not Acts
Philippa Foot agrees the bare physical difference is thin — but argues the distinction survives once we ground it in duties and rights. (Foot 1977)

A negative duty not to harm people (not to kill) is generally more stringent than a positive duty to aid people (to save).
I may not murder a stranger to use his organs, even to save five others — my duty not to kill him outweighs my duty to save them.

So “killing vs. letting die” is a stand-in for “violating a strong negative duty vs. a weaker positive one.” The distinction was tracking something real after all.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
3.7 Why Foot’s Move Matters


On Foot’s view, the reason Jones is as bad as Smith is not that letting die equals killing in general. It is that Jones had no right to let the boy drown — he had a duty to save him and a murderous intent besides.
Change the case to a doctor who may permissibly stop a treatment the patient refused, and the negative/positive distinction does real work: the doctor violates no duty by stopping, but would violate one by injecting.


The upshot
Rachels and Foot agree the bare act-type is morally inert. They split on whether the duties behind the act-type — strong duties not to harm, weaker duties to aid — revive a defensible line between euthanasia’s forms.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
3.8 Scoreboard: Rachels vs. Foot









Rachels (1975)
Foot (1977)




Bare act-type matters?
No
No


So the distinction is empty?
Largely, yes
No — re-ground it


What does the real work?
Consequences, motive, consent
Negative vs. positive duties


Implication for euthanasia
Removes a key objection
Leaves a principled brake



The debate does not end here — it relocates. If the line between active and passive is not bedrock, the next question is whether the values underneath it actually support assisted death.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
4.1 Brock’s Positive Case
Dan Brock turns the discussion around. Instead of asking whether killing is special, he asks: what values justify letting patients refuse treatment in the first place? (Brock 1992)
He finds two:

Self-determination — competent people have the right to shape their own lives, including their dying.
Well-being — we let people judge for themselves when continued life is a benefit or a burden.

These are the same two values, Brock argues, that support voluntary active euthanasia.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
4.2 Brock: The Parity Argument
If self-determination and well-being are weighty enough to let a patient refuse a ventilator and die, Brock asks, why are they not weighty enough to let a patient request a lethal drug and die?

In both, a competent person judges that continued life is worse than death.
In both, a doctor helps carry out that judgment.

Brock’s challenge to the standard view: you cannot consistently celebrate the right to refuse treatment and flatly forbid voluntary euthanasia, because the same moral foundation underlies both. He takes the worries seriously, but locates them in consequences and safeguards, not in the act itself.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
4.3 Singer: Rejecting Sanctity of Life
Peter Singer pushes further, attacking the premise he thinks blocks clear thinking: the sanctity-of-life doctrine — the idea that human life is always equally precious simply because it is human. (Singer 1994)

Singer replaces it with a quality-of-life ethic: what matters morally is the character of a life for the one living it.
For a preference utilitarian like Singer, killing a self-aware being is wrong mainly because it thwarts that being’s preferences — so when a person prefers death, voluntary euthanasia can respect rather than violate them.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
section: 4.4 Singer’s Edge — and the Backlash It Invites
text:
4.4 Singer’s Edge — and the Backlash It Invites






Important



Singer’s view has a sharp edge that will matter for the rest of the lecture.
If what makes killing wrong is thwarting a being’s preferences about its own future, then beings without such preferences — Singer’s examples include some severely impaired newborns — do not have the same protection. Singer has defended non-voluntary euthanasia in such cases. (Singer 2011)
This is exactly the reasoning that the disability-rights movement regards as dangerous — and it is why a quality-of-life ethic, meant to expand compassion, draws its fiercest critics from disabled people themselves.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
4.5 Thought Question

A patient with ALS is on a ventilator and fully competent. She asks her physician to turn it off, knowing she will die within minutes. The physician does, giving sedation for comfort. The patient dies. Everyone agrees this was lawful and ethical.
The next patient, with the same disease but not yet ventilator-dependent, asks the physician for a lethal medication to take at home.

Is the physician’s role really different in the two cases?
If the first is permitted, on what grounds would you forbid the second?
Does it change your answer that, in the first case, the patient could not have lived long without the machine?

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
4.6 Make It Contemporary

This is not hypothetical. After Brittany Maynard’s death, California passed the End of Life Option Act (2015), joining Oregon (1997), Washington, Colorado, and others. By the mid-2020s, assisted death was legal in eleven US jurisdictions, covering roughly a fifth of Americans.
Every US law allows only the prescribing model, requires a terminal diagnosis (usually six months), and demands the patient be competent and act voluntarily.
Discuss: Are those three safeguards — terminal, competent, voluntary — the right lines? Which would you tighten or loosen?

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
4.7 After You’ve Discussed






Note



A distinction the safeguards quietly assume:

In assisted death, the patient performs the final act. This builds in a last check — the person must still, at the end, choose to act.
In euthanasia, someone else performs it. This is available to patients too disabled to act for themselves, but it removes that final self-check.

US law has chosen the first model precisely because the patient’s last act is itself a safeguard. Canada and the Netherlands accept the second — and inherit a harder problem of verifying consent at the very end.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
section: 5.1 Velleman: Against the Right to Die
text:
5.1 Velleman: Against the Right to Die
J. David Velleman offers the most interesting secular objection — one that grants almost everything to the other side. (Velleman 1992)

He concedes that death can genuinely benefit some patients, and that they may be entitled to it.
He still argues against establishing a right or institution of assisted death.

His worry is not about the act. It is about what offering the option to everyone does to everyone.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
section: 5.2 Velleman: The Cost of Having a Choice
text:
5.2 Velleman: The Cost of Having a Choice

Why an option can harm you
Right now, a gravely ill person who keeps living needs no justification — there was simply no choice. But once death becomes an accepted option, the situation changes for everyone, including those who never wanted it.
Now a patient must, in effect, justify continuing to live — to family, to caregivers, even to herself. The frail and dependent may feel they ought to “stop being a burden.” Merely making the choice available can strip away the protection of having no choice at all. (Velleman 1992)

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
5.3 Velleman: A Careful Conclusion






Important



Velleman’s argument is easy to misread, so be exact.

He is not claiming euthanasia is intrinsically wrong, or that no one should ever have it.
He argues that a general right or public policy is dangerous, because the option’s mere existence reshapes the pressures on the vulnerable.

This is why his view cuts across the usual lines: a secular philosopher, granting that death can be a benefit, still resisting a right to it. It also sets up the critics who say the vulnerable are not hypothetical.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
5.4 The Disability-Rights Critique
For disability-rights groups such as Not Dead Yet, Velleman’s abstract worry is daily reality. Their objection is not religious; it is about who is actually at risk.

“Better dead than disabled.” Assisted-death laws rest on the judgment that some lives are not worth living — and the lives singled out are usually disabled ones.
People often request death not because of pain but because of dependence, lost dignity, and being a burden — conditions that better support could change.
The same society that offers suicide prevention to the able-bodied may offer suicide assistance to the disabled. That asymmetry is the heart of the critique.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
5.5 McBryde Johnson Meets Singer


Harriet McBryde Johnson was a lawyer and disability-rights activist who had a progressive neuromuscular condition and used a wheelchair. In 2002 she debated Peter Singer at Princeton and wrote about it in “Unspeakable Conversations.” (Johnson 2003)
Her core reply to Singer: his confidence that disability makes a life worse is an outsider’s assumption. Disabled people consistently rate their own quality of life far higher than the non-disabled imagine. The premise of the quality-of-life argument is, she said, simply mistaken.


The unsettling part
Johnson found Singer personable and his logic internally consistent — which is what frightened her. A coherent, well-meaning argument can still license treating some lives as expendable. The danger is not cartoon villainy; it is reasonable-sounding premises.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
5.6 Coercion, Duty, and Access
The critics converge on three concrete risks that safeguards must answer.

Coercion — subtle pressure from family, cost, or exhausted caregivers, not held at gunpoint but nudged.
The “duty to die” — once dying is an option, the dependent may feel obligated to take it (Velleman’s worry, lived).
Access disparities — those with the least social support may find death the most available form of “care,” while the well-resourced get treatment.

These are empirical questions. Which is why both sides now argue about data — and we turn to it after one more conceptual tool.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
6.1 The Doctrine of Double Effect
The doctrine of double effect tries to explain how an act with a foreseen bad effect can still be permissible — if the bad effect is not intended. (McIntyre 2023)
A classic four-part test: the act is permissible only if

the act itself is good or neutral (giving pain medication);
the bad effect is not the means to the good (relief doesn’t work by killing);
the bad effect is foreseen but not intended; and
there is a proportionate reason (the suffering is grave enough).

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
6.2 Double Effect in Practice
The standard medical example: a dying patient in severe pain is given escalating morphine. The dose needed for comfort may also depress breathing and hasten death.

Under double effect, this is permissible: the doctor intends relief and merely foresees the risk to breathing.
The same physical act — giving the drug — would be wrong if the doctor intended the death and used pain relief as a cover.

Double effect makes intention do the moral work. Critics (including Rachels) ask whether a distinction that turns on a doctor’s private intention can really bear that much weight.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
6.3 Palliative Sedation
Palliative sedation is the practice of sedating a dying patient — sometimes to unconsciousness — to relieve symptoms that nothing else can touch (intractable pain, agitation, breathlessness).

It is legal and widely accepted, and is usually justified by double effect: the aim is relief of suffering, not death.
In its deepest form — continuous sedation to unconsciousness, often while withholding artificial nutrition and hydration — the patient never wakes and dies within days.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
6.4 Is Palliative Sedation “Slow Euthanasia”?


The defense
The intention is to relieve refractory suffering, not to kill. Studies suggest properly titrated sedation does not clearly shorten life. By double effect, it stays on the right side of the line.



The worry
If you sedate someone to permanent unconsciousness and stop feeding them, the outcome is certain death. Critics call this “slow euthanasia” — euthanasia that keeps the form of double effect while abandoning its substance.


This is the same fault line as Rachels vs. Foot, in a new place: does the intention behind an act change what the act is?

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
7.1 Where It Is Legal

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
7.2 Oregon After 25+ Years
Oregon’s law is the most studied in the world, because the state publishes annual data. The findings are consistent year after year. (Oregon Health Authority, Public Health Division 2024)

The top reasons patients give are loss of autonomy, being less able to enjoy activities, and loss of dignity — each cited by most patients.
Inadequate pain control (or fear of it) is cited by a minority — roughly a quarter.
About a third of patients who get the prescription never take it; having the option is itself a comfort.

The data complicate both sides: little evidence of the predicted “carnage,” but confirmation that the drivers are existential, not just physical pain.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
7.3 Canada: The Fastest Expansion
Canada moved further and faster than anywhere else.

2015: In Carter v. Canada, the Supreme Court struck down the ban on assisted death. (Supreme Court of Canada 2015)
2016: Parliament legalized MAID for adults whose natural death was “reasonably foreseeable.”
2021: A new law removed the terminal requirement, allowing MAID for serious, incurable conditions that are not life-threatening.

By 2023, MAID accounted for about 1 in 20 deaths in Canada — among the highest rates in the world, and the speed of expansion is itself now part of the debate.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
7.4 The Psychiatric MAID Controversy
The hardest frontier is assisted death for psychiatric suffering — where the illness is mental, not terminal.

The Netherlands, Belgium, and Spain permit assisted death for non-terminal suffering, including conditions that are partly or wholly psychiatric.
The dispute is sharp because two of the law’s safeguards strain here: Is the suffering truly irremediable? Can a condition that distorts judgment leave the patient fully competent to consent?

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
section: 7.5 A Case at the Frontier: Noelia Castillo
text:
7.5 A Case at the Frontier: Noelia Castillo


In 2022, Noelia Castillo, a young Spanish woman with OCD and borderline personality disorder, attempted suicide and was left paraplegic and in constant pain. In 2024 she asked to die under Spain’s euthanasia law.
Her father fought her in court for 601 days, arguing her mental illness made her wish to die a symptom, not a choice. Every court — up to the European Court of Human Rights — found she had capacity. She died in March 2026, age 25.







Important



Why it’s the hard case
Her death was approved for physical pain, but fought over psychiatric capacity. She was not dying — she was disabled. So the case fuses both critiques: Is this autonomy honored, or a vulnerable woman society failed first and offered death second?

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
section: 7.6 Castillo: Two Readings of the Same Facts
text:
7.6 Castillo: Two Readings of the Same Facts


Autonomy honored
Refusing a capable woman’s request only because her suffering is psychiatric applies a double standard we never use for physical illness. Every court agreed she could choose.



Vulnerability abandoned
Safeguards checked whether she qualified, not why she got there: lost home, institutional care, untreated trauma, assaults never investigated. A pianist offered to fund her care. Was death simply more available than a livable life?


The paired case study works this through in full. It is the live form of every argument in this lecture.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
7.7 Canada Hits the Brakes

A pause worth noticing
Canada planned to extend MAID to people whose sole underlying condition is a mental illness. The start date was set for March 2023 — then delayed to 2024 — then delayed again to March 2027.
The reasons cited were exactly the critics’ worries: clinicians were not confident they could reliably tell an irremediable mental illness from a treatable one, or a settled wish from a symptom. Even a system committed to expansion found a line it was not ready to cross.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
section: 7.8 After You’ve Discussed: Two Kinds of Slope
text:
7.8 After You’ve Discussed: Two Kinds of Slope






Note



“Slippery slope” is often dismissed as a fallacy, but separate two versions:

A logical slope says permitting A commits you to B. This is usually weak — you can permit voluntary, terminal cases and still forbid the rest, as Oregon does.
An empirical slope says that in practice, categories drift: eligibility widens, safeguards loosen, numbers climb. Canada’s trajectory is evidence this can happen — not as logic, but as politics and habit.

The honest question is not “is there a slope?” but “which slope, and is this society good at stopping on it?”

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
7.9 Wrap-Up

We began with one disputed line — killing vs. letting die — and watched Rachels argue it carries no weight in itself while Foot rebuilt it from duties. Brock and Singer pressed the case for assisted death from autonomy and quality of life; Velleman and the disability-rights critics, led here by Harriet McBryde Johnson, pressed back — not from religion, but from concern for the vulnerable and for what a mere option does to them.
The doctrine of double effect let us see palliative sedation as the same fight in new clothing: does intention change what an act is? And the law on the ground — Oregon’s steady data, Canada’s rapid expansion and its sudden pause on psychiatric cases — shows a society still arguing, in real time, about where the brakes belong. The line between mercy and danger is exactly what no one has finished drawing.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
7.10 Review Questions


Recall
Explain the Smith and Jones case and state precisely what Rachels concludes from it — and what he does not conclude.


Apply
A hospice patient with no pain asks for assisted death, citing loss of dignity and fear of being a burden. Using Velleman and the disability critique, what would worry you about granting it?


Debate
Should assisted death ever be available for psychiatric suffering alone, as in Belgium and the Netherlands — or was Canada right to pause?

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
7.11 Key Terms

Active euthanasia — administering something that causes death.
Passive euthanasia — allowing death by withholding or withdrawing treatment.
Voluntary / non-voluntary / involuntary — with the patient’s request / without their input / against their wish.
Physician-assisted death (PAD/MAID) — the doctor prescribes; the patient performs the final act.
Bare difference argument (Rachels) — killing vs. letting die carries no moral weight in itself.
Negative vs. positive duties (Foot) — the duty not to harm is more stringent than the duty to aid.
Doctrine of double effect — a foreseen bad effect can be permissible if unintended and proportionate.
Palliative sedation — sedating a dying patient to relieve refractory suffering.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:
7.12 Paired Case
Work through the paired case study on Noelia Castillo — a woman left disabled by a suicide attempt who won a two-year legal battle for euthanasia in Spain. It tests every argument in this lecture against the hardest case: psychiatric suffering, contested capacity, and a family that fought to keep her alive.

title: Lecture 7 — End-of-Life Care
text:

Velleman, J. David. 1992. “Against the Right to Die.” Journal of Medicine and Philosophy 17 (6): 665–81. https://doi.org/10.1093/jmp/17.6.665.


Young, Robert. 2024. Voluntary Euthanasia. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy (Spring 2024 Edition), Edward N. Zalta and Uri Nodelman (eds.). https://plato.stanford.edu/archives/spr2024/entries/euthanasia-voluntary/.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Where We Left Off
In Part A we built four lenses for asking what a just society owes people: maximize good (utilitarian), protect the worst-off (Rawls), respect free choices (libertarian), secure what people need to function (capabilities). We argued, with Daniels, about whether health care is a right at all.
That was the theory. Part B is the machine those values have to deal with — the real US health care system. Before we can argue about how to fix it, we have to see clearly how it actually works, what it costs, and what it delivers.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Guess First

Before the numbers appear, write down your best guess.

How much does the US spend on health care per person, per year — counting everything (insurance, taxes, out-of-pocket)?
Among wealthy countries, where do you think the US ranks on life expectancy — near the top, middle, or bottom?
Do you expect Americans to use more doctor visits and hospital days than people in other rich countries, or fewer?

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
The Paradox
The United States spends far more on health care than any nation on Earth — and its people are not healthier for it.




United States
OECD average




Spending per person (2023)
~$14,570
~$6,000–7,000


Share of the whole economy
17.6% of GDP
~9–11%


Life expectancy (2023)
78.4 years
81.1 years


Maternal deaths / 100,000
~17
~10



We spend roughly double — and live nearly three years less (Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services 2024; Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development 2025). Everything in this lecture answers one question: why?

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


Hold onto the gap. The US spends the most and ranks near the bottom of its peers on life expectancy, infant mortality, and maternal mortality. This is not a small inefficiency — it is the central puzzle of American health policy.
And it is where Part A stops being abstract. A country that treats care as mostly a market good has built a system with extraordinary capabilities and extraordinary gaps. By the end you should be able to say, in the language of justice, what we are getting right and what we owe each other.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Today

By the end of Part B, you will be able to:

Explain the paradox: highest spending, middling outcomes.
Describe the four models of a health system (Beveridge, National Health Insurance, Bismarck, out-of-pocket) and name countries that use each.
Show why the US is “fragmented” — it runs all four models at once — and map its programs onto them.
Trace how the system got this way since 1945, and explain why US care is so expensive (“it’s the prices, not the utilization”) and why outcomes lag.
Name what the US does best and where it struggles, including the nursing workforce crisis.
Argue, using a Part A value, which model the US should move toward.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Two Questions Define Every System
Before we look at the US, we need a map. Every health system on earth is built from the answers to just two questions:

Who delivers the care? — does the government own the hospitals and employ the staff, or are they private?
Who pays for it? — the government from taxes, regulated insurance funds, or you, out of pocket?

The journalist T. R. Reid sorted the world’s systems into four models by those two answers (Reid 2009). Almost every country picks one. Keep that word — one — in mind.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
The Four Models









Model
Who delivers
Who pays
Where you’ll find it




Beveridge
government owns it
government (taxes)
UK (NHS), Spain, Cuba


National Health Insurance
private providers
one government insurer
Canada, Taiwan


Bismarck
private providers
regulated nonprofit funds (payroll)
Germany, France, Japan


Out-of-pocket
whoever you can find
you, if you can pay
most low-income countries



The deepest split is who pays, running from “the public, for everyone” (Beveridge, NHI) to “private but tightly regulated” (Bismarck) to “you’re on your own” (out-of-pocket).

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Beveridge vs. National Health Insurance


Beveridge (UK’s NHS, since 1948)
The government owns the hospitals and employs the doctors, and funds it all through taxes. Care is free at the point of use. This is what “socialized medicine” actually means — the state runs the whole thing.



National Health Insurance (Canada, Taiwan)
Doctors and hospitals stay private, but a single government insurer pays them, funded by taxes. One payer, private delivery. No marketing, no denials for profit, strong bargaining power on prices.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Bismarck vs. Out-of-Pocket


Bismarck (Germany since 1883, France, Japan)
Care and insurance are private — but insurers are nonprofit “sickness funds,” everyone must be covered, and prices are tightly regulated. Switzerland’s mandate, and the US ACA, are cousins of this model.



Out-of-pocket
No system at all: you get the care you can pay for, and go without the rest. This is the reality for most people in poor countries — and, as we’ll see, for the uninsured in the US.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
section: A Caution Before We Go On
text:
A Caution Before We Go On






Note


These four models are idealizations. Real countries blend at the edges — the British buy private top-up insurance, Germany has some public hospitals, Canada leaves dental and drugs largely private.
But the framework holds where it matters: each country builds its system around one model and makes it coherent. Hold that thought — because the United States did something no one else did.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
One Life, Every Program
The US has no single health system. It has a patchwork — and the easiest way to see it is to follow one person through a lifetime.

Meet Maria.

Born: her family is low-income, so she’s covered by Medicaid/CHIP.
Age 26: good job with benefits — employer-sponsored insurance.
Age 34: laid off. Briefly uninsured, then buys a plan on the ACA exchange with a subsidy.
Age 60: chronic illness, can’t keep working full-time — a coverage gap.
Age 65: ages into Medicare.
Age 81: needs a nursing home; Medicare won’t cover long-term care, so she spends down her savings until Medicaid does.

One person. Six different programs — and, as we’re about to see, three different countries’ models of health care, all without leaving the US.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Who Covers Whom
US health coverage in 2023 (Keisler-Starkey et al. 2024):



Source of coverage
Share of population




Employer-sponsored
~54%


Medicare (65+ and disabled)
~19%


Medicaid (low-income)
~19%


Direct purchase / ACA exchanges
~10%


VA / military
a few %


Uninsured
~8% (≈26 million)



Shares add to more than 100% because many people hold more than one type in a year. About 92.5% of Americans had some coverage — near a record high, and still leaving 26 million with none.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
section: The US Runs All Four Models at Once
text:
The US Runs All Four Models at Once
This is the heart of “fragmentation.” Other countries pick one model. The US runs all four simultaneously — and which one you live under depends on your age, job, income, and luck (Reid 2009).









If you are…
Your coverage
…is really this model
…like which country




A veteran (VA)
government hospitals
Beveridge
the UK


65 or older
Medicare
National Health Insurance
Canada


Working with benefits
employer insurance
Bismarck
Germany


Uninsured
pay or go without
Out-of-pocket
the developing world



A single country containing Britain, Canada, Germany, and the developing world — sorted by who you happen to be.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
section: Why “Fragmented” Is Expensive, Not Just Messy
text:
Why “Fragmented” Is Expensive, Not Just Messy
Running four systems at once isn’t only untidy — it has real costs.

No coherence: each model has its own rules, paperwork, eligibility, and incentives. Providers must bill all of them at once.
Gaps at the seams: people fall between models — too rich for Medicaid, too young for Medicare, no job-based plan. That’s where the 26 million uninsured live.
No single bargaining power: because no one payer dominates, no one can negotiate prices the way Canada’s or the UK’s system can. We’ll see the price consequences shortly.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Employer-Sponsored Insurance — the Core
About half of Americans get coverage through a job — the single largest piece of the system, and the one most people never chose.

Your employer buys a group plan and pays much of the premium; you pay the rest from your paycheck.
In 2024, the average family plan cost $25,572 a year in total premiums — most of it invisible to workers because the employer “pays” it (out of wages they’d otherwise get).
Lose or change the job, and you can lose the coverage. Maria’s layoff is the system’s built-in fragility.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Medicare and Medicaid — the 1965 Pillars


Medicare — federal, for people 65+ and the long-term disabled.

Part A: hospital
Part B: outpatient/doctors
Part C: private “Advantage” plans
Part D: drugs

Nearly universal for the old — the closest thing the US has to a right to care.



Medicaid — joint federal–state, for low-income people.

Rules and generosity vary by state.
The ACA let states expand it to more low-income adults; not all did.
Quietly enormous: it pays for about 4 in 10 US births and most nursing-home care.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
The VA — Actual Government Medicine
One corner of the US system looks like the rest of the wealthy world.

The Veterans Health Administration doesn’t just insure veterans — it owns the hospitals and employs the doctors. The government delivers the care directly.
This is pure Beveridge — the UK’s model, running inside the United States. It’s what people usually mean by “socialized medicine,” and it’s already the largest integrated system in the country.
So the same nation that leaves 26 million uninsured also runs a full British-style health service for its veterans. That is the patchwork in one breath.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
The Exchanges and the Uninsured
For people without a job-based plan, Medicare, or Medicaid, the ACA marketplaces (since 2014) sell individual plans, with income-based subsidies.

About 10% of Americans buy coverage this way or on the individual market.
Even so, ~26 million remain uninsured — too well-off for Medicaid, no job-based plan, or unwilling/unable to buy in.
The uninsured don’t simply go without: by law, emergency rooms must still treat them — a fact with its own history, coming up next.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
section: The Accident That Built the System
text:
The Accident That Built the System
America’s job-based system was not designed. It was an accident of World War II.

Wartime wage controls (1942–43) froze what employers could pay. To compete for scarce workers, they offered health benefits instead of raises.
Then the government ruled those benefits tax-free — confirmed in the 1954 tax code. A dollar of health insurance wasn’t taxed; a dollar of wages was.
That tax break made job-based coverage explode. The US tied health care to employment not by choice, but by a loophole no one ever closed.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
The Hidden Subsidy
That 1954 tax break is still here — and it is enormous.

Excluding employer health benefits from taxes cost the federal government about $299 billion in 2022 — the single largest tax expenditure in the entire federal budget (Tax Policy Center 2024).
It is invisible: most workers have no idea they receive it.
It is regressive — worth more to higher earners in higher tax brackets. The US already heavily subsidizes health care; it just does it quietly, and tilts it toward the well-off.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
A Short History, 1945–Today







G



a

1948
Truman's national
plan fails



b

1965
Medicare &
Medicaid



a->b





c

1986
EMTALA:
ERs must treat



b->c





d

1994
Clinton plan
fails



c->d





e

2003
Medicare
Part D (drugs)



d->e





f

2010
ACA: exchanges,
Medicaid expansion



e->f










Two big expansions (1965, 2010), two big failures (1948, 1994), and one unfunded promise — EMTALA, which since 1986 has required hospitals to screen and stabilize anyone in an emergency, regardless of ability to pay (United States Congress 1986).

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
What Changed, and What Didn’t
Eighty years of fights, and the core never moved.

What changed: the old got Medicare; the poor got Medicaid; emergencies got EMTALA; the individual market got the ACA’s exchanges and protections (United States Congress 2010).
What didn’t: coverage is still built around the employer, and the US still has no universal guarantee. Every reform added a patch rather than replacing the quilt.
The result is the fragmented system we just mapped — and the costs we turn to now.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
section: It’s the Prices, Not the Utilization
text:
It’s the Prices, Not the Utilization
The intuitive guess is that Americans must overuse care — too many tests, too many visits. The evidence says the opposite.

Americans actually have fewer doctor visits and fewer hospital stays than the OECD average.
What’s different is that each unit costs far more: the same drug, scan, or procedure simply has a higher price tag in the US.
Health economists summed it up in a famous title: “It’s still the prices, stupid” (Anderson et al. 2019). The volume isn’t the problem. The prices are.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
What Drives the Prices
If prices are the engine, what feeds them?

Drug prices — the US pays far more for the same medications than other countries, which negotiate or cap prices.
Higher salaries — for physicians and many staff, well above peer-country norms.
Hospital market power — mergers leave many regions with one dominant system that can name its price.
Opacity — patients almost never see a price before care, so normal market discipline never kicks in.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
The Paperwork Tax
Some of the money never buys care at all — it buys billing.

Administrative complexity. With hundreds of insurers, each with its own rules, codes, and denials, the US spends enormously just managing the money.

Administration is about 8% of US health spending, versus 1–3% in peer countries.
Hospital administration alone runs about 25% of costs — the highest of eight nations studied.
Billing and insurance paperwork costs roughly $2,497 per person in the US, against $551 in Canada (Himmelstein et al. 2020).

Part of this is the price of running four models at once: every provider must bill Medicare, Medicaid, dozens of private insurers, and self-pay patients, each with its own rules. Canada has one payer to bill. We have thousands.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Fee-for-Service: Paying for Volume
Underneath the prices is an incentive problem in how we pay.


Fee-for-service
Providers are paid per thing done — per test, scan, procedure. The built-in incentive is to do more, whether or not it helps. Volume is rewarded; restraint is not.



Value-based care
The alternative pays for keeping people healthy — a fixed amount per patient, or bonuses for good outcomes. It’s spreading slowly, but fee-for-service still dominates US medicine.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
A Lens Check






Note


“Let the market set prices” is a libertarian instinct from Part A — free transactions, minimal interference. It does drive innovation and rewards investment.
But health care breaks the assumptions a market needs: patients can’t shop while having a heart attack, can’t see prices, and can’t walk away. The utilitarian asks whether all this spending actually maximizes health (it doesn’t), and Daniels asks whether prices this high are compatible with fair access (they aren’t). Same facts, three verdicts.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Thought Question

You have a job and a health insurance plan. Your appendix bursts; you get emergency surgery and go home in two days, cured. Weeks later, a bill arrives: after everything your insurance paid, you owe $15,000 — a deductible, “out-of-network” charges from a surgeon you never chose, and coinsurance.

You did everything right and were insured. How did you still end up owing $15,000?
Did you have any real chance to “shop” or consent to these prices?
Should being insured mean you’re protected from this — and if so, who pays the difference?

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Make It Contemporary

This is ordinary, not rare.

Medical bills are a leading cause of personal bankruptcy in the US, and a large share of online fundraisers are for medical costs.
The bill in the scenario — surprise charges from out-of-network providers at an in-network hospital — was so common that Congress passed the No Surprises Act, effective 2022, to ban much of it (Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services 2022).

Discuss: A law was needed to stop hospitals from billing insured patients for doctors those patients never chose. What does needing that law tell you about the system?

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


A distinction the coverage numbers hide:

Uninsured means no plan at all (~8%).
Underinsured means you have a plan, but its deductibles and cost-sharing are so high that you still can’t afford care or get wiped out by a bill.

Tens of millions are underinsured. “92.5% covered” can hide people who skip prescriptions, avoid the ER, or go bankrupt anyway. Health economists call the bankrupting effect financial toxicity — when the treatment for the bill becomes its own illness.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Access Gaps
Part of the outcomes gap is straightforward: people who can’t afford care get less of it, later.

The uninsured and underinsured delay care, skip medications, and avoid preventive visits until problems become emergencies.
Emergency, late-stage care is both worse for patients and more expensive for everyone.
Peer countries with universal coverage remove this barrier — one clear reason their populations do better on basic measures.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
section: But It’s Not Only the System
text:
But It’s Not Only the System
Here honesty matters: a large share of the US disadvantage is not something hospitals can fix.

Americans face higher rates of obesity, firearm deaths, vehicle deaths, and drug overdoses (about 107,000 in 2022) than peers.
Deep income inequality and poverty shape health long before anyone reaches a clinic.
These are social and behavioral drivers. Even a perfect health system would leave much of the gap untouched — which is exactly why the gap is so stubborn.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
section: Shorter Lives, Even for the Advantaged
text:
Shorter Lives, Even for the Advantaged

The finding that surprised everyone.
A landmark 2013 National Academies report, U.S. Health in International Perspective: Shorter Lives, Poorer Health, found the US disadvantage runs across the whole life course — and is not explained only by the poor or by minorities (National Research Council and Institute of Medicine 2013).
Even white, college-educated, insured, high-income Americans were found to be in worse health than similar people in peer nations. The disadvantage is national, not just a problem of the margins.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Disparities Within
The national average also hides sharp gaps inside the US.

Life expectancy differs by years across racial and economic lines.
Maternal mortality for Black women is roughly three times that of white women.
Infant mortality for Black infants (~10.9 per 1,000) is about three times that of Asian infants (~3.5) (Ely and Driscoll 2025).

These are the population-level injustices Part A’s theories were built to name. They are not random — they track access, history, and bias.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Best in the World — Genuinely
The US system has real, world-leading strengths, and an honest account has to say so.

For complex, specialized care, US centers like the Mayo Clinic, Cleveland Clinic, and MD Anderson are among the best on Earth.
US cancer survival is at or near the top internationally — highest in the world for prostate cancer, top-tier for breast and colon (the CONCORD studies) (Allemani et al. 2018).
The US leads in biomedical innovation, and the well-insured face little or no waiting for elective and specialty care.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Where We Struggle
The same system that delivers the world’s best specialty care fails on the basics.

Prices no one can see or predict; access gaps for tens of millions.
A crushing administrative burden.
Rural hospital closures leaving whole regions without nearby care.
A life-expectancy stall that, even after the COVID rebound, leaves the US years behind its peers.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
The Workforce Crisis
The strain shows up most sharply in the people who deliver the care — especially nurses.

HRSA projects roughly a 10% shortage of registered nurses by 2027, and a shortfall of over 500,000 by 2030 (Health Resources and Services Administration, National Center for Health Workforce Analysis 2024).
The NCSBN found about 138,000 nurses left since 2022, and nearly 40% intend to leave the workforce by 2029.
In 2024, over 80,000 qualified nursing-school applicants were turned away — not for lack of demand, but for lack of faculty and clinical placements.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
section: Why Staffing Is an Ethics Problem
text:
Why Staffing Is an Ethics Problem






Note


A shortage of nurses is not just an HR headache — it is a justice and safety issue.

Thinner staffing means higher patient-to-nurse ratios, which research links to worse patient outcomes.
Worse conditions drive burnout, which drives more nurses out — a self-feeding cycle.
Shortages hit rural and safety-net settings hardest, widening the very disparities we just described.

The system’s failures land on patients through the workforce. Who staffs the bedside is a distributive-justice question too.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
section: The Menu, Now That You Know It
text:
The Menu, Now That You Know It
Every reform debate you’ll ever hear is really an argument about which model to grow, and which to shrink. The four are no longer abstract:

Beveridge — expand the VA’s approach to everyone (“the NHS option”).
National Health Insurance — one public insurer paying private doctors (“Medicare for All”).
Bismarck — keep private insurers but regulate them into universal coverage (a fully built-out ACA, like Switzerland or Germany).
Keep blending — patch the current four-model mix without choosing.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Choosing on Purpose: Taiwan


The US drifted into its patchwork by accident. It doesn’t have to stay there — and one country proves it.
In the early 1990s Taiwan had no universal system. Rather than guess, its government studied health systems around the world, then in 1995 deliberately built a National Health Insurance model — single public insurer, private delivery. It reached near-universal coverage in a few years, at a fraction of US cost.


The lesson
A country can look at the menu and choose. The US question is not “is reform possible?” — Taiwan, Canada, Germany, and Britain all answer yes. It is which model, and which value that choice serves.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Three Directions, Three Values
Each reform path advances a different Part A value — and sacrifices another.









Reform
Model
Serves…
Strains…




Medicare for All
NHI
equal access (Rawls, Daniels)
liberty; existing plans


Public option
NHI + Bismarck
a middle path
satisfies neither side fully


Build out the ACA
Bismarck
coverage and choice
complexity; cost control


Market reforms
toward out-of-pocket
liberty (libertarian)
the worst-off



There is no value-free choice. To pick a model is to pick which of Part A’s principles you put first.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Make It Contemporary

“Medicare for All” would cover every American through a single public insurer, ending most private plans and the link between jobs and coverage.

Which Part A value does it serve best — and which does it sacrifice?
It would likely lower total spending (less administration, real price negotiation) but raise taxes while ending premiums. Is that a good trade?
Tens of millions are happy with employer plans. Should a reform that helps the worst-off be allowed to change what they have?

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Wrap-Up

We started with a paradox: the US spends about double any peer nation, near $14,570 per person, and gets shorter lives in return. We saw why — a patchwork that runs all four models of health care at once (Beveridge for veterans, NHI for the old, Bismarck for workers, out-of-pocket for the uninsured), built by a wartime accident, with prices no one can see and an outcomes gap driven as much by guns, cars, food, and poverty as by the clinic. We named what the US does best (the world’s finest specialty and cancer care) and where it fails (access, cost, and a nursing workforce in crisis).
None of this settles the argument — it equips it. Other countries chose one model on purpose; the US can too. Which one depends on which of Part A’s values we put first: the most total health, the protection of the worst-off, the freedom to choose, or the capabilities every person needs. The model is yours to argue for.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Review Questions


Recall
Explain “it’s the prices, not the utilization,” and give two specific reasons US prices run higher than peer countries’.


Apply
A friend says, “Americans are unhealthy because they overuse the ER and eat badly — fixing insurance won’t help.” Using this lecture, where is your friend partly right, and where wrong?


Debate
Should the US adopt a single-payer system, a public option, or market reforms? Defend your choice using a specific theory of justice from Part A.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Key Terms

The four models — Beveridge (gov owns & pays), National Health Insurance (single gov payer, private care), Bismarck (regulated private funds), out-of-pocket (pay or go without).
Fragmentation — the US runs all four models at once, sorted by age, job, and income.
Employer-sponsored insurance — job-based coverage; ~half of Americans.
Medicare / Medicaid — federal coverage for the old/disabled / joint federal–state coverage for the low-income.
EMTALA — 1986 law requiring ERs to screen and stabilize regardless of ability to pay.
Tax exclusion — the untaxed status of employer health benefits; the largest federal tax expenditure.
“It’s the prices, not the utilization” — US spending is high because of prices, not volume of care.
Underinsurance / financial toxicity — having coverage but still unable to afford care, or bankrupted by it.
Single-payer / public option — one public insurer for all / a public plan competing with private ones.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
Further Reading

National Research Council and Institute of Medicine. 2013. U.s. Health in International Perspective: Shorter Lives, Poorer Health. Edited by Steven H. Woolf and Laudan Aron. The National Academies Press. https://doi.org/10.17226/13497.

Reid, T. R. 2009. The Healing of America: A Global Quest for Better, Cheaper, and Fairer Health Care. Penguin Press.


Tax Policy Center. 2024. How Does the Tax Exclusion for Employer-Sponsored Health Insurance Work? Urban Institute and Brookings Institution Tax Policy Center Briefing Book. https://taxpolicycenter.org/briefing-book/how-does-tax-exclusion-employer-sponsored-health-insurance-work.


United States Congress. 1986. Emergency Medical Treatment and Active Labor Act (EMTALA), 42 u.s.c. § 1395dd. Enacted as part of the Consolidated Omnibus Budget Reconciliation Act of 1985, Pub. L. No. 99-272. https://www.cms.gov/medicare/regulations-guidance/legislation/emergency-medical-treatment-labor-act.


United States Congress. 2010. Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act, Pub. L. No. 111-148. 124 Stat. 119. https://www.congress.gov/bill/111th-congress/house-bill/3590.

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
In Part A we built four lenses for asking what a just society owes people: maximize good (utilitarian), protect the worst-off (Rawls), respect free choices (libertarian), secure what people need to function (capabilities). We argued, with Daniels, about whether health care is a right at all.
That was the theory. Part B is the machine those values have to deal with — the real US health care system. Before we can argue about how to fix it, we have to see clearly how it actually works, what it costs, and what it delivers.




Before the numbers appear, write down your best guess.

How much does the US spend on health care per person, per year — counting everything (insurance, taxes, out-of-pocket)?
Among wealthy countries, where do you think the US ranks on life expectancy — near the top, middle, or bottom?
Do you expect Americans to use more doctor visits and hospital days than people in other rich countries, or fewer?





The United States spends far more on health care than any nation on Earth — and its people are not healthier for it.




United States
OECD average




Spending per person (2023)
~$14,570
~$6,000–7,000


Share of the whole economy
17.6% of GDP
~9–11%


Life expectancy (2023)
78.4 years
81.1 years


Maternal deaths / 100,000
~17
~10



We spend roughly double — and live nearly three years less (Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services 2024; Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development 2025). Everything in this lecture answers one question: why?









Note



Hold onto the gap. The US spends the most and ranks near the bottom of its peers on life expectancy, infant mortality, and maternal mortality. This is not a small inefficiency — it is the central puzzle of American health policy.
And it is where Part A stops being abstract. A country that treats care as mostly a market good has built a system with extraordinary capabilities and extraordinary gaps. By the end you should be able to say, in the language of justice, what we are getting right and what we owe each other.






By the end of Part B, you will be able to:

Explain the paradox: highest spending, middling outcomes.
Describe the four models of a health system (Beveridge, National Health Insurance, Bismarck, out-of-pocket) and name countries that use each.
Show why the US is “fragmented” — it runs all four models at once — and map its programs onto them.
Trace how the system got this way since 1945, and explain why US care is so expensive (“it’s the prices, not the utilization”) and why outcomes lag.
Name what the US does best and where it struggles, including the nursing workforce crisis.
Argue, using a Part A value, which model the US should move toward.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

That was the theory. Part B is the machine those values have to deal with — the real US health care system. Before we can argue about how to fix it, we have to see clearly how it actually works, what it costs, and what it delivers.
