title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
2.1 Two Questions Define Every System
Before we look at the US, we need a map. Every health system on earth is built from the answers to just two questions:

Who delivers the care? — does the government own the hospitals and employ the staff, or are they private?
Who pays for it? — the government from taxes, regulated insurance funds, or you, out of pocket?

The journalist T. R. Reid sorted the world’s systems into four models by those two answers (Reid 2009). Almost every country picks one. Keep that word — one — in mind.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
2.2 The Four Models









Model
Who delivers
Who pays
Where you’ll find it




Beveridge
government owns it
government (taxes)
UK (NHS), Spain, Cuba


National Health Insurance
private providers
one government insurer
Canada, Taiwan


Bismarck
private providers
regulated nonprofit funds (payroll)
Germany, France, Japan


Out-of-pocket
whoever you can find
you, if you can pay
most low-income countries



The deepest split is who pays, running from “the public, for everyone” (Beveridge, NHI) to “private but tightly regulated” (Bismarck) to “you’re on your own” (out-of-pocket).
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
2.3 Beveridge vs. National Health Insurance


Beveridge (UK’s NHS, since 1948)
The government owns the hospitals and employs the doctors, and funds it all through taxes. Care is free at the point of use. This is what “socialized medicine” actually means — the state runs the whole thing.



National Health Insurance (Canada, Taiwan)
Doctors and hospitals stay private, but a single government insurer pays them, funded by taxes. One payer, private delivery. No marketing, no denials for profit, strong bargaining power on prices.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
2.4 Bismarck vs. Out-of-Pocket


Bismarck (Germany since 1883, France, Japan)
Care and insurance are private — but insurers are nonprofit “sickness funds,” everyone must be covered, and prices are tightly regulated. Switzerland’s mandate, and the US ACA, are cousins of this model.



Out-of-pocket
No system at all: you get the care you can pay for, and go without the rest. This is the reality for most people in poor countries — and, as we’ll see, for the uninsured in the US.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
section: 2.5 A Caution Before We Go On
text:
2.5 A Caution Before We Go On






Note



These four models are idealizations. Real countries blend at the edges — the British buy private top-up insurance, Germany has some public hospitals, Canada leaves dental and drugs largely private.
But the framework holds where it matters: each country builds its system around one model and makes it coherent. Hold that thought — because the United States did something no one else did.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
3.1 One Life, Every Program
The US has no single health system. It has a patchwork — and the easiest way to see it is to follow one person through a lifetime.

Meet Maria.

Born: her family is low-income, so she’s covered by Medicaid/CHIP.
Age 26: good job with benefits — employer-sponsored insurance.
Age 34: laid off. Briefly uninsured, then buys a plan on the ACA exchange with a subsidy.
Age 60: chronic illness, can’t keep working full-time — a coverage gap.
Age 65: ages into Medicare.
Age 81: needs a nursing home; Medicare won’t cover long-term care, so she spends down her savings until Medicaid does.

One person. Six different programs — and, as we’re about to see, three different countries’ models of health care, all without leaving the US.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
3.2 Who Covers Whom
US health coverage in 2023 (Keisler-Starkey et al. 2024):



Source of coverage
Share of population




Employer-sponsored
~54%


Medicare (65+ and disabled)
~19%


Medicaid (low-income)
~19%


Direct purchase / ACA exchanges
~10%


VA / military
a few %


Uninsured
~8% (≈26 million)



Shares add to more than 100% because many people hold more than one type in a year. About 92.5% of Americans had some coverage — near a record high, and still leaving 26 million with none.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
section: 3.3 The US Runs All Four Models at Once
text:
3.3 The US Runs All Four Models at Once
This is the heart of “fragmentation.” Other countries pick one model. The US runs all four simultaneously — and which one you live under depends on your age, job, income, and luck (Reid 2009).









If you are…
Your coverage
…is really this model
…like which country




A veteran (VA)
government hospitals
Beveridge
the UK


65 or older
Medicare
National Health Insurance
Canada


Working with benefits
employer insurance
Bismarck
Germany


Uninsured
pay or go without
Out-of-pocket
the developing world



A single country containing Britain, Canada, Germany, and the developing world — sorted by who you happen to be.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
section: 3.4 Why “Fragmented” Is Expensive, Not Just Messy
text:
3.4 Why “Fragmented” Is Expensive, Not Just Messy
Running four systems at once isn’t only untidy — it has real costs.

No coherence: each model has its own rules, paperwork, eligibility, and incentives. Providers must bill all of them at once.
Gaps at the seams: people fall between models — too rich for Medicaid, too young for Medicare, no job-based plan. That’s where the 26 million uninsured live.
No single bargaining power: because no one payer dominates, no one can negotiate prices the way Canada’s or the UK’s system can. We’ll see the price consequences shortly.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
3.5 Employer-Sponsored Insurance — the Core
About half of Americans get coverage through a job — the single largest piece of the system, and the one most people never chose.

Your employer buys a group plan and pays much of the premium; you pay the rest from your paycheck.
In 2024, the average family plan cost $25,572 a year in total premiums — most of it invisible to workers because the employer “pays” it (out of wages they’d otherwise get).
Lose or change the job, and you can lose the coverage. Maria’s layoff is the system’s built-in fragility.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
3.6 Medicare and Medicaid — the 1965 Pillars


Medicare — federal, for people 65+ and the long-term disabled.

Part A: hospital
Part B: outpatient/doctors
Part C: private “Advantage” plans
Part D: drugs

Nearly universal for the old — the closest thing the US has to a right to care.



Medicaid — joint federal–state, for low-income people.

Rules and generosity vary by state.
The ACA let states expand it to more low-income adults; not all did.
Quietly enormous: it pays for about 4 in 10 US births and most nursing-home care.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
3.7 The VA — Actual Government Medicine
One corner of the US system looks like the rest of the wealthy world.

The Veterans Health Administration doesn’t just insure veterans — it owns the hospitals and employs the doctors. The government delivers the care directly.
This is pure Beveridge — the UK’s model, running inside the United States. It’s what people usually mean by “socialized medicine,” and it’s already the largest integrated system in the country.
So the same nation that leaves 26 million uninsured also runs a full British-style health service for its veterans. That is the patchwork in one breath.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
3.8 The Exchanges and the Uninsured
For people without a job-based plan, Medicare, or Medicaid, the ACA marketplaces (since 2014) sell individual plans, with income-based subsidies.

About 10% of Americans buy coverage this way or on the individual market.
Even so, ~26 million remain uninsured — too well-off for Medicaid, no job-based plan, or unwilling/unable to buy in.
The uninsured don’t simply go without: by law, emergency rooms must still treat them — a fact with its own history, coming up next.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
section: 4.1 The Accident That Built the System
text:
4.1 The Accident That Built the System
America’s job-based system was not designed. It was an accident of World War II.

Wartime wage controls (1942–43) froze what employers could pay. To compete for scarce workers, they offered health benefits instead of raises.
Then the government ruled those benefits tax-free — confirmed in the 1954 tax code. A dollar of health insurance wasn’t taxed; a dollar of wages was.
That tax break made job-based coverage explode. The US tied health care to employment not by choice, but by a loophole no one ever closed.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
4.2 The Hidden Subsidy
That 1954 tax break is still here — and it is enormous.

Excluding employer health benefits from taxes cost the federal government about $299 billion in 2022 — the single largest tax expenditure in the entire federal budget (Tax Policy Center 2024).
It is invisible: most workers have no idea they receive it.
It is regressive — worth more to higher earners in higher tax brackets. The US already heavily subsidizes health care; it just does it quietly, and tilts it toward the well-off.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
4.3 A Short History, 1945–Today







G



a

1948
Truman's national
plan fails



b

1965
Medicare &
Medicaid



a->b





c

1986
EMTALA:
ERs must treat



b->c





d

1994
Clinton plan
fails



c->d





e

2003
Medicare
Part D (drugs)



d->e





f

2010
ACA: exchanges,
Medicaid expansion



e->f










Two big expansions (1965, 2010), two big failures (1948, 1994), and one unfunded promise — EMTALA, which since 1986 has required hospitals to screen and stabilize anyone in an emergency, regardless of ability to pay (United States Congress 1986).
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
4.4 What Changed, and What Didn’t
Eighty years of fights, and the core never moved.

What changed: the old got Medicare; the poor got Medicaid; emergencies got EMTALA; the individual market got the ACA’s exchanges and protections (United States Congress 2010).
What didn’t: coverage is still built around the employer, and the US still has no universal guarantee. Every reform added a patch rather than replacing the quilt.
The result is the fragmented system we just mapped — and the costs we turn to now.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
section: 5.1 It’s the Prices, Not the Utilization
text:
5.1 It’s the Prices, Not the Utilization
The intuitive guess is that Americans must overuse care — too many tests, too many visits. The evidence says the opposite.

Americans actually have fewer doctor visits and fewer hospital stays than the OECD average.
What’s different is that each unit costs far more: the same drug, scan, or procedure simply has a higher price tag in the US.
Health economists summed it up in a famous title: “It’s still the prices, stupid” (Anderson et al. 2019). The volume isn’t the problem. The prices are.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
5.2 What Drives the Prices
If prices are the engine, what feeds them?

Drug prices — the US pays far more for the same medications than other countries, which negotiate or cap prices.
Higher salaries — for physicians and many staff, well above peer-country norms.
Hospital market power — mergers leave many regions with one dominant system that can name its price.
Opacity — patients almost never see a price before care, so normal market discipline never kicks in.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
5.3 The Paperwork Tax
Some of the money never buys care at all — it buys billing.

Administrative complexity. With hundreds of insurers, each with its own rules, codes, and denials, the US spends enormously just managing the money.

Administration is about 8% of US health spending, versus 1–3% in peer countries.
Hospital administration alone runs about 25% of costs — the highest of eight nations studied.
Billing and insurance paperwork costs roughly $2,497 per person in the US, against $551 in Canada (Himmelstein et al. 2020).

Part of this is the price of running four models at once: every provider must bill Medicare, Medicaid, dozens of private insurers, and self-pay patients, each with its own rules. Canada has one payer to bill. We have thousands.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
5.4 Fee-for-Service: Paying for Volume
Underneath the prices is an incentive problem in how we pay.


Fee-for-service
Providers are paid per thing done — per test, scan, procedure. The built-in incentive is to do more, whether or not it helps. Volume is rewarded; restraint is not.



Value-based care
The alternative pays for keeping people healthy — a fixed amount per patient, or bonuses for good outcomes. It’s spreading slowly, but fee-for-service still dominates US medicine.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
5.5 A Lens Check






Note



“Let the market set prices” is a libertarian instinct from Part A — free transactions, minimal interference. It does drive innovation and rewards investment.
But health care breaks the assumptions a market needs: patients can’t shop while having a heart attack, can’t see prices, and can’t walk away. The utilitarian asks whether all this spending actually maximizes health (it doesn’t), and Daniels asks whether prices this high are compatible with fair access (they aren’t). Same facts, three verdicts.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
6.1 Thought Question

You have a job and a health insurance plan. Your appendix bursts; you get emergency surgery and go home in two days, cured. Weeks later, a bill arrives: after everything your insurance paid, you owe $15,000 — a deductible, “out-of-network” charges from a surgeon you never chose, and coinsurance.

You did everything right and were insured. How did you still end up owing $15,000?
Did you have any real chance to “shop” or consent to these prices?
Should being insured mean you’re protected from this — and if so, who pays the difference?
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
6.2 Make It Contemporary

This is ordinary, not rare.

Medical bills are a leading cause of personal bankruptcy in the US, and a large share of online fundraisers are for medical costs.
The bill in the scenario — surprise charges from out-of-network providers at an in-network hospital — was so common that Congress passed the No Surprises Act, effective 2022, to ban much of it (Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services 2022).

Discuss: A law was needed to stop hospitals from billing insured patients for doctors those patients never chose. What does needing that law tell you about the system?
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
6.3 After You’ve Discussed






Note



A distinction the coverage numbers hide:

Uninsured means no plan at all (~8%).
Underinsured means you have a plan, but its deductibles and cost-sharing are so high that you still can’t afford care or get wiped out by a bill.

Tens of millions are underinsured. “92.5% covered” can hide people who skip prescriptions, avoid the ER, or go bankrupt anyway. Health economists call the bankrupting effect financial toxicity — when the treatment for the bill becomes its own illness.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
7.1 Access Gaps
Part of the outcomes gap is straightforward: people who can’t afford care get less of it, later.

The uninsured and underinsured delay care, skip medications, and avoid preventive visits until problems become emergencies.
Emergency, late-stage care is both worse for patients and more expensive for everyone.
Peer countries with universal coverage remove this barrier — one clear reason their populations do better on basic measures.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
section: 7.2 But It’s Not Only the System
text:
7.2 But It’s Not Only the System
Here honesty matters: a large share of the US disadvantage is not something hospitals can fix.

Americans face higher rates of obesity, firearm deaths, vehicle deaths, and drug overdoses (about 107,000 in 2022) than peers.
Deep income inequality and poverty shape health long before anyone reaches a clinic.
These are social and behavioral drivers. Even a perfect health system would leave much of the gap untouched — which is exactly why the gap is so stubborn.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
section: 7.3 Shorter Lives, Even for the Advantaged
text:
7.3 Shorter Lives, Even for the Advantaged

The finding that surprised everyone.
A landmark 2013 National Academies report, U.S. Health in International Perspective: Shorter Lives, Poorer Health, found the US disadvantage runs across the whole life course — and is not explained only by the poor or by minorities (National Research Council and Institute of Medicine 2013).
Even white, college-educated, insured, high-income Americans were found to be in worse health than similar people in peer nations. The disadvantage is national, not just a problem of the margins.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
7.4 Disparities Within
The national average also hides sharp gaps inside the US.

Life expectancy differs by years across racial and economic lines.
Maternal mortality for Black women is roughly three times that of white women.
Infant mortality for Black infants (~10.9 per 1,000) is about three times that of Asian infants (~3.5) (Ely and Driscoll 2025).

These are the population-level injustices Part A’s theories were built to name. They are not random — they track access, history, and bias.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
8.1 Best in the World — Genuinely
The US system has real, world-leading strengths, and an honest account has to say so.

For complex, specialized care, US centers like the Mayo Clinic, Cleveland Clinic, and MD Anderson are among the best on Earth.
US cancer survival is at or near the top internationally — highest in the world for prostate cancer, top-tier for breast and colon (the CONCORD studies) (Allemani et al. 2018).
The US leads in biomedical innovation, and the well-insured face little or no waiting for elective and specialty care.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
8.2 Where We Struggle
The same system that delivers the world’s best specialty care fails on the basics.

Prices no one can see or predict; access gaps for tens of millions.
A crushing administrative burden.
Rural hospital closures leaving whole regions without nearby care.
A life-expectancy stall that, even after the COVID rebound, leaves the US years behind its peers.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
8.3 The Workforce Crisis
The strain shows up most sharply in the people who deliver the care — especially nurses.

HRSA projects roughly a 10% shortage of registered nurses by 2027, and a shortfall of over 500,000 by 2030 (Health Resources and Services Administration, National Center for Health Workforce Analysis 2024).
The NCSBN found about 138,000 nurses left since 2022, and nearly 40% intend to leave the workforce by 2029.
In 2024, over 80,000 qualified nursing-school applicants were turned away — not for lack of demand, but for lack of faculty and clinical placements.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
section: 8.4 Why Staffing Is an Ethics Problem
text:
8.4 Why Staffing Is an Ethics Problem






Note



A shortage of nurses is not just an HR headache — it is a justice and safety issue.

Thinner staffing means higher patient-to-nurse ratios, which research links to worse patient outcomes.
Worse conditions drive burnout, which drives more nurses out — a self-feeding cycle.
Shortages hit rural and safety-net settings hardest, widening the very disparities we just described.

The system’s failures land on patients through the workforce. Who staffs the bedside is a distributive-justice question too.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
section: 9.1 The Menu, Now That You Know It
text:
9.1 The Menu, Now That You Know It
Every reform debate you’ll ever hear is really an argument about which model to grow, and which to shrink. The four are no longer abstract:

Beveridge — expand the VA’s approach to everyone (“the NHS option”).
National Health Insurance — one public insurer paying private doctors (“Medicare for All”).
Bismarck — keep private insurers but regulate them into universal coverage (a fully built-out ACA, like Switzerland or Germany).
Keep blending — patch the current four-model mix without choosing.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
9.2 Choosing on Purpose: Taiwan


The US drifted into its patchwork by accident. It doesn’t have to stay there — and one country proves it.
In the early 1990s Taiwan had no universal system. Rather than guess, its government studied health systems around the world, then in 1995 deliberately built a National Health Insurance model — single public insurer, private delivery. It reached near-universal coverage in a few years, at a fraction of US cost.


The lesson
A country can look at the menu and choose. The US question is not “is reform possible?” — Taiwan, Canada, Germany, and Britain all answer yes. It is which model, and which value that choice serves.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
9.3 Three Directions, Three Values
Each reform path advances a different Part A value — and sacrifices another.









Reform
Model
Serves…
Strains…




Medicare for All
NHI
equal access (Rawls, Daniels)
liberty; existing plans


Public option
NHI + Bismarck
a middle path
satisfies neither side fully


Build out the ACA
Bismarck
coverage and choice
complexity; cost control


Market reforms
toward out-of-pocket
liberty (libertarian)
the worst-off



There is no value-free choice. To pick a model is to pick which of Part A’s principles you put first.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
9.4 Make It Contemporary

“Medicare for All” would cover every American through a single public insurer, ending most private plans and the link between jobs and coverage.

Which Part A value does it serve best — and which does it sacrifice?
It would likely lower total spending (less administration, real price negotiation) but raise taxes while ending premiums. Is that a good trade?
Tens of millions are happy with employer plans. Should a reform that helps the worst-off be allowed to change what they have?
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
9.5 Wrap-Up

We started with a paradox: the US spends about double any peer nation, near $14,570 per person, and gets shorter lives in return. We saw why — a patchwork that runs all four models of health care at once (Beveridge for veterans, NHI for the old, Bismarck for workers, out-of-pocket for the uninsured), built by a wartime accident, with prices no one can see and an outcomes gap driven as much by guns, cars, food, and poverty as by the clinic. We named what the US does best (the world’s finest specialty and cancer care) and where it fails (access, cost, and a nursing workforce in crisis).
None of this settles the argument — it equips it. Other countries chose one model on purpose; the US can too. Which one depends on which of Part A’s values we put first: the most total health, the protection of the worst-off, the freedom to choose, or the capabilities every person needs. The model is yours to argue for.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
9.6 Review Questions


Recall
Explain “it’s the prices, not the utilization,” and give two specific reasons US prices run higher than peer countries’.


Apply
A friend says, “Americans are unhealthy because they overuse the ER and eat badly — fixing insurance won’t help.” Using this lecture, where is your friend partly right, and where wrong?


Debate
Should the US adopt a single-payer system, a public option, or market reforms? Defend your choice using a specific theory of justice from Part A.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: Lecture 8 — Justice and Health Care
text:
9.7 Key Terms

The four models — Beveridge (gov owns & pays), National Health Insurance (single gov payer, private care), Bismarck (regulated private funds), out-of-pocket (pay or go without).
Fragmentation — the US runs all four models at once, sorted by age, job, and income.
Employer-sponsored insurance — job-based coverage; ~half of Americans.
Medicare / Medicaid — federal coverage for the old/disabled / joint federal–state coverage for the low-income.
EMTALA — 1986 law requiring ERs to screen and stabilize regardless of ability to pay.
Tax exclusion — the untaxed status of employer health benefits; the largest federal tax expenditure.
“It’s the prices, not the utilization” — US spending is high because of prices, not volume of care.
Underinsurance / financial toxicity — having coverage but still unable to afford care, or bankrupted by it.
Single-payer / public option — one public insurer for all / a public plan competing with private ones.
crumbs: Part B — Why US Health Care Costs So Much

title: The Tuskegee and Guatemala Syphilis Studies: Research Ethics, Whistleblowing, and Medical Distrust
text:
Who: About 600 Black men of Macon County, Alabama — 399 with latent syphilis, 201 uninfected controls — and, later, more than 1,300 Guatemalans deliberately infected by the same U.S. agency.
What: The U.S. Public Health Service watched untreated syphilis progress to autopsy for 40 years (1932–1972). The agency withheld penicillin and deceived the men into believing they were being treated. A parallel PHS project in Guatemala (1946–1948) infected prisoners, soldiers, and psychiatric patients on purpose.
Where / When: Tuskegee/Macon County, Alabama, 1932–1972; Guatemala, 1946–1948. Exposed by a whistleblower and the press in 1972. The Guatemala records were found in 2003.
Why it matters: Tuskegee is widely called the single most important event in the rise of American bioethics. It produced the modern rules — informed consent, the IRB, the Belmont principles — and a debate, still unsettled, about whether rules were ever the thing that was missing, and about the medical distrust the study left behind.
Pair with: History & the Making of Research Ethics

title: The Tuskegee and Guatemala Syphilis Studies: Research Ethics, Whistleblowing, and Medical Distrust
text:
The Case
In 1932, the United States Public Health Service (PHS) — the federal agency that would become the CDC — began what its own scientists titled the Tuskegee Study of Untreated Syphilis in the Negro Male. The premise was simple. To its designers, it was scientifically attractive: find people with untreated syphilis and observe what the disease does to a human body if nothing is done, all the way to autopsy.
They chose Macon County, Alabama. Most residents were Black. Most were poor. Almost none had access to a doctor. That was the point. The men recruited — about 600 in all — were sharecroppers working white-owned land in a system of debt peonage. Of the 600:

399 had latent syphilis.
201 served as healthy controls.

The PHS enlisted the respected Tuskegee Institute and a trusted Black public-health nurse, Eunice Rivers, whose constancy kept the men coming back for decades. The men were never told they had syphilis. They were told they had “bad blood,” a vague local phrase, and that the government doctors were treating them for it.

What untreated syphilis does. Syphilis is a bacterial infection. After an early stage it can go latent — silent for years — and then, in roughly a third of untreated cases, attack the heart, blood vessels, brain, and spinal cord. The consequences the PHS’s own venereal-disease workers printed on a matchbook to scare people into testing were blunt and accurate: “Blindness, heart injury, insanity, death.” None of that was named to the Tuskegee men.

The deception was not passive. To collect the data they wanted, investigators did several specific things:

They performed painful diagnostic spinal taps and called them a free “spinal shot” — a treatment.
One PHS officer wrote that the men might refuse the procedure if they understood it was only diagnostic. Its real purpose, he said, should “be kept from them as far as possible.”
Because the study’s final data point was the autopsy, the PHS offered burial insurance. This was a powerful inducement in a poor rural community where a proper funeral mattered. Families would surrender the bodies in return.
One investigator wrote that the team had “no further interest in these patients until they die.”
When a mobile treatment unit later swept the county offering free penicillin to everyone, PHS staff quietly flagged their enrollees to local doctors with the instruction that the man was under study and not to be treated.







Figure 1: A Public Health Service worker draws blood from a study participant during an annual “roundup,” Milstead, Macon County, 1953. Nurse Eunice Rivers stands at left.




National Archives and Records Administration. Public domain. Reproduced in (Tobin 2022).

Penicillin became the standard cure for syphilis in the mid-1940s. The men were not given it. PHS scientists argued that the arrival of a cure made the study more valuable, not less. It was a never-to-be-repeated chance to see the full natural course of a disease that would soon no longer run its course anywhere else. The study generated about fifteen papers in reputable medical journals over thirty-seven years. The titles alone announced what it was — one 1964 article was called “The Tuskegee Study of Untreated Syphilis; the 30th Year of Observation.” A 1955 paper reported that more than thirty percent of the autopsied men had died directly of syphilis. No physician, anywhere, published a letter of objection.
It ended only because one PHS employee would not let it go. By the most-cited later reckonings:

Dozens of the men had died of syphilis or its complications.
Dozens of their wives had been infected.
Children had been born with congenital syphilis.

In 1973, after the story broke, a class-action suit produced an out-of-court settlement. The government eventually provided lifetime medical care to survivors and affected families. In 1997 — a quarter century after the study stopped — President Clinton apologized: what the government did “was shameful,” a study “so clearly racist.” No scientist was ever prosecuted.
The case is usually taught as a failure of consent. The questions it actually raises are harder than that, and they are still open.

title: The Tuskegee and Guatemala Syphilis Studies: Research Ethics, Whistleblowing, and Medical Distrust
text:
The Argument Tuskegee Started
Few cases have generated as much agreement that something monstrous happened and as much disagreement about what the monstrous thing was. The moves below trace that argument as a connected exchange — each answering the one before.

“We were missing rules”
The official lesson is institutional. Tuskegee, on this view, happened because the United States had no system requiring that subjects consent and no body to review research before it ran. So the country built one:

The 1974 National Research Act.
Institutional review boards (IRBs) to approve and monitor studies.
Federal informed-consent regulations.
In 1979, the Belmont Report’s three principles — respect for persons, beneficence, and justice.


Informed consent, the IRB, and Belmont. Informed consent means a competent person is told the truth about a study’s risks, benefits, and purpose and freely agrees to take part. An IRB is a committee that must approve human research before it begins and can stop it. The Belmont Report distilled the post-Tuskegee consensus into three duties:

Respect persons as autonomous agents.
Do not harm and maximize benefit.
Distribute research’s burdens and benefits fairly.

These are the spine of every research-ethics rulebook in use today (National Commission for the Protection of Human Subjects of Biomedical and Behavioral Research 1979).


The Regulatory-Gap Argument

If a system lacks independent review and consent regulations, then research abuses like Tuskegee are inevitable.
Modern research systems now have independent review and consent regulations.
Therefore, research abuses like Tuskegee are no longer possible in modern research systems.


Premise 1 is where this breaks. It assumes the gap was the absence of rules. The next move denies that.


“The rules existed — and were ignored”
The Nuremberg Code was written in 1947 after Nazi doctors were tried for experimenting on prisoners. It opens with the sentence “The voluntary consent of the human subject is absolutely essential.” It existed for the last twenty-five years of Tuskegee. American researchers of the era reportedly dismissed it as “a good code for barbarians” — something for Nazis, not for them. Rules were not missing. They were not felt to apply.
Then there is Guatemala. While researching Surgeon General Thomas Parran’s papers in 2003, the historian Susan Reverby opened the donated files of a PHS doctor named John Cutler and found something worse than Tuskegee (Reverby 2011). Between 1946 and 1948, the same Public Health Service had deliberately infected more than 1,300 Guatemalans — prisoners, soldiers, and patients in a psychiatric hospital — with syphilis, gonorrhea, and chancroid, to test prevention. When infection through arranged contact proved unreliable, investigators inoculated subjects directly. At least eighty-three deaths were recorded. One psychiatric patient, after months of induced disease, was dying when a researcher infected her further. She died days later.
The decisive fact about Guatemala is not its cruelty but its respectability. Consider the chain of approval:

The principal investigator, John Mahoney, was arguably the most honored American physician-scientist of his day. He won a Lasker Award the year the experiments began.
The proposal was reviewed and funded by an NIH study section staffed by physicians from the country’s leading medical schools.
The Surgeon General was kept informed and enthusiastic. He is recorded remarking that “we couldn’t do such an experiment in this country.”

Nothing failed in the chain of command. Everyone with authority approved.








G

(kept informed, approving)



div

(directors: Clark, Vonderlehr, Heller)



sg->div





nih

(reviewed & funded Guatemala)

(Mahoney, Cutler, Wenger)

Nurse Eunice Rivers
(kept the men enrolled)

(PHS interviewer, no authority)



bux->div


  objects, 1966–1968






Figure 2: The PHS chain of command in the Tuskegee and Guatemala studies. The point of the diagram is what it does not contain: a broken link. Approval and knowledge flowed through every legitimate level of authority — the Surgeon General, the funding review, the senior investigators, the trusted on-site nurse — down to instructing local doctors that enrolled men were ‘not to be treated.’ The one person who finally stopped Tuskegee, Peter Buxtun, sits outside this structure entirely (dashed). A rule is enforced by the people inside the boxes; that is why ‘add a rule’ did not, by itself, answer the problem.






The Conscience Argument

Rules only prevent abuse if researchers believe the rules apply to them and their subjects.
The PHS researchers did not believe existing ethical rules applied to their subjects.
Therefore, the failure was a failure of institutional and individual conscience, not a lack of rules.


That individual was Peter Buxtun. The PHS hired him in 1965 as a venereal-disease interviewer in San Francisco. He was a Czech-born social worker whose family had fled to America in 1937. He learned of the study from a coworker, obtained a folder of internal “roundup” reports, and recognized the shape of what he was reading. He had read the Nuremberg trial record. His path inside the agency went like this:

He filed formal objections in 1966 and again in 1968.
The CDC summoned him to Atlanta, and a senior official berated him.
A blue-ribbon panel was convened in 1969. Its dominant voice argued, “You will never have another study like this; take advantage of it.” Only one member, Gene Stollerman, urged that the men be treated as patients rather than as a data set.
The panel voted to continue. Buxtun asked the CDC, in writing, “What is the ethical thing to do?” He received no answer.
He took the documents to the press. The story broke in July 1972.







Figure 3: Peter Buxtun, the PHS social worker whose complaints, ignored inside the agency for years, finally reached the press in 1972.




Public domain. Reproduced in (Tobin 2022).

Why did it take one outsider seven years, when no insider acted in forty? Buxtun’s own answer was wry: “It’s tough being a whistleblower when you don’t even know you’re a whistleblower.” The stronger pressure to weave in here is from social psychology. Stanley Milgram’s obedience experiments — and a later study modeled on them, in which university students were asked to recruit others into an obviously dangerous sham experiment — found that most people, given a legitimate-seeming authority, comply and look away. They do this even when dissent is safe, anonymous, and easy (Elliott 2018). Milgram called this surrender the “agentic state”: we recast ourselves as instruments of the authority and let our conscience go quiet. The Tuskegee physicians were not monsters by Milgram’s lights. They were ordinary people inside a structure that made silence feel normal.


“But heroes are not a policy”
If the Conscience Argument is right, it leaves a problem. You cannot run a research system on the hope that each abuse will produce its own Buxtun — especially since the system punishes the ones it gets.

The Institutional-Design Reply

Relying on individual conscience only works if whistleblowers can safely come forward.
Under current institutional designs, whistleblowers who come forward are routinely punished.
Therefore, preventing future research abuses requires structural institutional reform, not just appeals to personal courage.


The constructive half of this comes, again, from Milgram. Obedience dropped sharply under three specific conditions:

When the authority figure was made less imposing.
When the victim was made more present.
And — most powerfully — when even one other person in the room dissented.

The design implication is concrete:

Deflate the prestige of the lead researcher.
Make subjects visible rather than abstract.
Protect real (not cosmetic) channels for objection.
Make it possible for objectors to find each other.

The recurring real-world successes — the New Zealand “unfortunate experiment,” for instance — came from teams of clinicians acting together, not solitary martyrs. An anonymous “ethics hotline,” the usual institutional answer, is the thing the evidence trusts least.


Who was chosen, and why
There is a wrong here that none of the moves above quite names. The Tuskegee men were not random. They were selected because they were poor, Black, unschooled, and without doctors. They were chosen for the very powerlessness that made them easy to deceive and unlikely to be believed. A PHS internal phrasing reduced them to a single compound label, a string of demographic adjectives ending in “men.” That is not a consent failure or an oversight failure. It is a justice failure: a vulnerable group made to carry a burden for a science that would never have been done to the powerful.

Vulnerability and fair subject selection. Kenneth Kipnis argued that “vulnerable” is not a list of groups but a set of pressures a participant is under — economic, social, deferential, institutional — that compromise a genuinely free choice (Kipnis 2001). The Belmont principle of justice demands fair subject selection. Research must not load its risks onto those least able to refuse, nor (the mirror error) exclude groups from research’s benefits. Tuskegee is the textbook case of wrongful exploitation of the vulnerable. Modern ethics also watches for wrongful exclusion.


The Justice Reframing

In Tuskegee, subjects were selected specifically because their poverty and race made them unable to refuse or complain.
Selecting subjects because of their vulnerability is a form of exploitation that violates the principle of justice.
Therefore, the deepest wrong of Tuskegee was a violation of justice, which cannot be fixed merely by improving consent procedures.


This is not safely historical. Research today still gravitates toward people under exactly Kipnis’s pressures:

The institutionalized.
The incarcerated.
The cognitively impaired.
The poor.

That is why “fair subject selection” remains an active demand and not a settled box to check.


The argument that hasn’t ended
There is a final move, and it is the one with the most current stakes. The standard account says: Tuskegee is why many Black Americans distrust medicine and research, so the remedy is outreach, apology, and education to repair the trust the study broke. After 1972, the empirical legacy is, in fact, measurable. Marcella Alsan and Marianne Wanamaker found that disclosure of the study was followed by reduced medical contact among older Black men and worse health outcomes — a real and lasting cost (Alsan and Wanamaker 2018).
But Vanessa Northington Gamble and Susan Reverby press a sharp objection to the standard account. Black medical distrust did not begin with Tuskegee and is not exhausted by it. It tracks a long history and, more importantly, present mistreatment — documented disparities in pain treatment and outcomes, structural barriers in care now (Gamble 1997). To explain today’s distrust by pointing only to a study that ended in 1972 can become a way of locating the problem safely in the past. It can cast Black patients as irrationally haunted rather than accurately observant. (Reverby also notes a precise irony: the Tuskegee men were not distrustful refusers. They trusted the government doctors, which is exactly how they were used.) The COVID-19 vaccination gap became the live test of this dispute: was the right response to recite Tuskegee and reassure, or to address the present-day inequities that make wariness a reasonable inference?
What actually moved the gap is instructive. Early 2021 messaging that leaned on history-and-reassurance — that was then, the rules are different now, the vaccine is safe — tended to land poorly. It answered a question many Black patients were not asking. A review of equity strategies found that the interventions that raised uptake were structural and relational rather than rhetorical:

Vaccine sites placed inside trusted Black churches and barbershops.
Outreach delivered by community health workers, clergy, and Black clinicians rather than by the institutions themselves (Dada et al. 2022).

The recurring mechanism is concordance — patients are more likely to accept advice, including vaccination, from clinicians who share their background or have earned standing in their community. A randomized field experiment in Oakland found that Black men assigned to a Black physician agreed to substantially more preventive services, including invasive screening, than those assigned to a non-Black physician — a gap that no recitation of history opens or closes (Alsan et al. 2019). The lesson tracks Reverby’s point precisely: the operative variable was not how vividly the past was explained but how much present trustworthiness the patient could actually observe in the room.
That gives a clinician something to do rather than something to recite. It does not, however, settle the argument. It arguably concedes the critique’s premise — trust follows present conditions, not historical correction — while leaving the standard view’s strongest reply intact: history is part of why those present conditions are read the way they are, and a patient who invokes Tuskegee is often naming a pattern, not a date — distrust as a response to everyday racism, not only to one archived study (Bajaj and Stanford 2021). The dispute is which description should drive the response.

The Distrust Dispute

If historical events like Tuskegee are the primary cause of medical distrust, then distrust should be solved by historical education and apology.
Medical distrust is primarily caused by ongoing, present-day systemic medical racism and disparities.
Therefore, medical distrust can only be solved by changing present-day medical practices, not just explaining the past.


Fifty years on, that is where the argument rests — not at a verdict, but at a question that a nurse, a researcher, and a public-health officer each have to answer the next time someone says, reasonably, that they are not sure they trust the people offering to help them.

title: The Tuskegee and Guatemala Syphilis Studies: Research Ethics, Whistleblowing, and Medical Distrust
text:
Discussion Questions

Write out The Regulatory-Gap Argument and The Conscience Argument in your own words — premises first, then the conclusion. They disagree about one premise. Identify it. Then explain what the Guatemala study — run by a respected lead researcher, reviewed by NIH, approved by the U.S. Surgeon General — tells us about which side has the better case.
Nurse Eunice Rivers was trusted by the men in the study because she was caring, consistent, and one of their own. That trust is part of what kept them enrolled for decades. Imagine you are a nurse who has just realized that the “treatment” you have been giving is a deception. What do you owe these particular patients that the institution does not? What, concretely, do you do — knowing that nurses who reported similar abuses often lost their jobs?
The Justice Reframing says the deepest wrong of Tuskegee was who the researchers chose as subjects, not the missing consent forms. Imagine a future study with perfect informed consent — but where the volunteers are poor, uninsured, and badly need the money. Would the consent fix Tuskegee’s wrong, or just move it somewhere else? Use the idea of fair subject selection to defend your answer.
The Institutional-Design Reply says hospitals can do specific things to keep individual dissent from being silenced. Pick a hospital you know. Name one concrete change — not an ethics hotline — that would make a dissenting nurse or resident less alone. Drawing on Milgram’s obedience studies, explain why your change would actually work.
A patient eligible for a new vaccine tells you she does not trust it. She mentions Tuskegee. The case describes two ways to respond: reassure her and explain the science, or acknowledge that present conditions still give some patients real reasons to distrust. Give the strongest version of each response. Then say which you would actually give her, and why.

title: The Tuskegee and Guatemala Syphilis Studies: Research Ethics, Whistleblowing, and Medical Distrust
text:
Further Reading

James H. Jones, Bad Blood: The Tuskegee Syphilis Experiment (Free Press, 1981; rev. 1993) (Jones 1981) — the definitive history.
Susan M. Reverby, Examining Tuskegee: The Infamous Syphilis Study and Its Legacy (University of North Carolina Press, 2009) (Reverby 2009).
Harriet A. Washington, Medical Apartheid (Doubleday, 2006) (Washington 2006) — the longer history of which Tuskegee is one chapter.
Carl Elliott, “Tuskegee Truth Teller,” The American Scholar 87, no. 1 (2018) (Elliott 2018) — the Buxtun profile and the whistleblowing evidence.
Presidential Commission for the Study of Bioethical Issues, “Ethically Impossible”: STD Research in Guatemala from 1946 to 1948 (2011) (Presidential Commission for the Study of Bioethical Issues 2011).
